input.case_report: A 75-year-old male presented with difficulty moving his left fingers for one month. He had a history of extra-periosteal paraffin plombage therapy 54 years ago for pulmonary tuberculosis. Chest computed tomography (CT) showed an enlarged plombage space with surrounding soft tissue density in the left chest cavity and bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy. Laboratory results included a white blood cell count of 4,800/µL (neutrophils 53%, lymphocytes 28%, eosinophils 10%), normal hemoglobin and platelet levels, normal liver and renal functions, and a serum C-reactive protein (CRP) level of 0.36 mg/dL. Tests for antinuclear antibody, anti-DNA antibody, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies, and rheumatoid factor were negative. Complement component 3 (C3) and complement component 4 (C4) levels were within normal limits. A tuberculosis T-cell spot (T-SPOT) assay was negative, and the soluble interleukin (IL)-2 receptor level was 1,065 U/mL. Whole-body 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) positron emission tomography (PET)-CT indicated abnormal FDG accumulation in the soft tissue mass around the plombage, enlarged salivary glands, cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy, and a retroperitoneal soft tissue mass surrounding the abdominal aorta.

input.case_report: A 26-year-old white female presented in February 2008 with approximately four enlarged lymph nodes in the left posterior cervical area, accompanied by joint pain and a fever lasting about 2 weeks. Initial laboratory data were reportedly normal. She was initially diagnosed with infectious mononucleosis. Two weeks later, she returned with complaints of cervical lymphadenopathy and joint pain. Laboratory data at that time showed leukopenia and thrombocytopenia. She was referred to a hematologist and hospitalized for a comprehensive workup. Tests revealed a white blood cell count of 2.0 (103/mm3), a total lymphocyte count of 900 cells/μL, a total granulocyte count of 1000/μL, a hemoglobin level of 12.9 g/dL, a mean cell volume of 91.5 fL, and a platelet count of 100,000/μL. HIV-1 and -2 screening tests were non-reactive, and anti-DNA and anti-nuclear antibodies were negative. Epstein-Barr Virus (EBV) and cytomegalovirus (CMV) tests were negative. Bone marrow biopsy showed no evidence of malignancy and normal cytogenetics (46, XX). A chest X-ray suggested chronic bronchitis with minimal peribronchial thickening. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis without contrast revealed mild hepatosplenomegaly, with the spleen measuring at least 17 cm in length, some dilation of the portal venous system, and bibasilar infiltrates with a small amount of pleural reaction. A repeat chest X-ray confirmed the interval development of bibasilar infiltrates, worse on the left side, suggesting pneumonia. She was treated with intravenous antibiotics, and a follow-up chest X-ray showed no active infiltrates. No adenopathy was noted in the chest on any study performed. A PET/CT scan showed hypermetabolic adenopathy bilaterally in the neck, left supraclavicular, and left subclavian areas. The largest lymph node measured 2.4 cm in the anterior triangle of the left neck, with SUV maximums ranging from 3.7 to 9.8. There was no hyper-metabolism in the axillary, mesenteric, retroperitoneal, or pelvic areas.

input.case_report: A previously healthy, 10-year-old male, Asian child arrived at the emergency department with a two-month history of easy bruising. There were no reports of mucocutaneous bleeding or petechiae, except for one brief episode of self-limited nosebleed two weeks prior. There was no history of recent viral illness, immunizations, drug exposure, travel, or contact with sick individuals. The review of systems was negative for bone pain, fevers, night sweats, anorexia, or weight loss. There was no family history of bleeding disorders, childhood malignancy, or autoimmune conditions. On initial examination, the child was afebrile and appeared well, with good energy and color. Numerous large bruises were observed over the bony prominences of his shoulders and elbows, and several bruises were noted on his shins. There was no noticeable lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly. The liver was palpable approximately 1.5 cm below the right costal margin. The rest of the physical exam was normal. Bloodwork performed in the emergency department showed: platelets = 17 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin = 128 g/L, white blood cell count = 5.43 × 10^9/L, lymphocytes = 2.29 × 10^9/L, and neutrophils = 1.63 × 10^9/L. A blood smear was reported as normal pending final hematopathologist review. Urate was 370 umol/L (normal = 100–277) and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) was 803 U/L (normal = 432–700). Calcium, phosphate, potassium, creatinine, PTT, and INR were all within normal limits. A chest radiograph showed no mediastinal mass or hilar adenopathy. The child was admitted to the hospital. A hematology consult was requested. Therapeutic options were discussed. Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) therapy was administered, and corticosteroids were avoided. Following IVIG therapy, the platelet count increased from 17 to 58 × 10^9/L.

input.case_report: A 60-year-old male carpenter experienced an incident where a foreign object entered his left eye while using a grinder. One month later, he reported blurred vision in the affected eye and sought medical attention. Initial treatment included levofloxacin and cefmenoxime eye drops, but there was no improvement after one week, and bacterial cultures were negative. He was then referred to our department. Upon examination, his best corrected visual acuity (BCVA) was 1.2 in the right eye and 0.04 in the left eye. Intraocular pressures (IOPs) were normal in both eyes. Slit-lamp examination of the left eye revealed ciliary injection, cells in the anterior chamber, and feathery infiltration in the upper nasal peripheral cornea with fibrin formation on the corneal endothelium. Small keratic precipitates (KPs) were observed in the lower cornea. The right eye and fundus appeared normal. Despite continued use of topical antibiotics, the feathery lesion expanded to the central cornea and developed into a ring-shaped lesion with slight hypopyon after two weeks. Cochet-Bonnet esthesiometer readings were 60 mm for the right cornea and 50 mm for the left cornea. No growth was observed in bacterial and fungal cultures. Hourly topical voriconazole 1% and miconazole 0.1% were added to the treatment regimen.

input.case_report: A 51-year-old homeless male with a history of hepatitis C, long-standing intravenous heroin use, and methamphetamine abuse arrived at the emergency department with 1 day of speech difficulties and left-sided weakness, numbness, and reduced sensation. A lumbar puncture showed normal protein and glucose levels at 37 mg/dL and 51 mg/dL, respectively. The patient's condition worsened rapidly, necessitating intubation.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 7-month-old male infant was brought to our tertiary-care hospital due to a one-month history of increasing painless abdominal swelling. His prenatal and family history were unremarkable, and his medical history included surgical correction of bilateral inguinal hernia and a diagnosis of thalassemia minor. The infant appeared healthy, with growth and neurological development appropriate for his age. Physical examination revealed a distended and tense abdomen that was painless upon deep palpation, with a positive fluid-thrill; there was no organ enlargement or abdominal masses detected. Laboratory and imaging studies showed no signs of portal hypertension or liver disease. Abdominal ultrasound revealed fluid in Morison’s pouch, the left upper abdominal quadrant, and Douglas space, appearing free in the abdomen without septation or a multi-cystic appearance, consistent with ascites. No abdominal masses or organ abnormalities were noted. Extensive testing ruled out hepatic, renal, cardiac, intestinal, pancreatic, inflammatory, infectious diseases, malignancy, and congenital metabolic disorders. The patient had a history of mild pleuro-pericardic effusion during a febrile illness and transient mild hyperkalemia, suggesting possible stomatocytosis due to a PIEZO1 mutation, but PIEZO1 gene analysis was negative. An initial positive interferon-gamma test led to consideration of peritoneal tuberculosis, but chest x-ray, Mantoux test, and gastric aspirate cultures were negative. A repeated interferon-gamma test was also negative, excluding atypical mycobacterial infections. MRI confirmed a large amount of abdominal fluid, with centralization of bowel loops and normal abdominal organs. Biochemical analysis of the ascitic fluid showed pale-yellow serous fluid with a normal serum albumin-ascites gradient (gradient = 1.1), normal triglycerides, and total leukocyte counts (98% lymphocytes). Cytological examination showed clear yellowish fluid with reactive lympho-monocytes, mesothelial cells, isolated foam cells, and no malignant cells. The fluid re-accumulated rapidly after the first paracentesis. Symptomatic palliative diuretic management was initiated with spironolactone (3 mg/Kg/daily) and furosemide (1 mg/Kg/daily) to promote a negative sodium balance and reduce ascites production. The child remained stable for a short period, but abdominal distension recurred, causing discomfort and breathing difficulty, necessitating multiple paracenteses over the following months. Repeated imaging and fluid analysis did not provide new information. Eighteen months after symptom onset, with no improvement and normal diagnostic tests, the initial diagnosis of ascites was reconsidered. Repeated US and MRI studies did not provide new information. Due to refractory ascites, recurrent hospitalizations for paracentesis, and poor quality of life, surgery was proposed. The plan included a diagnostic laparoscopy to confirm free ascites and the absence of other conditions, followed by the creation of a peritoneo-venous shunt if necessary.

input.case_report: A 75-year-old male with no significant medical history presented with a progressively enlarging lump on the left frontal area of his head, following a fall one month prior. input.case_report: A thirty-five-year-old male patient presented with double vision lasting for 6 days. He had no significant medical history and had recently recovered from a chest infection treated with antibiotics. On examination, his visual acuity was 6/5 in both eyes without correction. Color vision was normal as tested with Ishihara plates, and visual field testing by confrontation was also normal. Both pupils were dilated and unresponsive to light and accommodation. Ocular motility testing indicated bilateral fourth and sixth nerve palsy. The rest of the cranial nerves were intact. Anterior segment examination and fundoscopy were normal in both eyes. All reflexes were absent, and the patient exhibited truncal unsteadiness during heel-to-toe walking. A formal orthoptic assessment confirmed bilateral fourth and sixth nerve palsy. Pharmacological testing with 0.125% Pilocarpine resulted in miosis in both eyes. A brain MRI scan showed no abnormalities.

input.case_report: A 16-year-old female patient presented with a painful umbilical lump for a duration of 3 months. The lump had been progressively enlarging and was associated with significant pain and itching. The patient had multiple medical conditions including learning difficulties with autistic spectrum disorder due to a mutation in the TBL1XR1 gene, early onset puberty, obesity (body mass index of 35.4 kg/m²), constipation, heavy menstrual bleeding, and nighttime bedwetting attributed to presumed bladder overactivity. On physical examination, she had a firm, dark, painful, irreducible swelling, 2 cm in diameter, located in the umbilicus. The findings were consistent with an umbilical hernia with a narrow neck, possibly containing mesentery or intra-abdominal fat. The lesion was not reducible on examination.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 36-year-old woman, gravida 10 para 7 abortus 2 at 36 + 5 weeks of gestation, presented with a two-day history of increasing acute left upper quadrant pain. The pain was constant with intermittent exacerbations, radiating to her left flank. During this pregnancy, she had previously experienced abdominal pain with nausea lasting for one week, which was attributed to gastritis. Initially, the pain was thought to be due to fetal movements or uterine contractions, and she was given antispasmodics. However, the pain intensified, prompting her to seek further medical attention. She did not report nausea, vomiting, or decreased appetite but had experienced two days of constipation. Her medical history was unremarkable, and her obstetrical history included two spontaneous pregnancy losses requiring dilation and curettage in the first trimester, along with seven previous uncomplicated vaginal deliveries. Upon hospital presentation, her vital signs were as follows: blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, pulse 84 beats per minute, temperature 36.9°C, oxygen saturation 100%, and respiratory rate 20. Physical examination revealed a soft abdomen with a gravid uterus and no fundal tenderness. Bowel sounds were normal, and there was moderate tenderness in the left upper quadrant under the rib cage with mild rebound tenderness. Costovertebral angle tenderness was noted on the left side. The patient was admitted to the delivery suite and received intravenous fluid hydration, a proton pump inhibitor, and analgesia, which provided minimal relief. Nonstress test monitoring showed reassuring fetal heart rate reactivity with no contractions. Laboratory tests were largely unremarkable except for microcytic anemia with hemoglobin at 8.9 g/dl and hematocrit at 29%. Liver enzymes, pancreatic enzymes, and creatinine levels were normal. An abdominopelvic ultrasound revealed oligohydramnios and sludge in the gallbladder, with no visualization of the appendix, no gallstones, no biliary duct dilation, and no hydronephrosis. Doppler of the splenic vessels was not performed. Due to oligohydramnios, labor was induced, resulting in the delivery of a live-born boy with APGAR scores of 9 and 10 at 1 and 5 minutes, respectively, weighing 3040 grams. Postpartum, the pain persisted, and a surgical consultation recommended further imaging to evaluate the cause.

input.case_report: A 47-year-old female patient presented to the GIT Department at Smart Health Tower with a history of laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic gallstones. She reported experiencing upper abdominal pain and anorexia for 4 weeks prior to presentation. There was no vomiting or diarrhea. Her past medical history was unremarkable. Physical examination revealed yellow discoloration of the sclera without pallor. The abdomen was soft, non-distended, and non-tender upon palpation, with no evidence of organomegaly. Bowel sounds were present. A digital rectal examination showed normal-colored stool. Vital signs were within normal limits. Blood tests, including white blood cell count, serum creatinine, blood urea, C-reactive protein, serum amylase, carcinoembryonic antigen, and CA 19-9 levels, were all within normal ranges. However, the liver function profile was elevated, with total serum bilirubin at 4.2 mg/dl and alkaline phosphatase at 250 IU/l. Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) showed a dilated proximal bile duct and mild dilatation of the intrahepatic biliary tree due to a 1-cm stricture located 16 mm distal to the confluence of the right and left hepatic ducts. Dynamic liver magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) confirmed the bile duct stricture due to a clip on the bile duct. The case was reviewed by a multidisciplinary team, and surgery was decided upon. During surgery under general anesthesia, a laparotomy revealed a hard bile duct mass measuring 2 cm in its greatest dimension, along with multiple enlarged lymph nodes in the peri-hepatic region. Bile duct excision with proximal and distal safe margins and peri-hepatic lymphadenectomy (14 lymph nodes resected) was performed. A Roux-en-Y jejunostomy was also carried out, and a drain was placed in the subhepatic region.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: The patient is a 27-year-old Javanese male with notable physical features and borderline intellectual functioning. His parents, both Javanese and non-consanguineous, are in their sixties and have no history of similar conditions or congenital abnormalities. He is the third child, born after a normal pregnancy and cesarean delivery, with two healthy sisters. There was no reported trauma, infection, or drug use during the pregnancy. The patient attends a special needs school in Cirebon, West Java, Indonesia, where he engages in social activities, drawing, and music. On physical examination, his weight is 36 kg, height 158 cm, and occipital frontal circumference 54 cm. Observed features include hypertelorism, down-slanting palpebral fissures, strabismus, ocular proptosis, depressed nasal bridge, short philtrum, and low-set ears. Additional findings include acrocephaly, asymmetrical flat facies, nasal deformity, and a prominent jaw. Oral examination revealed maxillary hypoplasia with a high arch palate, a V-shaped maxillary arch with double rows of teeth, and dental fusion between the maxillary premolar and first molar. Panoramic radiographs confirmed these dental abnormalities. Other findings include mild scoliosis and mild pectus excavatum. The patient also has symmetrical cutaneous bilateral syndactyly involving four fingers on each hand, with spoon-shaped palms and inwardly placed thumbs (type 2). input.case_report: A 49-year-old male arrived at our emergency department with a 2-hour history of sudden onset aphasia and right-sided paralysis. His wife reported a transient ischemic attack three months prior, characterized by dysarthria, right hemiparesis, and right hemihypesthesia lasting 15 minutes, which went untreated. She also mentioned a hospitalization for meningitis three years ago, though no medical records were available. Given the acute onset and stroke-like presentation, a head CT scan was performed, ruling out hemorrhage. Intravenous thrombolysis with Alteplase was initiated. Fifteen minutes into the procedure, mild gingival bleeding was observed, leading to the interruption of Alteplase infusion. After bleeding ceased, the patient remained hemodynamically stable but began perseverating a few words. An hour later, he experienced explosive vomiting, shivering, and a temperature rise to 37.5°C. Two hours later, a focal myoclonic seizure in the right arm occurred, and treatment with intravenous midazolam was administered. Throughout the day, the patient's symptoms fluctuated; he became somnolent, responded only to repetitive verbal stimuli, did not follow commands, and motor aphasia reappeared. Despite antipyretic treatment, shivering persisted, and his temperature rose to 39.5°C. He became hypotensive (90/60 mmHg) and tachycardic (130 beats/min). Over the next 12 hours, myoclonic seizures increased in frequency, with two generalized episodes. The patient became more somnolent, responding only to painful stimuli, with the only motor response being a flexion of the right superior limb to pain. Empiric treatment with intravenous acyclovir was initiated. Brain MRI showed cortical enhancement in the left frontal lobe on diffusion-weighted images.

Both cystic lesions had thick walls and contained turbid, tenacious fluid. Abdominal ultrasound showed no renal masses or perinephric collections, and no defined intestinal masses or calcifications. The gynecologic ultrasound showed a small amount of fluid in the endocervical canal with a thickness of 1.2 mm, and normal ovaries with some bilateral cystic structures likely of functional nature. The bladder ultrasound noted a vaguely nodular thickening area in the anterior wall, measuring 9 mm × 4 mm with relatively regular contours. Urinalysis and urine culture results were unremarkable. Given these findings and observed clinical improvement, a follow-up pelvic ultrasound was scheduled for 2 months later. The patient later visited the emergency department with painful urination, frequent urination, and urinary urgency. These symptoms were treated as cystitis with a single dose of fosfomycin. A follow-up bladder ultrasound, performed 2 months after the initial one, revealed a hypoechoic nodule on the anterior wall, measuring 16 mm × 11 mm × 6 mm, with regular contours, well-defined limits, and homogeneous texture, suggesting a benign lesion. The patient was referred to a urology consultation. Approximately 2 months after this referral and 1 month before the urology consultation, she returned to the emergency department with painful and frequent urination. A urine dipstick analysis indicated cystitis, and she was prescribed norfloxacin.

input.case_report: The patient is a 25-year-old African American male who initially presented with right eye pain and bilateral blurry vision. A month later, he was seen by a retinal specialist and underwent fluorescein angiography that revealed bilateral serous retinal detachments. He was started on prednisone 60 mg daily, with a plan to taper by 10 mg over the course of four weeks. He was initially compliant with his steroid regimen, but due to symptoms of anxiety and jitteriness, he stopped taking it after three weeks. Subsequently, his vision worsened, and a few days later, he developed right eye ptosis, nausea, vomiting, headache, and difficulty swallowing liquids. He presented to the emergency room a few days after. Examination revealed right eye ptosis with a 7-mm unreactive pupil and limited movement in all directions as well as impaired abduction of the left eye. He underwent MRI brain, MRA head and neck, and MR orbits, although only non-contrast images were obtained due to patient anxiety and body habitus, and all imaging sequences were unremarkable. Prednisone was recommenced 60 mg daily. About two days later, he was run as a stroke code with emergent imaging for new-onset tongue deviation, slurred speech, and diminished hearing in his right ear. MRI brain with and without contrast noted extensive cranial nerve thickening and enhancement, including bilateral cranial nerves II and III, left cranial nerve IV, bilateral cranial nerve V, left cranial nerve VI, right greater than left cranial nerve VII and VIII, and left cranial nerve XII. He was newly diagnosed with HIV based on positive HIV1 antibody titer, with 63,000 copies/mL and CD4 count 41 cells/μL. Further infectious workup returned positive for Hepatitis A, with negative antibodies for CMV, HSV, VZV, Hepatitis B, toxoplasma, coccidioidomycosis, aspergillus, gonorrhea, and chlamydia, as well as negative RPR and quantiferon. Lumbar puncture was performed and revealed glucose 62, protein 195, WBC 83 with 94% lymphocytes, and RBC 1460. CSF was negative for oligoclonal bands, with normal MBP and IgG index. CSF cytology was negative, and flow cytometry was not obtained. CSF infectious workup was positive for EBV with 150,000 copies/mL and was negative for HSV 1/2, cytomegalovirus (CMV), varicella zoster virus (VZV), HHV 6/7, mumps, measles, West Nile virus, mycobacterium, enterovirus, and cryptococcus. Serum and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) bacterial, fungal, and AFB cultures were negative. Serum autoimmune panel returned positive for ANA with mildly elevated RNP of 1.1 and elevated SS-A of 2.4. Ganglioside panel was notable only for elevated GD1a antibody of 72. Serum and CSF paraneoplastic panel including LGI1, CASPR2, AMPAR 1/2, GABAR, and NMDAR were negative. He was started on sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim, fluconazole, and azithromycin prophylaxis for his low CD4 count. Given his symptomatic worsening following cessation of steroids, he was treated empirically with IV methylprednisolone 1000 mg daily for three days and was continued on prednisone 60 mg daily. This was followed by empiric treatment with IVIG 0.5 g/kg divided over four days. On day 9 of hospitalization following completion of IVIG, he was started on emtricitabine and raltegravir. His right eye ptosis improved, but on day 10, he started to develop a left eye third nerve palsy with restricted eye movements. On day 11, he was noted to have a loss of reflexes. On day 13, he started to complain of dysphagia and dysarthria and developed limb weakness (4-/5 ankle dorsiflexion, 4-/5 hip flexion, 4-/5 finger abduction, 4+/5 elbow extension, and 4/5 shoulder abduction), so he was treated with a five-day course of plasma exchange (PLEX). On day 14, a fiberoptic endoscopic evaluation of swallowing was performed noting right vocal cord paralysis, edema, and copious secretions. On day 16, EMG of the left upper and lower extremity was performed, which revealed only a left ulnar motor neuropathy, and MRI of the cervical and lumbar spine showed thickening and enhancement of multiple ventral and dorsal cervical and lumbar nerve roots. Due to continued dysphagia, he underwent EGD with gastric and duodenal biopsies and percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) placement. He had nominal subjective improvement in strength and return of biceps reflex (1+), therefore was discharged to a rehabilitation center on prednisone 60 mg daily. One week later, he was readmitted due to persistent nausea and vomiting. CT of chest, abdomen, and pelvis areas was negative.

input.case_report: A 17-year-old male presented with lower abdominal pain in September 2014. Abdominal computed tomography (CT) identified a solid mass measuring approximately 74 mm × 45 mm attached to the greater curvature of the stomach. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy showed no significant findings. He was referred to our hospital in December 2014 for further management. Subsequent CT at our facility revealed a solid mass with slightly inhomogeneous enhancement, measuring 57 mm × 44 mm, indicating a slight reduction in size. Follow-up observation was suggested. In March 2015, a third CT scan showed the mass had further reduced to 56 mm × 37 mm with the same enhancement pattern. Continued observation was recommended. In April 2015, MRI demonstrated a tumor with isointensity to the spleen on T1-weighted images and slightly inhomogeneous hypointensity on T2-weighted images. A fourth CT in June 2015 revealed further reduction to 49 mm × 34 mm. Despite the gradual shrinkage, a definitive nature of the mass could not be established. The patient remained asymptomatic, including no lower abdominal pain. A surgical procedure was chosen for treatment. Laparoscopic partial gastrectomy with partial splenectomy was performed using an automatic suture device. Operative findings showed a hard mass firmly attached to the greater curvature of the stomach and the inferior pole of the spleen. The branches of the right and left gastroepiploic arteries supplied the tumor. The feeding artery was clipped, and the tumor was detached from the stomach and spleen using an automatic suture device. Macroscopically, the tumor measured 60 mm × 50 mm × 25 mm, with a pink and uniform cut surface. Microscopically, the tumor showed proliferation of spindle-shaped cells and dense collagen bundles, primarily at the muscularis propria of the stomach, and was identified as a stomach-derived mass.

Intravenous immunoglobulin (0.4 g/kg per day) was administered again for 5 days, leading to clinical improvement. Repeated NCS showed no evoked responses in the median, ulnar, tibial, and common peroneal nerves, indicating severe demyelination. Intravenous immunoglobulin (0.4 g/kg per day) was administered for 5 days with only slight symptom improvement. Four days after the last discharge, the patient presented to the emergency department with severe pain in the right lower abdominal quadrant and fever. He had no prior gastrointestinal symptoms, including diarrhea. Abdominal examination revealed tenderness and rebound tenderness in the right lower quadrant. Abdominal computed tomography (CT) suggested a periappendiceal abscess, leading to emergent surgical resection of the ileum, cecum, and appendix.

input.case_report: A 52-year-old woman (weight, 59 kg; height, 162 cm) was scheduled for left microvascular decompression due to involuntary left facial movement. Her medical history indicated good functional capacity without respiratory issues. She was on 150 µg levothyroxine for hypothyroidism. There was no other significant medical history, including tracheal intubation, and routine airway evaluation, including Mallampati class, showed no abnormalities. Preoperative assessments, including a chest radiograph, electrocardiogram, spirometry, and routine laboratory tests, were normal. Standard monitoring equipment, such as an electrocardiogram, pulse oximetry, and noninvasive blood pressure monitor, were used. After preoxygenation with 100% oxygen, general anesthesia was induced using a target-controlling infusion system (Orchestra; Fresenius Vial, Brezins, France) with propofol and remifentanil. Upon confirming loss of consciousness via the eyelid reflex, 50 mg rocuronium was administered. Manual mask ventilation was performed without difficulty. The vocal cords were visualized using conventional direct laryngoscopy, appearing as Cormack–Lehane grade 1. A 6.5-mm inner diameter (ID) reinforced endotracheal tube (8.9-mm outer diameter [OD]; Lo-Counter Oral/Nasal Tracheal Tube cuffed, Shiley™; Covidien, Bedford, MA, USA) passed through the vocal cords easily but could not be advanced further due to resistance. The tube was withdrawn, and a smaller tube (6.0-mm ID and 8.4-mm OD) was attempted, but it also could not be advanced past the same point. Assistance was requested, and another anesthesiologist made a third attempt with a 6.0-mm ID tube, which also failed. Further attempts were deemed inappropriate due to the potential for vocal cord and tracheal edema. The operation was postponed for further evaluation. The procedure lasted 15 minutes. The patient was awakened with 600 mg (10 mg/kg) sugammadex (Bridion; Merck Sharp & Dohme, Kenilworth, NJ, USA). Dexamethasone (5 mg) was administered for edema. A neurosurgeon consulted an otolaryngologist for airway evaluation. Flexible endoscopy by the otolaryngologist revealed no tracheal abnormalities. The patient and the neurosurgeon requested anesthesia later the same day, suspecting a transient tracheal spasm. A second anesthesia was induced with propofol and cisatracurium (Nimbex; Mitsubishi Tanabe Pharma Co., Osaka, Japan) due to the prior use of sugammadex 6 hours earlier. Manual mask ventilation was again performed without difficulty. Before attempting tracheal intubation, further evaluation was considered to identify the cause of the resistance.

Admission labs indicated a WBC of 18,000/μL with 91.1% neutrophils, a C-reactive protein level of 429.8 mg/L, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 123 mm/h. Post-procedure, the patient showed significant improvement, with WBC and creatinine levels decreasing to near normal by discharge. Maintenance immunosuppression was managed with tacrolimus (dose adjusted to maintain a trough level of 8-10 ng/mL during the first year, 6-8 ng/mL during the second year, and 4-7 ng/mL thereafter) and mycophenolate mofetil (500 mg twice daily), with early steroid withdrawal. Prophylaxis against Pneumocystis jiroveci infection was provided with trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (400/80 mg) once daily. The allografts began functioning shortly after transplantation without delayed graft function. Serum creatinine stabilized at 1.3 mg/dL, and the patient maintained euglycemia. Six years post-transplant, serum creatinine increased to 1.8 mg/dL with a blood urea nitrogen level of 20 mg/dL. Urinalysis indicated proteinuria and microhematuria. Additional laboratory values included: WBC 3.29 µ/mL, hemoglobin 10.7 g/dL, platelets 209 µ/mL, serum albumin 3.7 g/dL, serum total cholesterol 167 mg/dL, fasting blood glucose 102 mg/dL, and trough tacrolimus level 5.4 ng/mL. Tests for CMV, BK virus, and Epstein-Barr virus PCR were negative. The patient exhibited proteinuria of 5 g/d. Further laboratory work-up showed negative results for serum ANA, anti-double-stranded DNA, cryoglobulins, rheumatoid factor, anti-streptolysin O, proteinase-3 and myeloperoxidase ANCA, as well as hepatitis B and C antibodies. Serum complement levels and immunoglobulins were within normal ranges, and no monoclonal bands were detected on serum and urine immunofixation electrophoresis. Serum free light chain assay revealed elevated free kappa (33.8 mg/dL, normal range 3.3-19.4) and normal lambda (25.1 mg/dL, normal range 5.7-26.3) fractions.

input.case_report: A 4-month-old female infant was referred to the ophthalmology unit for a unilateral red eye, sensitivity to light, excessive tearing, and spontaneous bleeding in the eye (OD). The mother reported the appearance of several red-orange skin nodules on the thorax, head, and neck at 2 weeks of age. Over time, these lesions increased in number and some developed crusts or mild superficial bleeding. A dermatologist was consulted, and skin biopsies were performed. A bone marrow biopsy was conducted, which excluded any malignant process. No treatment was indicated, and observation was recommended. At 3 months, the mother noticed a color change in the OD and consulted an ophthalmologist, who observed the aforementioned symptoms and referred the child to a tertiary center for further evaluation. The general clinical examination was normal except for numerous cutaneous nodules, varying in color from yellowish to bright red, primarily on the head, neck, and thorax, with fewer lesions on the arms and legs. All additional investigations, including laboratory tests and imaging scans, were within normal limits and showed no internal organ lesions or other systemic alterations. The ophthalmology exam revealed visual acuity in both eyes as “fixes and follows.” Under general anesthesia, intraocular pressure was 25 mmHg in OD and 12 mmHg in OS. The corneal diameter was increased in OD (12 mm) compared to OS (10 mm), corresponding with increased axial lengths in OD (19.12 mm) versus OS (18.49 mm) as measured by A-scan ultrasound. Anterior segment examination showed normal structures in OS, while OD exhibited mild epithelial corneal edema, peripheral iris synechiae (360 degrees), a thick fibrinoid membrane on the iris surface, a 2mm hyphema, and a wide blood clot on the iris surface (11-4 o’clock meridians). Fundus examination was not possible in OD due to low visibility, while OS appeared normal. B-scan ultrasound of OD showed no abnormalities in the choroid, retina, or optic nerve.

Other negative tests included thick blood smear, latex agglutination test, anti-Smith antibodies, hemoglobin electrophoresis, and liver and kidney type 1 anti-microsomal antibody (anti-LKM-1). Abdominal ultrasound showed an enlarged liver (11.1 cm) with altered echogenicity, defined borders, no masses or cysts, and a patent portal vein measuring 6.2 mm. input.case_report: An 85-year-old male presented to the respiratory medicine department in August 2018 with left-sided pleural effusion. He did not report symptoms such as dyspnea, cough, sputum production, chest pain, or fatigue. The patient had a significant smoking history of 75 pack-years and used a wheelchair. His medical history included coronary artery disease, chronic kidney disease, surgically treated meningioma, and Alzheimer's disease. He had regular follow-ups in the cardiology department and no family history of cardiopulmonary diseases. He had a 20-year history of silica exposure from ages 9 to 28, working as a quarry worker for 17 years and a tunnel worker for 3 years without using a dust mask. On examination, he was afebrile with an oxygen saturation of 96% on room air. Chest auscultation revealed normal breath sounds without adventitious sounds, and the rest of the physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory tests, including complete blood count, liver function tests, thyroid hormone levels, tumor markers, anti-nuclear antibody, and serum free light chain assays, were within normal limits. Tests for QuantiFERON-TB® and autoantibodies associated with connective tissue diseases were negative. Elevated levels of brain natriuretic peptide (77.7 pg/mL) and creatinine (1.67 mg/dL) were noted, consistent with previous measurements from a year prior. Echocardiography showed a normal ejection fraction, known akinesis of the anteroseptal wall, and no new or worsening wall motion abnormalities. Chest CT identified left-sided pleural effusion, small nodules in the right upper lung fields, and egg-shell calcification of the hilar and mediastinal lymph nodes, with no signs of asbestos exposure such as pleural thickening or plaques. Thoracentesis revealed a clear, yellow-colored, lymphocyte-predominant exudative pleural effusion with fluid protein at 4.3 g/dL, serum protein at 6.6 g/dL, fluid lactate dehydrogenase at 341 U/L, and serum lactate dehydrogenase at 230 U/L. Cytological analysis classified the effusion as class II, showing atypical cells but no malignancy, and Gram staining was negative. No treatment was administered as the pleural effusion was asymptomatic.

input.case_report: The patient, a 15-year-old boy, was born to healthy, non-consanguineous Japanese parents through a normal delivery at 39 weeks of gestation, weighing 3090 g. At six months old, he developed tachypnea and muscle hypotonia, necessitating hospitalization. Physical examination revealed delayed motor development, including incomplete head control and rolling due to hypotonia. Two months later, tonic spasms and hypsarrhythmia on EEG were observed. Brain MRI results were normal. Treatment with vitamin B6 and valproate led to the cessation of seizures and abnormal EEG findings. At 13 months, exacerbation of symptoms required ACTH therapy. Routine lab tests showed mildly elevated transaminases: AST ranged from 77–170 IU/L (normal, 8–38 IU/L) and ALT from 58–206 IU/L (normal, 4–44 IU/L), while serum direct bilirubin and PT-INR values were normal during infancy. A transient increase in serum alkaline phosphatase (up to 16,480 IU/L) was noted during ACTH therapy, with no identified cause. Despite the success of ACTH therapy, focal spikes persisted on EEG, necessitating ongoing anticonvulsant use. At 15 months, a viral infection led to increased transaminase activities and decreased coagulation activities, including prolonged PT/APTT and low antithrombin levels: AST 465 IU/L, ALT 122 IU/L, PT-INR 1.48, APTT 48.4 sec (reference 33.0 sec), and AT activity 29%. Protein C and protein S activities were also 50% below normal. These findings improved within two weeks without severe acute liver failure symptoms. Horizontal nystagmus appeared around the same time, but the patient could sit unaided, smile at caregivers, and showed interest in playing with toys. He developed slowly, walking unaided at 3 years and speaking several words by 4 years. Although EEG continued to show spikes and waves mainly over the frontal area, he remained seizure-free.

input.case_report: An 83-year-old man was admitted with worsening shortness of breath on exertion for 3 days. His medical history included high blood pressure, aortic stenosis, heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, and chronic kidney disease. A transthoracic echocardiogram showed normal left ventricular function and a moderate decrease in right ventricular function. There was moderate aortic stenosis with a peak velocity of 2.9 m/s and an estimated area by velocity of 1.43 cm². The dimensionless velocity index was 0.34. The exam was inadequate for assessing the atrial level shunt by color flow imaging. The patient underwent left heart catheterization, which revealed multivessel coronary artery disease and moderate aortic stenosis. He subsequently had an urgent sternotomy and 4-vessel coronary artery bypass graft surgery without intervention to the aortic valve. The patient had a myocardial infarction, the aortic stenosis was graded as moderate, and he had not reported symptoms related to aortic stenosis before his hospital admission. His preferences regarding valve replacement were not discussed prior to the procedure. General anesthesia was maintained without major intraoperative events.

input.case_report: The patient is a 31-year-old African-American, left-handed female with a recent history of hypertension. She experienced left-handed clumsiness, particularly in her left index finger and thumb, along with an episode of slurred speech and left-sided facial numbness lasting between 30 minutes to 1 hour. Initially evaluated at a local hospital, she had an abnormal EKG showing T wave inversions in leads V2–V4 and left ventricular hypertrophy. The patient declined admission and was admitted to our institution 10 days later. Her symptoms improved two days post-episode, but she continued to have difficulty with fine motor tasks such as typing. During her stay, her oxygen saturation was maintained at 95-96%. An MRI of the brain revealed a subacute infarct in the right precentral gyrus, affecting the hand knob area, which correlated with her left hand weakness. Transthoracic echocardiography showed moderate left ventricular hypertrophy without regional wall motion abnormalities, a mildly dilated left atrium, and an increased mitral valve E point to ventricular septal separation. The visually estimated ejection fraction was 45–50%. There was no evidence of right-to-left shunting on an agitated saline bubble contrast study performed in the right arm. Chest radiography indicated abnormal soft tissue densities along the right paratracheal region extending to the right main stem bronchus and along the right cardiomediastinal margin, which was further evaluated.

input.case_report: A 66-year-old African American male with a medical history of paranoid schizophrenia, diabetes, hypertension, and asthma experienced dizziness and fell on his right shoulder, missing his morning insulin dose. Physical examination showed ecchymosis over the right upper arm, blood pressure of 90/68 mmHg, heart rate of 66, and respiratory rate of 18. Treatment included intravenous hydration, subcutaneous insulin, and chlorpromazine 200 mg three times daily, which he had been taking for 10 years for schizophrenia. A CT scan of the head showed mild cortical atrophy without acute changes. An X-ray revealed a mildly displaced fracture of the right humeral neck, with orthopedic surgery recommending no surgical intervention. On the second day, glucose level was 123, venous blood gas pH was 7.44, and creatinine was 0.4 (normal). The patient developed tachycardia with a heart rate of 110 and blood pressure of 144/90 mmHg. Over the next three days, blood pressure fluctuated between 103/70 and 150/94 mmHg, and heart rate ranged from 109 to 128. The patient became lethargic, with slurred, mumbled, and mostly inaudible speech, but was able to follow simple commands and name three objects.

input.case_report: A 3-year-old Caucasian boy was referred from the accident and emergency department to the pediatric surgical team with a history of sudden onset abdominal pain. The pain was central and accompanied by fever, non-bilious vomiting, and the passage of red, jelly-like stools. There was no history of weight loss, jaundice, foreign travel, contact with sick individuals, or recent dietary changes. The child had a mild upper respiratory tract infection but was otherwise healthy with no significant medical history. On examination, the child appeared well-nourished but was miserable and lethargic with a fever of 38.1°C and a heart rate of 120 beats per minute. He was warm and well-perfused with a capillary refill time of less than 2 seconds both centrally and peripherally. His abdomen was tender, distended, and resonant, with hyperactive bowel sounds and a small, tender, rubbery mass palpable centrally. An ultrasound scan confirmed the presence of an intussusception. A contrast enema was performed, and control films showed a filling defect in the ascending colon at the site of the colo-colic intussusception. During a subsequent air enema, air insufflation to a pressure of 120 mmHg successfully reduced the intussusception. The child was kept in the hospital overnight for observation. By the following day, his condition had improved with the resolution of abdominal pain; he was able to eat, drink, and interact with his parents and staff. However, he continued to have a remittent fever up to 38.5°C and remained lethargic and miserable. He subsequently developed a diffuse morbilliform rash over the trunk and limbs, and cervical lymphadenopathy was noted along with a congested throat and enlarged tonsils. Blood tests showed a C-reactive protein level of 61 mg/L, with normal blood count, serum electrolytes, liver function tests, and chest X-ray. Ebstein Barr viral serology, blood cultures, and urine cultures were negative. Five days after the initial presentation, he developed conjunctival edema and edema of the hands and feet with desquamation of the fingertips and cracking of the lips; there was no evidence of a strawberry tongue.

No petechial rash, splinter hemorrhage, or Roth spots were observed. Chest x-ray showed an enlarged cardiac silhouette with bilateral interstitial opacities. There was no hemolysis, with normal reticulocyte count and bilirubin, and elevated haptoglobin. Blood urea nitrogen and creatinine levels were elevated at 57 and 3.4 mg/dl, respectively. Viral screening was negative for HIV antibody, hepatitis B surface antigen, and hepatitis C antibody.

input.case_report: A 58-year-old woman visited the Coburg Clinic in February 2013 with symptoms of shortness of breath, fatigue, mild swelling in the lower legs, and a 4 kg weight loss over the previous 4 weeks. Her medical history included only an appendectomy over 30 years ago, with no other hospitalizations or medications. She had never smoked. She had worked as a seamstress until 1973 and later in the ceramics industry from 1976 to 1980 and 1985 to 1997, without significant exposure to ceramic dust. Annual chest X-rays during that period showed no abnormalities. During a follow-up examination, she reported experiencing exercise-induced shortness of breath since 2003 but had maintained her regular work activities. An electrocardiogram on admission showed sinus tachycardia at 109 beats per minute, incomplete right bundle branch block, and preterminal inverted T-waves in leads II, III, aVF, and V1–V6. Body plethysmography indicated slight restriction with a forced expiratory volume in one second at 68% predicted, inspiratory vital capacity at 70% predicted, and a Tiffeneau index of 101%. Her diffusion capacity was reduced to 60%. Oxygen therapy was administered for respiratory insufficiency (pO2 60 mmHg and pCO2 33 mmHg with 3 L per minute of oxygen). A transthoracic echocardiogram on February 1, 2013, showed right ventricular dilation to 38 mm, left atrial dilation to 43 mm, aortic valve sclerosis, minimal aortic insufficiency, first-degree mitral insufficiency, significant tricuspid valve insufficiency with a diastolic gradient of 67 mmHg, and diastolic dysfunction of the left ventricle with a slightly abnormal E/A ratio. The patient presented to the pulmonary consultant with shortness of breath and an oxygen saturation of 83% on the bronchoscopy table (pO2 38 mmHg, pCO2 31 mmHg). She reported a rapid decline over the previous 2 days compared to her initial admission. Her weight was 58 kg, with a reported 19 kg weight loss over the past 2 months. Her height was 158 cm (BMI 23.1 kg/m2). A chest X-ray taken on admission showed a prominent bihilar region with a suspicious mass in the left lung apex. input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: Ms. W, a 33-year-old Caucasian female with a history of multiple sclerosis, chronic low back pain, and migraines, initially presented to a rural hospital emergency department with confusion and was discharged with an Ativan prescription. She returned within days and was admitted to the county’s psychiatric observation unit for paranoia, thought disorganization, irritability, and impulsivity. Communication with her primary care provider, family, and neurologist confirmed her history of multiple sclerosis, diagnosed three years prior, for which she had not been receiving treatment. She had experienced significant weight loss that year, and her mother noted anxiety, paranoia, and hyperactivity within the past month. Speech abnormalities, such as stuttering or stammering, were observed. Her thought content included themes of having a tampon blocking her vagina, being pregnant, being an immigrant, and deserving punishment. Her speech later evolved to reduced verbal output. A history of substance abuse and a positive urine toxicology for cannabinoids led to an initial treatment with oral low dose atypical anti-psychotics, but adherence was infrequent due to paranoia. No symptom resolution after 6 days led to further investigation. Given her history of multiple sclerosis, an MRI of her brain was obtained on day 7 of admission, showing possible acute or subacute changes with multifocal bilateral white matter lesions in the cerebral hemispheres and small lesions in the right posterior parietal and left occipital region. Internal medicine noted diplopia on extreme gaze to the left side. Cognitive screening tests were not possible due to the patient’s paranoia, but staff observed mental status fluctuations. Extensive investigation for delirium was suggested, and on day 8, the patient was transferred to medicine. Laboratory tests including complete blood count, blood sugar, blood urea nitrogen, creatinine, electrolytes, troponin, liver function tests, thyroid stimulating hormone, rheumatoid factor, and human immunodeficiency virus were all within normal limits or negative. Serum antinuclear antibodies (ANA), extractable nuclear antigen screen (ENA), and anti ribonucleoprotein (RNP) were positive, with ANA positivity characterized by a titre of 1:80 and a nucleolar pattern. On day 10, cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis showed normal protein and glucose levels, but 39 total nucleated cells with 100% lymphocytes. No organisms were noted on the gram stain. Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV) and Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) tests were ordered, and Acyclovir IV was started. HSV, HIV, and hepatitis paneling returned negative. A neurotropic virus such as syphilis was also considered, but antibody screening was non-reactive. On the date of her lumbar puncture, the patient reported recent crystal methamphetamine use. Consultation-liaison psychiatry services were consulted on day 13, noting continued anxiety, fear, and confusion. She had insight into her confusion and distress about memory loss, requiring constant reminders and redirection. Themes of paranoia remained, and she denied perceptual disturbances. Her symptoms did not improve in the absence of substance use and antipsychotics. Antibody panels for neuronal surface antigens were still pending. Lorazepam 2 mg by mouth or intramuscular every 4 h as needed was ordered for agitation. Steroids were recommended in the absence of an infectious cause. Neurology consultation was recommended for guidance on immunotherapy. Substance-induced psychosis versus primary psychosis remained the primary consideration, and she was treated with risperidone, but adherence was sporadic. Her symptoms continued to fluctuate, and she remained thought disordered and anxious. Previous MRI scans became available on day 18, and comparison indicated that other than one lesion in the splenium of the corpus callosum, white matter lesions were relatively unchanged. Neurology consultation on day 19 led to the initiation of Methylprednisolone 1 g IV every 24 h for three days. Significant improvement in cognition was noted, with improved arousal, attention, awareness, executive functioning, and memory. Her paranoia and fear also improved, but she remained somatically preoccupied with delusions of a damaged or blocked reproductive tract.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 40-year-old woman, who had her first child, came to the hospital with fever and ongoing acute abdominal pain. She had an emergency C-section 45 days earlier due to fetal heart rate issues, delivering a healthy baby boy weighing 3000 grams. During her pregnancy, she took iron supplements intermittently and was on aspirin 100 mg/day from early pregnancy until the 38th week, followed by low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) for 2 weeks. On the day of delivery, her blood count was normal with mild anisocytosis, and her postoperative recovery was uneventful. During her initial hospital stay, she was given oxytocin, diclofenac, and a second-generation cephalosporin antibiotic for 3 days. She was discharged 4 days later with LMWH for venous thrombosis prevention. Upon re-admission, her clinical appearance and nutritional status were normal, lung auscultation was normal, and her abdomen was soft and tender with a palpable spleen. No neurological deficits were noted. She reported frequent headaches and self-treated iron deficiency anemia. Prior to re-admission, she had a full blood count and an abdominal ultrasound at a private clinic. The ultrasound showed a normal liver, no bile duct dilation, but a dilated splenoportal axis with echogenic content and no blood flow. The gall bladder was normal, and the pancreas was enlarged with heterogeneous consistency. The spleen was enlarged (14.7 × 6 cm) with a small area of reduced echogenicity, indicating possible splenomegaly and partial ischemia. A new full blood count, CT scan, MRI scan, and gastroscopy were performed upon her arrival at the hospital. The CT scan revealed an enlarged spleen with infarcts, splenoportal axis dilation with a subdense image throughout its course, and extensive thrombosis in the portal vein and its branches. The pancreas had an edematous texture, and there was distention of small intestine helices with mesenteric vessel congestion. The MRI scan showed no blood flow in the upper mesenteric vein and throughout the splenoportal axis, indicating extensive thrombosis. The spleen was enlarged with extensive ischemic areas, and the pancreas, kidneys, inferior vena cava, and hepatic veins were normal. The gastroscopy showed antral gastritis with two punctuate intramucosal infarcts in the bulb. A thrombophilia profile test was performed, revealing MTHFR C677T variant heterozygosity. The patient was treated with fondaparinux sodium 7500 IU subcutaneously per day, acenocoumarol, and meropenem 2g three times a day. She was discharged 15 days later with instructions to continue anticoagulant therapy. Her follow-up treatment was changed to LMWH after 7 months of acenocoumarol use. One year later, abnormal liver laboratory values were found during a follow-up blood screen, leading to a second hospital admission two months later, where portal hypertension and esophageal varices were confirmed. LMWH treatment was replaced again by acenocoumarol.

input.case_report: The patient, a 28-year-old man, was admitted to the Endocrinology Department of Peking University International Hospital on November 15, 2022, due to six years of excessive drinking and urination, and six months of intermittent nausea and vomiting. A posterior examination revealed a fasting plasma glucose level of 8.5 mmol/L (normal range: 3.9–6.1 mmol/L). He was treated with short-acting insulin for meals and long-acting insulin before bed for hyperglycemia. He was hospitalized multiple times for diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) due to irregular insulin injections. Upon discharge, he was prescribed preprandial subcutaneous insulin lispro and presleep subcutaneous insulin glargine, with daily insulin doses ranging from 47 to 64 units, adjusted based on blood glucose levels. Six months ago, he began experiencing intermittent nausea and vomiting without abdominal pain or diarrhea. Random intravenous plasma glucose was 21.4 mmol/L, arterial blood gas pH was 7.37, and urine ketone body was 3+. Symptoms improved, and ketone bodies were negative after rehydration and insulin supplementation. Post-discharge, he continued to have intermittent nausea, vomiting, abdominal distension, and pain, especially after meals. He was prescribed itopride hydrochloride tablets, pancreatin enteric-coated capsules, and pinaverium bromide tablets. Psychologists evaluated his anxiety and prescribed duloxetine and oxazepam. The treatment was ineffective, leading him to stop insulin injections and occasionally measure random peripheral blood glucose > 20 mmol/L. He was admitted for further treatment. During his diabetes management, he did not experience blurred vision, limb numbness, cold sensations, tingling, sleeve-like sensations, or intermittent claudication. He lost approximately 5 kg over 9 months. His past medical history includes a renal cyst, kidney stone, and prostate cyst diagnosed 3.5 years ago, 8 months ago, and 6 months ago, respectively. He denied a history of pancreatitis or pancreatic surgery. He was born at full term with a birth weight of 2.5 kg without hypoglycemia. His growth and development were comparable to his peers. His family history was negative for diabetes. Physical examination revealed: temperature 36.2°C, pulse 72/min, respiration 20/min, blood pressure 140/87 mmHg, height 175 cm, weight 50 kg, BMI 16.33 kg/m2, waistline 65 cm, hip 77 cm, and waist-to-hip ratio 0.84. His serum lactate was 3.2 mmol/L, international normalized ratio (INR) was 2.44, creatinine was 1.1 mg/dL, and procalcitonin was 0.28 ng/mL. input.case_report: A 53-year-old man was admitted to the hospital with complaints of impotence, intermittent claudication, and a sensation of coldness in his lower extremities. He had a history of smoking from age 17 to 52, averaging 15 cigarettes per day (Brinkman Index: 525). His family history included a father with type 2 diabetes, a mother with angina pectoris, type 2 diabetes, and renal insufficiency of unknown origin, an elder brother who had a cerebral infarction at age 53, and a younger sister who died from sudden renal failure at age 48. The patient frequently experienced nosebleeds since childhood. At age 46, he was diagnosed with effort angina, and coronary angiography showed 75% stenosis in the right coronary artery #2, leading to stent implantation. At age 52, he experienced chest pain on exertion again, and left coronary angiography revealed 75% stenosis in the left main trunk ostium and moderate to severe diffuse stenosis from the ostium to the distal portion of the right coronary artery. Due to severe coronary artery stenosis, including the left main trunk and right coronary artery, he was scheduled for coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery. He was administered aspirin, ethyl icosapentate, and pitavastatin. At that time, due to profound systemic atherosclerosis, a re-evaluation of atherosclerosis risk factors was conducted. His body mass index was 23.4 kg/m2. Physical examination revealed orange-colored swollen tonsils and mild hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory results showed HDL-C at 2 mg/dL and undetectable apolipoprotein A-1 (ApoA1). Additionally, low hemoglobin and platelet counts were noted, with mean platelet volume indicating larger platelets. Activated partial thromboplastin time was prolonged, and bleeding time exceeded 10 minutes. Among the atherosclerosis risk factors, HDL-C was 2 mg/dL. Fasting blood sugar level was 121 mg/dL, fasting insulin was 5.9 µU/mL, and hemoglobin A1c (NGSP) was 4.9%. A 75 g oral glucose tolerance test was not performed. Evaluation of the patient's systemic condition, particularly the peripheral arteries, revealed irregular and eccentric stenosis of the brachiocephalic artery, which was treated with stent implantation (8 × 27 mm). Severe stenosis was also found in the left subclavian artery and treated with stent implantation. Angiography of the abdominal aorta and lower limbs showed severe stenosis in the right common iliac artery, with the right internal iliac artery not contrasted. Severe stenosis was also found in the left internal iliac artery. Stenosis with dissection in the right common iliac artery was treated with stent implantation (10 × 60 mm). Following treatment of the peripheral arteries, CABG was performed (right internal thoracic artery-posterior descending branch, AV node branch, and left internal thoracic artery-left anterior descending branch #8). Five days post-surgery, cardiac tamponade occurred and was managed with platelet transfusion and pericardial drainage. Six months later, the patient experienced effort angina again. Coronary angiography showed complete obstruction of the radial artery graft between the posterior descending branch #4 and AV node branch #4. A drug-eluting stent was implanted in the proximal right coronary artery to relieve the unprotected ischemic area. Thirteen months later, at age 53, the patient was admitted with complaints of impotence, intermittent claudication, and a sensation of coldness in his lower extremities. The ankle–brachial pressure index was 0.79 on the right and 0.66 on the left. Angiography revealed severe stenosis at the bifurcation of the common iliac artery and dissection of the abdominal aorta. Three-dimensional computed tomography (CT) angiography showed incremental detritus stenosis with calcification at the ostium of the left renal artery and near the bifurcation of the common iliac artery. The patient underwent aorta–external iliac artery bypass surgery and replacement of the abdominal aorta with a blood vessel prosthesis. Histology of tissue from the abdominal aorta indicated an aggregation of foam cells. The following day, difficulty in hemostasis was observed post-surgery. As in the previous bypass surgery, platelet transfusion was administered to achieve hemostasis. However, internal bleeding could not be controlled, leading to compartment syndrome and subsequent death from rhabdomyolysis of the lower extremities.

input.case_report: A 47-year-old female with a history of autoimmune thyroiditis and essential hypertension, managed with levothyroxine and losartan, was admitted with a 20-day history of fever, muscle pain, migratory joint pain, fatigue, loss of appetite, sore throat, and an itchy rash. Physical examination revealed a generalized rash, more prominent on the chest and limbs, including the palms and soles, along with a high fever of 39ºC. No lymph nodes were palpable. Blood tests showed an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 49 mm/h, C-reactive protein (CRP) 5.10 mg/dL, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) 346 UI/L, and ferritin 877 ng/mL. Antinuclear antibody (ANA) titer was 1/160 with a nucleolar staining pattern, but tests for anti-Smith, anti-SSA, anti-SSB, anti-dsDNA, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies, anti-citrullinated protein antibody, and rheumatoid factor were negative, and complement levels were normal. Infectious agents were ruled out through negative blood and urine cultures, and tests for SARS-CoV-2, tuberculosis, syphilis, HIV, viral hepatitis, herpes simplex 1 and 2, cytomegalovirus, Epstein-Barr virus, Parvovirus-B19, and common zoonoses. A full-body contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) scan showed mediastinal lymphadenopathies. A positron emission tomography (PET)/CT scan revealed extensive metabolically active lymph nodes both above and below the diaphragm, including two to three lymph nodes in the right posterior-lateral cervical region. A biopsy of a mediastinal lymph node obtained via endobronchial ultrasound excluded lymphoproliferative, granulomatous, or infectious processes. Skin biopsies indicated non-specific changes suggestive of drug toxicity or viral infection. Bone marrow biopsy and aspiration were normal. The patient was treated empirically with prednisolone starting at 1 mg/kg/day, tapering down over several weeks, resulting in complete resolution of symptoms and normalization of laboratory findings within a month. Five months later, the patient experienced fever, facial papular lesions, lymphopenia, and increased CRP, and was diagnosed with SARS-CoV-2 infection, responding well to steroid therapy. Two months later, a pruritic maculopapular/urticarial rash reappeared on the face, chest, and lower limbs, accompanied by fever up to 40ºC, wrist and ankle joint pain, fatigue, sore throat, and left neck pain, leading to readmission. Laboratory tests showed new-onset lymphopenia (540/microL), ESR 23 mm/h, CRP 7.14 mg/dL, and ferritin 933 ng/mL. ANA titer was again 1/160 with a nucleolar staining pattern, with negative results for anti-SM, anti-SSA, anti-SSB, anti-dsDNA, and antiphospholipid antibodies. Blood and urine cultures were negative. A PET/CT scan showed increased metabolic activity in cervical lymph nodes bilaterally, multiple supradiaphragmatic lymph node groups, infradiaphragmatic lymph nodes, spleen, and bone marrow, with discreet splenomegaly on morphological evaluation. During hospitalization, the patient had persistent high fever (39.8ºC), general malaise, new-onset liver dysfunction (aspartate aminotransferase 1477 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 829 U/L, LDH 4134 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 434 U/L, gamma-glutamyl transferase 968 U/L, albumin 3.1 g/dL, normal bilirubin, and no coagulopathy), hemoglobin 10.1 g/dL, mild lymphopenia, normal platelet count, marked increase in CRP to 18.61 mg/dL, ESR 45 mm/h, ferritin > 16500 ng/mL, fibrinogen 394 mg/dL, and triglycerides 432 mg/dL. Abdominal ultrasound excluded hepatomegaly and splenomegaly. A bone marrow biopsy showed no signs of hemophagocytosis or lymphoproliferative disease. A liver biopsy revealed mild inflammatory changes in resolution.

input.case_report: A 34-year-old Han female patient presented with ataxia. She had a slight limp that began 7 or 8 years ago without any clear cause. About a year before presentation, she gave birth and then developed progressively worsening walking instability. Five months prior to evaluation, she began experiencing static tremors in her hands and head, difficulty speaking, occasional limb numbness, and coughing after drinking water. The tremors worsened with movement. She also reported headaches, dizziness, and nausea. One year prior, she experienced a loss of appetite and lost over 10 pounds. Her medical history includes an allergy to cephalosporins and an appendectomy 20 years ago. She had a 10-year history of tobacco and alcohol use and quit smoking 6 months ago. Her parents are alive, and she is an only child with no relatives reporting similar symptoms. She has 27 teeth, smaller tooth size, normal sex hormone levels, and regular menstruation. On examination, she had dysarthria, upbeating nystagmus, and right gaze-evoked nystagmus. Limb reflexes were absent, and her bilateral finger-nose test was unstable. She showed instability on the calcaneus tibial test, had a positive Romberg sign, and bilateral Babinski and Chaddock's signs. Her total score on the scale for the assessment and rating of ataxia was 24, with individual scores of gait (5), stance (4), sitting (2), speech (2), finger chase (2), nose-finger test (2), fast alternating hand movement (3), and heel-shin slide (4). Her Mini-Mental State Examination score was normal, but she could not complete the Montreal Cognitive Scale due to hand and head tremors. Laboratory results for tumor markers, ceruloplasmin, immune-related autoantibodies, thyroid function tests, rheumatism markers, immunoglobulins, complement levels, antinuclear antibody, anticardiolipin antibody, and anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody screening were not significantly abnormal. Lumbar puncture results were also normal.

input.case_report: A 56-year-old male presented to the otorhinolaryngology department with multiple palpable masses in the right cervical and submandibular regions, which had gradually increased in size over the past 8 years. The patient had no significant past medical history or relevant family history. Physical examination revealed no palpable lymph nodes or masses in other body areas. Initial laboratory tests showed mild anemia (Hb 11.6 g/dL) with normal leukocyte and platelet counts. Blood urea nitrogen (BUN), creatinine, calcium, inorganic phosphate, aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), and serum electrolyte levels were within normal ranges. Serum albumin was 3.3 g/dL, and total protein was 8.9 g/dL. Routine urine analysis showed no abnormalities. Computed tomography (CT) scans of the neck and paranasal sinuses were performed for surgical evaluation, revealing numerous enlarged lymph nodes conglomerated in the right submental and submandibular regions (levels II, III, IV, V), with the largest measuring approximately 6.7×4.7 cm. The right internal jugular vein appeared to be severely compressed by the enlarged lymph nodes. No abnormalities were found in the tonsil, salivary gland, paranasal sinus, or nasal cavity. The patient underwent surgery to remove the masses, although some small remnants remained. Gross examination of the excised lymph nodes showed multiple grayish-white masses ranging from 2.5×1.8×1.5 cm to 6.5×5×4.5 cm. Sectioning of the lymph nodes revealed grayish-white cut surfaces with focal hemorrhaging.

input.case_report: A 41-year-old white woman was referred to our unit in February 2008 with proteinuria and microscopic haematuria, initially identified in 2006. In 2001, she experienced swelling and stiffness in her hand joints and bilateral conjunctival hyperaemia. By 2003, she reported joint pain affecting both upper and lower extremities and her left elbow. Ocular sicca syndrome was confirmed through the Schirmer test, and antinuclear antibodies (ANA) with a speckled pattern at a 1/320 titre were detected. Due to persistent joint pain, ocular symptoms, and ANA positivity, a diagnosis of undifferentiated connective tissue disease (UCTD) was made. The patient declined a salivary gland biopsy. In February 2008, due to ongoing proteinuria, she was hospitalized. Examination revealed right eye sicca syndrome, widespread joint pain, and no hypertension. Key laboratory findings included ANA at a 1/320 titre with speckled pattern positivity, proteinuria (280–300 mg/dl), slight microscopic haematuria, moderate anaemia, and creatinine clearance of 108 ml/min. The proteinuria was glomerular and non-selective, with 50% albumin. Notably, ANCA, ENA, anti-DNA antibodies, and cryoglobulins were negative. Serum and urinary immunoelectrophoresis showed no monoclonal proteins. Serology for HCV Ab and HBsAg was negative. A bone marrow biopsy revealed no plasmocytosis, and immunophenotyping showed no evidence of abnormal B cell clones.

input.case_report: A 13-month-old girl was admitted to the hospital due to developmental regression. She was born at full term with a birth weight of 3700 grams and had normal development until 12 months of age, including walking with one hand held, grasping objects with two fingers, and speaking a few meaningful words. Her family history was unremarkable. One month before hospital admission, she had a three-day fever following inactivated Japanese Encephalitis and Hepatitis A vaccinations. About two weeks post-vaccination, she lost the ability to walk and could only stand with assistance. Her oral intake decreased, and she became more irritable. Upon presentation, she was unable to sit without support, could not produce meaningful speech, and required a nasogastric tube for feeding. Nystagmus was observed, and she had poor eye contact. Neurological examination revealed spastic extremities and bilateral ankle clonus. Laboratory tests showed normal complete blood cell count, liver function tests, renal function tests, and C-reactive protein levels. Serum lactate was 2.3 mmol/L. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis showed lympho-dominant pleocytosis with no significant increase in protein levels (red blood cell count: 0/µL, white blood cell count: 28/µL, lymphocyte count: 24/µL, protein level: 19 mg/dL, glucose level: 55 mg/dL). Microbiological studies of the CSF were negative for infection. Brain MRI revealed leukodystrophy in the bilateral periventricular white matter (PVWM) and corpus callosum, with the subcortical white matter unaffected. Diffusion restriction was noted in the PVWM without contrast enhancement. Arylsulfatase A and beta-galactosidase activities were within normal limits.

input.case_report: A 75-year-old female patient (height 141 cm, weight 44 kg) presented to the emergency room with low back pain and radiating pain, leading to her admission for further evaluation. She had been diagnosed with spinal stenosis and was scheduled for L4-5-S1 PLIF surgery. Ten days before this admission, she experienced abdominal pain, nausea, and poor oral intake. Abdominal CT and upper gastrointestinal endoscopy were normal, and she was discharged after conservative management improved her condition. She had no underlying diseases or family history of neuromuscular disease. Preoperative electrocardiogram showed nonspecific ST and T wave changes, while echocardiogram, chest radiograph, and routine laboratory findings were normal. Upon arrival in the operating room, her blood pressure was 130/80 mmHg, heart rate 115 beats/min, and pulse oxygen 92%. She received intramuscular glycopyrrolate 0.2 mg before surgery. General anesthesia was induced with pentothal sodium 175 mg, rocuronium 35 mg, and remifentanil 0.05 µg/kg/min, followed by endotracheal intubation using a light-wand. Arterial cannulation was performed with a 20 G catheter in the right radial artery, and central venous cannulation in the right internal jugular vein. Anesthesia was maintained with desflurane, a mixture of oxygen and air (FiO2 0.5), and intravenous remifentanil. Desflurane concentration was adjusted to keep the bispectral index between 40-60. Warm forced air devices were used to maintain body temperature. Vital signs remained stable throughout the operation. Phenylephrine 50 µg/min was infused to maintain systolic blood pressure between 90 and 110 mmHg. Blood loss was approximately 1,000 ml, and the patient received 2,150 ml of crystalloid solution, 500 ml of colloid solution, and three pints of packed red blood cells. The surgery was completed without complications, and anesthesia lasted 310 minutes. Core body temperature was around 36℃ during the operation. Neuromuscular blockade was reversed with glycopyrrolate 0.4 mg and neostigmine 1.5 mg at the end of the procedure. Postoperatively, the patient had significantly delayed emergence from anesthesia, was drowsy, and required assisted ventilation due to weak spontaneous respiratory efforts for 40 minutes. Arterial blood gas analysis showed pH 7.23, PaCO2 41 mmHg, PaO2 284 mmHg, HCO3 17.2 mEq/L, and SaO2 100%. Laboratory results included sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.9 mmol/L, calcium 1.18 mmol/L, Hb 9.9 g/dl, and glucose 112 mg/dl. No specific laboratory values explained the delayed emergence. The patient was transferred to the intensive care unit intubated. She was extubated after 30 minutes but remained minimally arousable. Motor power decreased from grade IV (mild weakness) to grade I (visible contraction but no movement) in bilateral upper and lower extremities, with no sensory loss. Cervical spine MRI, brain MRI, and EEG were performed on postoperative day (POD) 1. Cervical spine MRI was normal. Brain MRI showed lesions in the bilateral post central gyrus, medial thalami, periventricular region, periaqueductal region, and hypothalamus. She was started on vitamin B1, but motor functions and mental status did not improve. EEG indicated diffuse cerebral dysfunction. On POD 10, nerve conduction study and electromyography showed axonal dominant sensorimotor polyneuropathy. The patient was treated with intravenous immunoglobulin. She was intubated due to continuous respiratory muscle weakness, and a pulmonary infection led to respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation. On POD 12, hyponatremia (118 mmol/L) and clinical epileptic form discharge on EEG were detected. Symptomatic treatment included phenytoin and hypertonic saline.

input.case_report: A 31-month-old black girl presented with recurrent episodes of breathing difficulties, bluish skin discoloration, easy fatigability, failure to thrive, and intermittent swelling of the lower limbs and face. Her past medical history included frequent coughs and fevers. She was delivered at term weighing 3.5 kg, the first-born and only child of her parents, with no perinatal complications. The Apgar score was 9/10, and she breastfed immediately. Shortly after birth, her mother observed that the patient was often inactive and weak despite adequate breastfeeding. Bluish skin discoloration and easy fatigability appeared a few weeks post-birth. Developmental milestones were delayed; she sat unsupported at 12 months and began crawling at 20 months. Currently, she can walk short distances due to easy fatigability. She experienced multiple episodes of upper respiratory infections treated on an outpatient basis. There was no family history of congenital heart disease or asthma, and her mother had no history of diabetes during pregnancy. Physical examination showed a small-for-age, alert, and afebrile girl with swollen lower limbs, a puffy face, cyanosis, and finger clubbing. Her pulse rate was 168 beats/min, blood pressure was 102/64 mmHg, and respiratory rate was 28 breaths/min with oxygen saturation at 50%. Fine bibasal crepitations were noted on respiratory examination. Cardiac examination revealed S1 and S2 sounds with a grade 3 systolic murmur at the right upper sternal border, without a thrill. Hematological tests showed normal hemoglobin, leukocytes, and platelets. Tests for sickling, syphilis, and HIV were negative.
