input.case_report: A 49-year-old male presented with left-sided weakness and confusion for two days. His past medical history included diabetes mellitus treated with metformin and hypertension treated with valsartan/hydrochlorothiazide and clonidine. He also had a right knee replacement one week prior to his presentation. Physical examination showed a well-appearing man, who was oriented to only place and person and was noted to have confabulations and illogical thoughts, in addition to visual and auditory hallucinations. On admission, the patient was afebrile with a blood pressure of 167/100 mm Hg, a heart rate of 134, a respiratory rate of 17 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation of 100% on ambient air. His initial electrocardiogram (EKG) showed sinus tachycardia. The patient was admitted for further evaluation of his encephalopathy. His initial laboratory tests were significant for a potassium of 2.7 (normal: 3.5 - 5.3 mmol/L), serum bicarbonate of 35 (normal: 22 - 31 mmol/L), blood glucose of 183 (normal: 70 - 99 mg/dl), thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) of 1.28 (normal: 0.34 - 4.82 microunits/mL), and hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) of 8.1%. Further workup was done to rule out other causes of the encephalopathy which were all negative, including urine toxicology, chest x-ray, computed tomography (CT) scan, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the head, and an electroencephalogram. However, given his sinus tachycardia and confusion, a pulmonary embolism was considered as a differential, which was confirmed on a CT scan of the chest and appropriate treatment was started. Despite the treatment of underlying causes, the patient remained agitated, confused, and paranoid. The patient was seen by a psychiatrist for his symptoms, who suggested an initial therapy with haloperidol, 1 mg twice daily, and lorazepam as required for agitation, along with constant observation for patient safety. Due to the lack of desired clinical improvement over the next few days, other antipsychotics, including risperidone and citalopram, were added. However, those measures failed to control his episodes of severe psychosis. The patient’s hospital stay was further complicated by uncontrolled hypertension despite being on four antihypertensive medications of different classes at their maximum doses (valsartan, hydralazine, labetalol, and amlodipine). Acute psychosis in the setting of uncontrolled hypertension, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, and venous thromboembolism raised the suspicion for metabolic disorders. An endocrinologist was consulted and further workup was initiated. Serum aldosterone was 3.1 (normal: ≤ 23.2 ng/dl) and plasma renin activity was < 2.1 (normal: ≤ 33.2 pg/ml), which ruled out hyperaldosteronism. Plasma metanephrines (< 10; normal: 0 - 62 pg/ml) and normetanephrines (< 10; normal: 0 - 145 pg/ml) were within normal limits. The 24-hour metanephrines (129; normal: < 400 mcg/24 hr), normetanephrines (381; normal: < 900 mcg/24 hr), vanillylmandelic acid (3.9; normal: < 8 mg/24 hr), dopamine (189; normal: 0 - 510 mcg/24 hr), norepinephrine (124; normal: 0 - 135 mcg/24 hr), and epinephrine (9; normal: 0 - 20 mcg/24 hr) were also within normal limits.

input.case_report: A 69-year-old female with a history of right-sided breast cancer in 1991, treated with lumpectomy and axillary lymph node dissection, followed by chemotherapy, whole breast radiation therapy, and two years of tamoxifen (discontinued due to uterine polyps), presented in January 2021 with extensive skin changes on her right breast. She reported an intermittent rash on her right breast for two and a half years, with occasional stinging, pruritus, scaliness, and intermittent bleeding, but no palpable concerns bilaterally. A recent skin punch biopsy of the right breast showed specific findings. A diagnostic mammogram from an outside institution showed no significant changes over multiple years, and no suspicious radiologic findings were present in the right breast. Static ultrasound imaging of the right subareolar breast demonstrated no suspicious mass. A breast MRI from an outside institution, despite being of suboptimal quality, showed no gross suspicious findings with minimal background parenchymal enhancement. Her past medical history includes hypertension, osteoporosis, and hyperparathyroidism status post parathyroidectomy. Family history includes liver cancer in her paternal uncle and grandmother, with no reported family history of breast or ovarian cancer. The patient is of Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry and chose not to undergo genetic testing. On presentation to the surgical oncologist, the review of systems was positive for tenderness in the right breast and negative for hematologic or lymphatic complaints. Clinical breast examination revealed the right breast was slightly smaller than the left, with extensive skin excoriation and erythema spanning 9 x 5.5 cm, obscuring the nipple-areolar complex. There were no dominant masses in either breast, no nipple discharge, and no palpable axillary lymphadenopathy bilaterally.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 22-year-old female college student began experiencing tender fingertips, one or two at a time, in August. She had an episode in early October of sudden onset numbness followed by pain in her proximal right forearm, associated with tenderness on deep palpation. Throughout October, she had episodes of soreness in rotating sites including the back of her head, her abdomen, her right thigh, and right calf. Pain in her dorsal left foot awoke her in the middle of the night in late October. She woke up with a painful swollen right wrist, making it difficult to write, in early November. The patient presented on November sixth to a nurse practitioner in a public health clinic with a chief complaint of intermittent muscle pain. She denied fever, chills, fatigue, bleeding, or skin rash. Her temperature was 36.9 °C (98.5 °F), pulse 104 beats/minute, blood pressure 112/76 mmHg, and respirations 18 breaths/minute. She had normal heart sounds with no murmur heard, and normal peripheral pulses, musculoskeletal, and neurological examination. Her white blood cell count was 7,800/cu mm (79% neutrophils) and hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL; platelets, blood urea nitrogen, creatinine, sodium, potassium, chloride, bicarbonate, calcium, glucose, bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), albumin, and total protein were all normal. She was diagnosed with “sensory disturbance (primary)” and given no treatment. Over the next two months, the patient continued to have episodes of pain and tenderness for which she took over-the-counter ibuprofen and acetaminophen. She had an episode of “skin blotching” on the pad of a finger that she photographed⁠—one of the fingertips where she had been having transient tenderness before taking over-the-counter analgesic medications. She denied fever, chills, or fatigue. She was seen again on January third and was noted to be tearful and “worried about everything”. Her temperature was 36.5 °C (97.7 °F), pulse 112 beats/minute, blood pressure 128/80 mmHg, and respirations 18 breaths/minute. She had no murmur heard, and unchanged physical examination. She was diagnosed with a generalized anxiety disorder for which duloxetine was prescribed, and myalgia for which over-the-counter ibuprofen and acetaminophen were recommended. The patient continued to have episodes of pain and tenderness for which she took ibuprofen and acetaminophen, but she discontinued taking duloxetine. She was seen in a hospital emergency department on January 18 with a chief complaint of “hurting all over with pain in fingers, toes, inflammation, swelling” and fingers going numb for six months. Her temperature was 36.6 °C (97.8 °F), pulse 111 beats/minute, and blood pressure 119/73 mmHg. Her white blood cell count was 9,580/cu mm (76% neutrophils), hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL and C-reactive protein 9.36 mg/dL (reference range, 0.02-0.50). She was discharged from the emergency department with a diagnosis of “multiple musculoskeletal pains-swelling” for which methocarbamol was prescribed. The patient had an episode of paresthesia (tingling) on her upper back, an episode of left facial numbness, and an episode of vertigo. She had an episode of skin blotching on her soles, which were tender, on January 30, that she photographed. The patient was seen in the emergency department on February 13 with sudden onset left flank pain, temperature 36.5 °C (97.7 °F), pulse 120 beats/minute, blood pressure 114/97 mm Hg, and respirations 18 breaths/minute. She had normal heart sounds with no murmur heard, but tenderness over the left kidney. Her white blood cell count was 15,250/cu mm (85% neutrophils), hemoglobin 9.9 g/dL and albumin 3.2 g/dL; platelets, blood urea nitrogen, creatinine, sodium, potassium, chloride, bicarbonate, calcium, glucose, bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, ALT, AST, lipase, and total protein were all normal. Urinalysis showed 15 white blood cells/high power field, 27 red blood cells/high power field, and occasional bacteria. Abdominal computed tomography showed no abnormalities. Pyelonephritis was diagnosed and treated with morphine, acetaminophen-oxycodone, and ceftriaxone. Urine culture was ordered, but not obtained before the patient was discharged with prescriptions for tramadol, ibuprofen, and ciprofloxacin. The patient presented on February 20 to the nurse practitioner in the public health clinic with episodes of moderate dizziness, a sensation of the room spinning with moving her head, lasting for minutes, associated with nausea, intermittently over the previous two weeks. The worst episode occurred that day and prompted her visit to the clinic. The patient denied headache, fever, chills, ear pain, teeth pain, sore throat, or anxiety. Her temperature was 36.8 °C (98.2 °F), pulse 86 beats/minute, blood pressure 118/84 mm Hg, and respirations 18 breaths/minute. While listening to the patient’s heart, the nurse asked her if she had ever had a heart murmur. The patient was unaware that she had and replied no. The nurse recorded hearing no murmur. Vertigo was diagnosed and the patient was prescribed meclizine. The patient was admitted to a community hospital in another city on March fourth with sudden onset epigastric pain, associated with nausea, vomiting, and weight loss (3.6 kg). She had been lightheaded for several days and had had hematochezia for three days, three weeks prior. Her temperature was 36.6 °C (97.9 °F), pulse 104 beats/minute, blood pressure 116/79 mmHg, respirations 18 breaths/minute, and oxygen saturation 96% on room air. She had epigastric tenderness and stool positive for occult blood. “No murmur heard” was reported by the admitting physician and no murmur was reported by any of the multiple other physicians on the case. The patient’s hemoglobin was 8.7 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume 83 fL, platelets 405,000/cu mm, and white blood cell count 11,600/cu mm (82% neutrophils). She had been taking ibuprofen, 1600 mg/day. Erosive gastritis was diagnosed by endoscopy and her anemia was attributed to acute blood loss from ibuprofen. She was discharged March fifth on pantoprazole, sucralfate, iron, and tramadol. Gastric biopsy was subsequently seen to show no abnormalities.

input.case_report: A 29-year-old woman visited the clinic with a six-day history of decreased visual acuity in her left eye. The symptoms began a month prior with a gradual decline in vision, worsening over the past six days. The patient reported recent consumption of a large amount of fried food and poor blood glucose control. She had type 2 diabetes mellitus for two years, attributed to dietary habits and lack of exercise, and had been receiving treatment for diabetic retinopathy. There were no other diabetic complications noted, and the patient denied any family history of similar conditions. Menstruation was reported as normal. Physical examination findings were unremarkable. Laboratory results were as follows: total cholesterol 13.98 mmol/L (3.6–6.5), triglyceride 20.55 mmol/L (0–1.71), high-density lipoprotein 0.75 mmol/L (0.83–1.96), low-density lipoprotein 6.91 mmol/L (0–3.36), apolipoprotein-A1 0.64 g/L (1.0–1.6), apolipoprotein-B 0.49 g/L (0.6–1.1), total protein 85.8 g/L (65–85), fasting blood glucose 10.89 mmol/L (3.9–6.1), glycosylated hemoglobin 12% (4.5–6.3), hemoglobin 216 g/L (115–150), mean corpuscular hemoglobin 46.2 pg (27–34), mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration 548 g/L (316–354), erythrocyte sedimentation rate 40 mm/h (0–20), and blood uric acid 424 μmol/L (140–340). Other blood routine tests, including C-reactive protein 4.45 mg/L (0–10), liver function, kidney function, electrolytes, and homocysteine, were within normal limits. Imaging studies, including a B ultrasound of the bile, pancreas, and spleen, an electrocardiogram, and a computed tomography scan of the chest, showed normal results. The B ultrasound indicated the presence of fatty liver.

input.case_report: A 24-year-old Black female with no significant past medical history presented to the emergency room with a two-day history of painful vision loss in the left eye. She reported having fevers of unknown temperature, malaise, and a sore throat three days prior, along with a two-day history of a clear productive cough. The day before her ER visit, she experienced a frontal headache and darkened central vision in the left eye, accompanied by retrobulbar pain. This progressed to decreased vision in both eyes, with the left eye being worse. She took over-the-counter Tylenol and Zicam without symptom relief. The following day, her vision did not improve, and she still had pain with extraocular movements, prompting her to visit urgent care, which referred her to the ER. Her only home medication was an oral contraceptive pill, ethinyl estradiol-norethindrone (Tarina Fe 1/20), taken once daily for the past six years. She reported diarrhea (three to four bowel movements a day) starting on the day of admission and lasting about four days, for which Imodium was administered as needed during her hospital stay. She denied redness, tearing, or discharge from the left eye, as well as any sensory deficits, previous vision loss, or a family history of multiple sclerosis. On admission, visual acuity was 20/70 in the left eye (OS) and 20/20 in the right eye (OD). Laboratory tests revealed mild hypokalemia (3.5 mmol/L), leukopenia (3.3 x 103/μL) with a low absolute neutrophil count (1800 per microliter) and elevated monocytes (13.5%), normal D-dimer (<150 ng/dL), and elevated C-reactive protein (2.5 mg/dL, trending down to 0.6 mg/dL). Respiratory multiplex PCR was positive for SARS-CoV-2. The patient had received two doses of the Pfizer mRNA vaccine approximately one year and three months prior. A CT angiogram of the head with contrast showed no acute findings, with patent intracranial vasculature and no evidence of acute intracranial thrombosis or occlusion. An MRI of the brain, face, neck, and orbits with and without contrast showed no acute findings or T2/FLAIR signal abnormality of the optic nerves, optic chiasm, or optic tracts. The patient was started on 1 gram of methylprednisolone once daily for three days. On the second day of IV steroid treatment, she noted improvement in left eye pain but no significant improvement in vision. She also received a two-day course of intravenous remdesivir (200 mg on day one and 100 mg on day two). Ophthalmology consultation revealed a slight color vision deficit on the Ishihara color test (11/11 OD, 9/11 OS). Intraocular pressure was within normal limits (14 OD, 18 OS). Fundoscopic examination showed no optic disc edema or relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD). The macula was flat and sharp with a good reflex in the right eye (OD), but the left macula had a petaloid appearance with a mild reflex suggesting a macular process. Extraocular muscles were intact in both eyes, and the pain with lateral gaze deviation of the left eye improved. There was no diplopia with any directional gaze of either eye, and pupils were equally round and reactive to light and accommodation. Visual acuity remained stable during the four-day hospital course, with 20/20 vision in the right eye (OD) and 20/50 in the left eye (OS).

input.case_report: A 15-year-old phenotypically female adolescent was evaluated for delayed puberty and proteinuria. There was no family history of kidney disease or delayed puberty. The patient exhibited slow growth starting 3 years prior, with less than 2 cm of growth per year, delayed breast development, and absence of pubic or axillary hair. Intellectual development was normal, and the patient experienced no other discomfort. At age 14, the patient sought medical attention for lack of menstruation and growth retardation. Laboratory tests showed a growth hormone (GH) level of 0.31 ng/mL (Normal range (NR) 0.02–5.42), insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) at 56.8 ng/mL (NR 220.00–972.00), urine albumin (+++), estimated glomerular filtration rate of 56.77 mL/min/1.73 m2, and creatinine at 184 umol/L. Bone age was determined to be approximately 10.3 years (less than 3rd percentile). A growth hormone stimulation test with arginine and insulin showed peak GH levels of 7.77 ng/mL and 7.01 ng/mL, respectively. The patient declined growth hormone therapy. Later that year, the patient was admitted to the nephrology department with systemic edema, lower back pain, and proteinuria. Laboratory studies indicated nephrotic syndrome with hypoalbuminemia (23.1 g/L), hyperlipidemia (total cholesterol, 9.74 mmol/L), and massive proteinuria (urine albumin (+++)) without hematuria. A renal biopsy showed focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS). After 1 year of steroid therapy without resolution of heavy proteinuria, the patient was transferred for further assessment. Physical examination revealed a height of 136 cm (age-specific height < -3SD), BMI of 17.84 kg/m2, and a waist circumference of 64 cm. Notable facial features included prominent ears and a saddle nose. Both breasts were undeveloped with minimal areola pigmentation, and there was no armpit or pubic hair. The vulva appeared female with a visible vaginal opening, no clitoris enlargement, and no posterior lip fusion (Tanner stage 1). The estimated glomerular filtration rate was 22.64 mL/min/1.73 m2, creatinine was 262 umol/L, blood albumin was 29.0 g/L, urine albumin was (++++) and urine protein quantification was 5.40 g/24 h. Tests for anti–glomerular basement membrane, antinuclear antibody, anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody, complement C3, C4, antistreptolysin-O, and immunoglobulins were negative. The parathyroid hormone (PTH) level was 81.98 pmol/L (NR 1.6–6.9) and 25-hydroxy-vitamin D was 15.8 nmol/L (NR 47.7–144).

input.case_report: A 45-year-old Hispanic woman arrived at a local hospital with a 2-week history of worsening diffuse back pain, fatigue, generalized weakness, and daily fevers and sweats. She did not experience any parasthesias or dyasthesias, but the pain was severe enough to limit some daily activities. She reported no specific factors that aggravated or relieved the pain. She had a significant history of von Willebrand's disease diagnosed at age 35 and had otherwise been in good health. Physical examination revealed a palpable, non-mobile, 2×2 centimeter mass at the level of the left 10th rib with no overlying skin changes. There was no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Neurological examination was grossly normal. The complete blood count showed leukocytosis of 33.8×10^9/L, 2% blasts with a left-shifted differential, no thrombocytosis (416×10^9/L), and anemia (9.0 g/dL). The chemistry panel indicated hypercalcemia (13.8 mg/dL), elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) level (4,823 IU/L), and elevated alkaline phosphatase (257 IU/L). A bone scan showed increased uptake in the left hip, suggestive of a degenerative left acetabulum. Plain radiographs and MRI imaging of the left hip revealed lytic lesions in the left femoral neck. Both a marrow biopsy and a tissue sample of the rib mass were non-diagnostic. The patient was treated with pamidronate and intravenous fluids for hypercalcemia. Broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics were administered due to a low-grade fever of 99.6°F. The patient was transferred to our institution. Upon arrival, the white blood cell count (WBC) was 33.7×10^9/L with 80% neutrophils, a left shift including 2% metamyelocytes and 2% blasts. No tear drop cells or schistocytes were identified. Hemoglobin was 9 g/dL and platelet count was 416×10^9/L. LDH levels were elevated at 4,823 IU/L (upper limit of normal [ULN] 618 IU/L), corrected calcium was 11.94 mg/dL, and alkaline phosphatase levels were 257 IU/L (ULN 126). Renal function was normal. A bone survey shortly after transfer showed normal bone mineral content, multiple discrete lytic lesions in the skull, ribs, pelvis, and shoulder girdle, and a lytic lesion in the adjacent left supra-acetabular ilium and femoral neck. A PET/CT scan showed diffuse activity of the marrow spaces throughout the axial skeleton and extending into the appendicular skeleton as well as throughout the spleen. Large lytic lesions were noted in several regions including the left femoral neck and left acetabular roof, corresponding to the lesions seen on the bone survey. Serum protein electrophoresis and immunofixation did not show evidence of a monoclonal protein. Kappa and lambda light chain ratio was normal (1 : 1.62). Urine was not tested. During hospitalization, her temperature remained elevated between 99.3°F and 102.4°F despite broad-spectrum antibiotics. Blood and urine cultures were consistently negative, and no source of infection was identified. Within 2 weeks of admission, blood counts deteriorated, and the patient became increasingly anemic, thrombocytopenic, and leukopenic.

She ceased this practice after moving to Taiwan due to public health education. The patient presented with fever, fatigue, and malaise for 1 day. Laboratory findings showed pyuria. She was treated for a urinary tract infection with intravenous levofloxacin, but a low-grade fever persisted. She did not exhibit coffee ground nasogastric tube drainage, vomiting, abdominal pain, or hemodynamic instability. Despite switching to heparin-free hemodialysis, hematochezia continued.

input.case_report: A 35-year-old male reported experiencing pain, swelling, and locking in his left knee joint for seven months. The McMurray test was positive, while signs of joint instability were absent. No other joints were affected, and both personal and family medical histories were unremarkable. input.case_report: On 13 August 2020, a 57-year-old female was admitted to the hospital due to right-eye vision loss for 1.5 months. Previously, the patient had received treatment in a regional hospital on 21 June. She was treated for a vitreous hemorrhage with oral medications. Two days later, the patient developed a fever, red eyes, and eyelid edema in the right eye. Positive symptoms of the right eye included mixed conjunctival hyperemia, retrocorneal pigment KP, aqueous turbidity, and exudation in the pupil area, as recorded by the regional hospital. The structure of the posterior segment of the eye was unclear. The local ophthalmologist applied mydriatic drugs, anti-inflammatory dexamethasone eye drops, and intravenous infusion of clindamycin and dexamethasone sodium phosphate 10 mg. After these treatments, the patient felt the symptoms lessened, and the systemic dexamethasone sodium phosphate was gradually reduced to 5 mg. However, the eye symptoms then worsened. The patient was transferred to a tertiary hospital afterward. Her initial visual acuity was Hand Motion OD and 20/30 OS. Slit-lamp examination revealed slight hyperemia of the conjunctiva, transparent cornea, deep anterior chamber, turbid aqueous humor, less round pupil, posterior iris adhesion, opaque lens, and obvious vitreous opacity with an invisible fundus of the right eye. There was no ocular pathology of the left eye. Systemic steroids were suspended after admission, and a series of examinations were performed to identify the pathogen. General checkups included brain MRI, abdominal ultrasound, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), rheumatoid factor, antinuclear antibody spectrum, and pathogen detection for tuberculosis bacilli, HIV, syphilis, and hepatitis. A B-ultrasound was performed for the right eye, and an anterior chamber puncture was done to extract aqueous humor for routine pathogen detection. The blood test showed elevated ESR and C-reactive protein, and decreased eosinophils. The brain MRI showed multiple cerebral infractions, white matter demyelination changes, and brain atrophy. The abdominal ultrasound showed dense light spots in the liver and an enlarged spleen. The ocular B-scan ultrasonography showed vitreous opacity, vitreous membranes, and retinal detachment in the right eye. Routine pathogenic detection did not show a positive result.

The girl had an open posterior instrumented correction from T2 to L4. Her postoperative course was uneventful, and she was discharged home five days later. Her wounds healed well, and she gradually returned to normal activities. Three years later, at 16 years old, she developed a painful swelling on her upper right back. This swelling increased in size over the following weeks, prompting a review. Clinical examination revealed a tense, tender, right paraspinal mass, palpable from C7 to T2. She was admitted for urgent investigation.

input.case_report: A 39-year-old female presented with generalized abdominal distension, weight loss, swelling of legs, and muscle weakness for several weeks. She later transitioned to intermittent hemodialysis due to personal preference. A few weeks after this transition, she developed ascites requiring repeated large-volume paracentesis with 5-7 liters of ascitic fluid removed every other week. She had no symptoms of coughing, palpitations, or shortness of breath and was chronically anuric. Vital signs included blood pressure of 160/80 mmHg, heart rate of 74 beats/min (regular), respiratory rate of 16/min, and temperature of 36.9 °C. Oxygen saturation was 98% on room air. Physical examination revealed moderate ascites and lower extremity edema. Neurological, respiratory, and cardiovascular examinations were normal without organomegaly or signs of chronic liver disease or heart failure. Other labs included CD4 T-cell count of 600/mm3, HIV viral load of 986 copies/mL, serum creatinine of 10.3 mg/dL, aspartate aminotransferase of 13 U/L, alanine aminotransferase of 17 U/L, bilirubin of 0.4 mg/dL, prothrombin time INR of 1.01, serum albumin of 2.3 mg/dL, serum amylase of 105 IU/L, and serum lipase of 40 IU/L. Tumor antigens CA 15-3 and CA19-9 were within reference range, but CA 125 levels were elevated (197-416 U/mL). Ascites fluid analysis showed an albumin level of 1.5 g/dL and protein of 3.5 g/dL, with a serum-to-ascites albumin gradient of < 1.1. Peritoneal fluid culture was negative for acid-fast bacilli, and no fungi were seen by calcofluor white stain. Histoplasma and Aspergillus antigens were negative. Computed tomography reported a normal pancreas. β-2 microglobulin was elevated to 47.30 mg/L. Serum protein electrophoresis showed hypoalbuminemia and decreased total protein, with minimal monoclonal M-component (IgG-κ 0.30 g/dL in the γ zone). Immunofixation electrophoresis showed polyclonal immunoglobulins with polyclonal light chains. κ and λ light chains were increased to 66.86 mg/dL and 18.55 mg/dL, respectively, with a ratio of 3.6. Ascitic fluid analysis showed a marked increase in plasma cells with an abnormal κ:λ ratio of > 5:1. Liver biopsy was essentially normal.

input.case_report: A 43-year-old woman arrived at the hospital with sensorimotor paraparesis, urinary retention, and headache lasting 24 hours. Nine days prior, she had received her first dose of the ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 (Vaxzevria; AstraZeneca) vaccine. Her medical history included only migraine. Upon admission, she exhibited mild paraparesis, hyperreflexia, bilateral positive Babinski sign, a thoracic sensory level at T10, meningism, and a fever of 38°C, with an Expanded Disability Status Scale (EDSS) score of 5.0. Spinal MRI showed T2 hyperintense lesions from C6 to T1 and at T3 and T4, indicative of transverse myelitis. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis showed granulocytic pleocytosis of 545 cells/μl, elevated lactate (4.4 mmol/l), elevated CSF protein (135 mg/dl), and a reduced CSF to serum glucose ratio. Oligoclonal bands were negative, and no immunoglobulin abnormalities were found. Broad empiric treatment included 1000 mg of intravenous (IV) methylprednisolone (IVMP) daily, ceftriaxone, and ampicillin, with plasma exchange (PLEX) planned. Over the next 5 days, this regimen, including 5 g of IVMP and one PLEX session, led to slight clinical improvement. However, on the fifth day, the patient developed a stuporous to comatose state with fever over 40°C and sensorimotor tetraparesis (EDSS 9.0), necessitating ICU monitoring. A second CSF examination showed increased granulocytic pleocytosis (720 cells/μl). Anti-infective treatment was escalated to meropenem, and extensive infectious evaluation, including next-generation sequencing (NGS) for over 1500 pathogens, was conducted. All results were negative except for an increased CSF concentration of Cutibacterium acnes DNA in the initial evaluation (912 of 1824 reads), which was not confirmed in follow-up and considered contamination. Early follow-up brain MRI showed new T2 hyperintense lesions in the frontal cortex, periventricular space, pulvinar thalamic nuclei, brain stem, and cerebellar peduncles. Spinal lesions had progressed, now involving C3 to C5. Consequently, anti-infective therapy was discontinued.

input.case_report: A 68-year-old Japanese female experienced convulsive seizures following sudden sweating early in the morning on April 10, 2016. The seizures lasted about 3 minutes. Approximately one week prior, she had a loss of appetite and a fever of 38°C on April 9. She was prescribed antibiotics for a suspected urinary tract infection (UTI). She was admitted to the Hospital of International University of Health and Welfare with suspected pneumonia, UTI, and epilepsy. Physical examination showed a height of 153 cm, weight of 33.4 kg, body mass index of 14.3 kg/m2, body temperature of 37.9°C, heart rate of 124 bpm, blood pressure of 106/89 mm Hg, SpO2 of approximately 85% in room air, and a Glasgow coma scale score of E3V3M4. The patient occasionally opened her eyes but was unresponsive. The seizures had subsided by the time of hospital admission. Chest examination revealed no rattling sounds in the lungs, abdominal examination showed no redness at the percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy site, and limb examination indicated flexion contracture of the left upper limb. Muscle strength was difficult to assess, but manual muscle testing suggested a grade of 3-4 in the upper limbs. Grip strength was also challenging to evaluate, though she could grip a plastic bottle. Muscle tone was increased on both sides, more so on the left. Her activities of daily living (ADL) included simple conversation, assistance with meal setup and cleanup, self-feeding with a spoon and fork, full assistance with transfers, grooming, and dressing, capable of wiping her face with a towel, using a portable toilet with full assistance, moving with a wheelchair, and full assistance with defecation and urination. She previously worked as a nurse from ages 18 to 25, then became a full-time housewife after marriage. She has two children, a daughter aged 43 and a son aged 41, both unmarried. She began treatment for diabetes at age 41 and started using a hearing aid at age 50. At age 67, she was admitted to another hospital for a cerebral infarction in the left temporal lobe, with no evident limb paralysis but a disturbance of consciousness and subsequent convulsive seizures. She was readmitted in October 2014 for similar symptoms and treated for a cerebral infarction in the right temporal lobe. She was discharged in June 2015, with rapid progression in ADL impairment and cognitive dysfunction since then. Her mother had hearing loss, used a hearing aid, and lived to 96 years. She has seven siblings, with the third-born sister dying of kidney failure at age 19. The eldest sister is 83 years old and independent in ADL.

The following year, he presented with severe dyspnoea and bilateral knee arthritis, rendering him unable to walk. Physical examination revealed euvolemia, swollen bilateral knees, and severe pes cavus deformity of the feet. Laboratory findings included chronic lactataemia of 4–6 mmol/L (normal range: 0.5–2.2 mmol/L), hyperammonaemia of 177 µmol/dL (normal range: 11–33 µmol/dL), and hyperuricaemia of 11.5 mg/dL (normal range: 3.7–7 mg/dL). Autoimmune panel, anti-Trypanosoma cruzi antibodies, and Coxsackie virus antibodies were negative. Imaging showed an enlarged cardiac silhouette with echocardiographic findings of left ventricular dilation, an ejection fraction (EF) of 25%, and diffuse hypokinesis. Electrocardiogram (EKG) indicated left axis deviation, left bundle branch block, and diffuse tall QRS complexes. Cardiac catheterization performed at an outside facility a month prior showed no evidence of coronary disease. The patient resumed arthritis treatment with oral steroids and began heart failure therapy with carvedilol, furosemide, and lisinopril. He declined implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) placement and was lost to follow-up after discharge. Later that year, he presented with shortness of breath and haemoptysis, and was admitted to the medical intensive care unit with elevated lactate levels of 18 mg/dL without acidosis (bicarbonate 27.3 mmol/L; normal range: 22–29 mmol/L). Vitals and physical examination were otherwise unremarkable. Troponin-T peaked at 0.798 ng/mL (normal range: <0.01 ng/mL) but EKG remained unchanged. Repeat echocardiography showed a worsened EF of 18%, reduced tricuspid annular plane systolic excursion, right ventricular dilation, and biventricular thrombi in the apical walls. Intravenous contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis revealed wedge-shaped hypodensities in the lung parenchyma and kidney, indicative of thrombotic infarctions. He was anticoagulated and stabilized. Biventricular ICD placement was considered but deferred due to the presence of intracardiac thrombus. The patient was not eligible for cardiac transplant due to ongoing medication non-compliance and social instability. Over the following year, he was admitted multiple times for polyarthritic flares and dyspnoea. Arthrocentesis identified monosodium urate crystals, and he was treated with intra-articular and systemic steroids and colchicine. He experienced multiple episodes of hypoglycaemia, with concurrent evaluation showing non-insulin-mediated hypoglycaemia characterized by decreased glucose, insulin, and c-peptide levels.

input.case_report: A 52-year-old man was referred to our hospital with a liver mass that had been incidentally detected on a liver CT at an outside hospital. He presented with general weakness of one month duration. He denied a past history of eating raw freshwater fish. The results of his physical examination were unremarkable. The results of his laboratory tests, including the peripheral eosinophil count, serum alkaline phosphatase, serum bilirubin, aspartate aminotransferase, and alanine aminotransferase, were all in the normal ranges. The viral markers for hepatitis were all negative. The levels of tumor markers, including α-fetoprotein, CA 19-9, and carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), were normal. His past medical history was unremarkable except for laparoscopic cholecystectomy that was performed due to a gall stone six years previously. In our hospital, this patient underwent two-phase liver dynamic CT because the CT images taken outside our hospital were not available. The CT showed a 4-cm, ill-margined, lobulated, low-attenuation mass in the right posteroinferior segment of the liver. The mass had heterogeneous, peripheral contrast enhancement during the hepatic arterial phase and then more central enhancement was seen from the peripheral portion of the mass during the portal venous phase. A transient hepatic attenuation difference that surrounded the mass was noted during the hepatic arterial phase. There was a small portion of poorly enhancing, low attenuation in the peripheral area of the mass, which represented a necrotic portion. Neither the intrahepatic nor extrahepatic bile ducts were dilated. There was an approximately 1.5-cm sized lymph node in the aortocaval space. The patient underwent segmentectomy of the liver and resection of the lymphadenopathy in the aortocaval space.

input.case_report: A 65-year-old male with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and anxiety visited his primary care physician for abdominal aortic aneurysm screening. An abdominal ultrasound incidentally found a mixed echogenic area near the stomach. A subsequent abdominal CT identified a 4.5-cm solid mass between the stomach and the pancreas. An upper EUS showed a 40-mm hypoechoic mass on the lesser curvature of the stomach, with no significant findings in the main pancreatic duct and pancreas. Initial evaluations suggested a need for further investigation, and a transduodenal fine-needle aspiration (FNA) indicated an epithelioid mesenchymal tumor of unclassified type, negative for CD117 and DOG1. The GI tumor board recommended surgical resection due to ambiguous biopsy results and the patient's good health status. Negative markers included AE1, CAM5.2, PAX8, CD34, S100, MelanA, HMB45, inhibin, EMA, myogenin, synaptophysin, and chromogranin. No postsurgical adjuvant chemotherapy was indicated. The patient was advised to follow up with annual abdominal CT scans. One year later, surveillance was negative, but a repeat CT after two years revealed a new 3.7 cm x 2.0 cm mass in the posterior gallbladder fundus, an 11-mm perihepatic node, and a 3.7-mm low-density lesion in the right hepatic lobe. The patient developed new-onset nocturnal abdominal pain. An abdominal MRI showed a soft tissue mass along the gallbladder fundus/body and enlarged pathologic portal caval, peripancreatic, and pericholecystic lymph nodes, consistent with metastatic disease. Tumor markers were within normal ranges: CEA 1.6 ng/ml, CA199 < 0.6, and chromogranin A 41 ng/ml. A repeat upper EUS demonstrated one abnormal periportal lymph node, an irregular gallbladder mass, and a clear pancreas and common bile duct. FNA of the lymph node and gallbladder mass showed a small cell malignant neoplasm with a high nucleus-to-cytoplasm ratio and coarse nuclear chromatin. Extensive necrosis prevented a definitive diagnosis. Due to imaging studies suggesting aggressive pathology, the patient agreed to a laparoscopic cholecystectomy and wedge resection of a segment four liver lesion for an excisional biopsy.

Further measures were taken to determine the cause of the condition, and CFH autoantibodies were negative.

Her mental status and serum ammonia levels (115 μmol/L) improved the next day, leading to extubation. However, her mental status and serum ammonia levels fluctuated over the following days. Blood hemoglobin remained stable post-transfusion, and coagulopathy was corrected with vitamin K. Despite standard hyperammonemia treatment, her condition worsened, necessitating reintubation. On the 12th day of admission, she developed status epilepticus, controlled only with propofol infusion. A repeat CT of the head showed diffuse cerebral edema.

input.case_report: A 32-year-old Hispanic woman was being managed for symptoms initially thought to be rheumatoid arthritis (RA). She had tried methotrexate and adalimumab with limited success. She presented for a second opinion due to persistent symptoms. Initially, she experienced two hours of morning stiffness and pain in her right shoulder, which gradually progressed to involve bilateral wrists, fingers, and knees. She also had skin lesions on her left elbow and the extensor aspect of bilateral fingers, previously considered xanthomas. These lesions were biopsied, but results were inconclusive. Laboratory tests showed hypercholesterolemia, and she was started on cholesterol-lowering medication. She also reported secondary amenorrhea but denied any history of inflammatory bowel disease, uveitis, preceding gastrointestinal infection, nail changes, or axial joint involvement. Physical examination revealed synovitis in her right shoulder, right knee, bilateral metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints, and bilateral proximal and distal interphalangeal (PIP and DIP) joints. Her right shoulder appeared deformed with decreased range of motion. The left elbow had a flexion contracture and limited range of motion. She also had limited range of motion in bilateral MCP, PIP, and DIP joints and was unable to make a fist in either hand. A well-circumscribed papulonodular lesion was noted on the left elbow. Nails and axial spine appeared disease-free. Laboratory studies showed a high rheumatoid factor (RF) of 56.4 IU/ml (normal < 7 IU/ml) and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 38 mm/hr. C-reactive protein (CRP) and antinuclear antibody (ANA) were normal. Anti-Sjögren’s syndrome related antigen A (anti-SS-A) antibody was elevated at 8.0 U, and anti-Sjögren’s syndrome related antigen B (anti-SS-B) antibody was elevated at 5.2 U. Other antibodies, including anti-Smith, anti-double-stranded DNA (anti-dsDNA), anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA), anti-topoisomerase (anti-scl-70), ribonucleoprotein (RNP), anti-Jo1, and hepatitis panel were negative. For secondary amenorrhea, she had a low estradiol level of 11 pg/ml, high follicle stimulating hormone (FSH) level of 76.6 mIU/ml, and high luteinizing hormone (LH) level of 62.8 mIU/ml, indicating premature primary ovarian failure. X-rays of her digits showed arthritis mutilans, predominantly in bilateral DIP joints, MCP, and PIP joints. MRI and x-ray of the right shoulder showed widening of the acromioclavicular joint and glenohumeral degenerative changes. Despite a clinical presentation similar to RA, some symptoms were unexplained, particularly DIP joint involvement, skin findings, and lack of response to methotrexate and adalimumab. She was given multiple biological agents, including etanercept, abatacept, and golimumab, over 12 months, but none were effective. She was also diagnosed with osteopenia and started on alendronate without symptom improvement. Due to refractory symptoms, she was referred to a quaternary center and given rituximab, which led to some improvement in synovitis in her right knee and hand joints within the first month. However, symptoms in her right shoulder persisted.

input.case_report: A 37-year-old Caucasian man with no prior medical history arrived at the Emergency Department after experiencing high fevers, diarrhea, and malaise for three days following outdoor activities. He had been hiking and swimming in a local lake with blue-green algae and sewage waste. His condition worsened at home, leading to Emergency Medical Services being called due to altered mentation and lethargy. He had no sick contacts and did not recall any mosquito bites. He had no significant medical or surgical history, took no medications, and had no allergies. There was no notable family history of autoimmune or neurological diseases. He did not use alcohol, smoke, or take recreational drugs. A review of systems revealed no other significant findings. Upon admission, his temperature was 103°F, heart rate was 150 bpm, respiratory rate was 32 bpm, and blood pressure was 149/115 mmHg. BNP was 1,309 pg/ml, and troponin was 0.8 μg/dl. Arterial blood gas showed a pH of 7.5, pCO2 of 29.7 mmHg, and PO2 of 54.3 mmHg, indicating acute hypoxemia with respiratory alkalosis. Physical examination revealed reduced lower extremity strength bilaterally and diminished deep tendon reflexes. He had bilateral crackles and 2+ pitting edema in the lower extremities. He was intubated due to rapidly declining oxygen saturation. Over the next four days, his Glasgow Coma Scale score decreased from 15 to 5 without additional sedation after the first day of intubation. He developed progressive upper extremity weakness and hearing loss. Laboratory results on admission showed a WBC count of 22.6 k/mm3 with 91% neutrophils. Chest X-ray indicated pulmonary edema with bilateral interstitial infiltrates. The complete metabolic panel was largely unremarkable except for hypokalemia at 3.0 mEq/dl. EKG showed persistent sinus tachycardia without ST or T wave changes, and no AV nodal blocks or other tachyarrhythmias were noted. A transthoracic echocardiogram revealed left ventricular global hypokinesis with a reduced ejection fraction of 25-30% without valvular abnormalities. A lumbar puncture showed pleocytosis without bacterial infection, and the meningitis viral panel was negative for several viruses. Lyme, fungal, and Coxsackie viral serology were also negative. Initial WNV IgM was negative. The patient was treated with IVIG and aggressive diuresis with furosemide. Due to lack of improvement and worsening respiratory status on mechanical ventilation, he was transitioned to plasmapheresis on hospital day 3. He was placed on norepinephrine and dobutamine due to concerns of mixed cardiogenic and septic shock. Despite plasmapheresis, his flaccid paralysis and mentation continued to worsen by day 5.

The initial neck swelling was anterior and not linked to joint swelling, morning stiffness, or tenderness in small joints, and it regressed over time. Another static swelling later appeared on the right side of the neck near the first one. The fever was intermittent, reaching up to 104°F, and was associated with rigors and chills, subsiding after taking oral paracetamol. He also experienced severe body aches and joint pain in the wrists, elbows, and knees bilaterally. There were no reports of diurnal variation, night sweats, rash, or oral ulcers. Two small swellings were also observed bilaterally in the inguinal region, each measuring approximately 1.0 × 1.0 cm, and were similarly non-tender, non-erythematous, firm, and immobile with no discharging sinus. Blood pressure was 80/40 mm Hg, pulse 92 beats/min, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, and temperature was afebrile in the Emergency Department. Systemic examination revealed a soft and non-tender abdomen with no visceromegaly; the chest, cardiovascular system, and central nervous system were unremarkable. Meropenem 1 g IV three times a day (TDS) was started due to high total leukocyte count (TLC = 49) and procalcitonin (PCT = 8.17). Echocardiography was performed and found to be unremarkable. The general surgery team was consulted for an excisional biopsy of the right cervical lymph node. The patient was moved to a private room as blood pressure improved, and ionotropic support was tapered off. Azithromycin 500 mg was added to the treatment regimen due to persistent fever. The Infectious Diseases Department advised VDRL and HIV serology tests, which were negative, and azithromycin was switched to ciprofloxacin 500 mg IV twice daily.

input.case_report: A 38-year-old male presented with a non-healing ulcer over the left Achilles tendon region for four months, along with dry skin and hoarseness of voice. input.case_report: A 16-year-old male patient presented with very severe aplastic anemia (VSAA) following a brief history of petechiae and pale skin. Standard diagnostic procedures for acquired bone marrow failure excluded acute leukemia, Fanconi anemia, dyskeratosis congenita (DC), paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH), myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS), and primary immunodeficiency (PID) syndrome. The patient's family history was unremarkable. Allogeneic HSCT from an HLA-identical brother was delayed for several months in hopes of identifying a causal agent and its treatment to reverse bone marrow effects. During this period, supportive care included frequent, mostly weekly transfusions with platelets and packed red cells, and antimicrobial prophylaxis with topical amphotericin B, oral fluconazole, and cotrimoxazole. The patient's neutrophil count remained consistently below 500 µl. Consent for HSCT was obtained after 6 months of neutropenia when the patient developed a severe soft tissue infection in his left hand caused by Streptococcus anginosus, necessitating surgical intervention. The allogeneic HSCT was performed using unmanipulated bone marrow (2.4 × 10^6 CD34-positive cells/kg) from his HLA-identical sibling following conditioning with cyclophosphamide (CYC, 50 mg/kg/day for 4 days) and antithymocyte globulin (ATG, 15 mg/kg/day for 4 days). On day -4, the patient experienced a febrile episode due to a bloodstream infection by Pseudomonas aeruginosa, which was successfully treated with piperacillin/tazobactam and tobramycin. Antimicrobial prophylaxis included aciclovir (later switched to foscarnet due to cytomegalovirus-positivity of donor and recipient) and micafungin (1 × 50 mg). For GvHD prophylaxis, methotrexate (10 mg/m2/day on day +2, +4, +7) and ciclosporin A (CSA) were administered. On day +23, CSA was replaced by tacrolimus due to peripheral neuropathy. On day +8, the patient developed another fever episode with elevated inflammatory markers, though blood cultures remained sterile. Antibacterial treatment was switched to meropenem and teicoplanin, leading to rapid fever resolution. Two days later, a painful fissure appeared between the 4th and 5th left toe without any physical injury. Microbiological samples identified multisensitive Escherichia coli, so the antibacterial treatment was continued. The lesion progressed to necrosis and became cavernous with a maximum depth of 1.5 cm. On day +15, painful, erythematous skin nodules were observed across the skin. Micafungin was replaced with liposomal amphotericin B (LAmB, 1 × 3 mg/kg, from day +31 5 mg/kg, stop date day +34) and intravenous (i.v.) voriconazole (VCZ, loading dose 2 × 6 mg/kg, maintenance therapy 2 × 4 mg/kg). Several cutaneous biopsies taken on day +17 histologically showed hyalohyphomycosis. Serum Aspergillus-galactomannan antigen on day +21 was negative.

input.case_report: A 41-year-old male arrived at the emergency department with primary complaints of high-grade intermittent fevers and severe abdominal pain. The fever was described as episodic, reaching a maximum temperature of 103°F, without chills or rigors, and was accompanied by a non-productive cough for one week. The patient also reported severe, intermittent, diffuse abdominal pain, diarrhea, and hematochezia for two days prior to the visit. He did not experience sore throat, rhinorrhea, joint pain, or rash. His medical history included ulcerative colitis (UC). He had lived in the Great Lakes region his entire life, worked in construction for many years, and had no recent travel history. He lived with three young children who had recently experienced a viral respiratory tract infection lasting about one week. He had pets at home, including a gecko, a rabbit, and two dogs. Ten months earlier, he had visited the ED with similar symptoms of diffuse abdominal pain and diarrhea with hematochezia for six weeks. Common conditions such as gastrointestinal infections, endocrine disorders, food allergies, and medication changes were ruled out, and a colonoscopy with tissue biopsy was performed. The biopsy revealed non-specific histological findings, including crypt abscess, mild architectural distortion of the lamina propria, and chronic inflammation. Markers of acute inflammation, such as erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and c-reactive protein (CRP), were elevated. The patient was started on prednisolone 40 mg daily, which resolved his symptoms. Attempts to switch to mesalamine resulted in mesalamine-induced pancreatitis, and treatment with Vedolizumab caused an allergic reaction after the second dose. Therefore, the patient continued on prednisolone 40 mg daily. Family history included inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) in his mother. He had no other significant medical history. On examination, he was febrile with a temperature of 103°F, heart rate of 112 beats per minute, and blood pressure of 124/74 mmHg. Abdominal examination revealed mild splenomegaly without tenderness. Laboratory investigations were conducted, and infectious disease consultation recommended starting broad-spectrum antibiotics. Laboratory results showed hemoglobin of 11.8 g/dL, white blood cell count of 4.1 × 10^9 cells/L with 70% granulocytes, 22% lymphocytes, and 0.1% eosinophils. Procalcitonin was elevated at 0.39 ng/mL, and liver enzymes were elevated with alanine aminotransferase at 232 U/L, alkaline phosphatase at 266 U/L, and aspartate transaminase at 79 U/L. Blood cultures showed no growth, and stool analysis was negative for Clostridium difficile and parasites.

input.case_report: Patient 1 is a 22-year-old woman who experienced a concussion without loss of consciousness in August 2016 due to a motor vehicle accident. A previously asymptomatic Chiari I malformation became symptomatic following the accident and was surgically decompressed in November 2016, but her symptoms persisted and worsened. In March 2017, she was evaluated at the W-TBI clinic, reporting difficulties with reading, concentrating, visual tracking, and focusing. Review of systems revealed headaches, neck and back pain, balance issues, fatigue, heartburn, frequent urination, swallowing difficulties, and impaired cognition, dysphagia, and choking. Examination showed convergence insufficiency and deficiency of smooth pursuits, evidenced by nausea and dizziness while tracking a pendulum. She was given computer-generated orthoptic exercises for home use and instructed to return in three months for re-evaluation. In June 2017, after completing the exercises, she still had trouble reading and concentrating, with additional complaints of headache, neck and back pain, dizziness, and poor balance. The exercises caused nausea and headaches. Minimal improvement was noted, so gentler orthoptic exercises were recommended. In October 2017, she reported discontinuing the exercises due to headaches and nausea. She had also been diagnosed with postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS). Review of systems showed minimal improvement. In the clinic, she demonstrated hyper-extensible elbows (Beighton score at least 3/9). She was provided more primitive orthoptic exercises, such as using a Brock string, tracking a printed labyrinth, and following a marble in a pie pan. In January 2018, she returned with minimal improvement in fatigue, headaches, dizziness, and inability to focus. The labyrinth and Brock string exercises were uncomfortable and nausea-inducing. Review of systems was otherwise unchanged. Recommendations were made for accommodations to assist with daily activities. In May 2018, she visited the emergency room after being struck in the face near her right eye, resulting in temporary visual blurriness.

input.case_report: A 71-year-old woman with a history of rheumatoid arthritis presented with generalized swelling, increased blood pressure, and non-blanchable red papules with a net-like pattern on both legs, persisting for 4 months. Rheumatoid arthritis was diagnosed 5 years earlier, characterized by joint pain and a positive rheumatoid factor. She was treated with prednisolone, methotrexate, and subcutaneous etanercept, achieving full clinical remission after 2 years, after which the medications were discontinued, and remission was maintained. Four months prior to admission, she developed swelling in both ankles, which gradually extended to the pretibial areas. She noticed her urine becoming foamier over the past 2 months, along with an increase in blood pressure as measured at home. There were no changes in urine color or volume. Physical examination revealed high blood pressure (190/94 mm Hg), bilateral pitting edema, and a net-like pattern on both legs. No other skin lesions were observed. There was no active joint inflammation, fever, or lymph node enlargement. Initial laboratory results showed a serum creatinine level of 1.86 mg/dL, dysmorphic red blood cells in urine, and proteinuria. Further tests indicated a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) with a homogeneous, fine-speckled, and nucleolar pattern (titer > 1:1280). The rheumatoid factor level was 54.40 IU/mL (normal < 20 IU/mL). Serum cryoglobulin was positive. Tests for HCV and HIV were negative. Hepatitis B virus serology showed negative HBsAg, positive anti-HBc, and positive anti-HBs antibodies. Complement C3 was normal, and C4 was 3.09 mg/dL (10–40 mg/dL).

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 16-year-old male presented to the hospital for evaluation and management of left femoral deep vein thrombosis (DVT) that had developed one month earlier. Initially, he was treated with enoxaparin 50 mg twice daily, later transitioning to rivaroxaban 20 mg once daily. During a visit to the hematology clinic, he reported ongoing pain in his left leg and new pain and swelling in his right leg, despite adherence to rivaroxaban. An emergent Doppler ultrasound confirmed bilateral thrombosis in the femoral veins. This finding led to his admission for further management. Three months prior, the patient experienced flu-like symptoms and acute otitis media following a SARS-CoV-2 booster shot. He had no other history of thrombosis. He denied any history of connective tissue diseases, oral or genital ulcers, Raynaud’s phenomenon, joint pain, or photosensitivity, and reported no use of other medications or supplements. There were no lifestyle factors such as smoking or recent immobility, and no symptoms suggestive of pulmonary embolism or stroke. He also denied any unusual or excessive bleeding episodes. Family history was negative for thrombosis, cancer, autoimmune diseases, and known thrombophilia conditions. Initial lab work during admission showed elevated levels of D-dimer (1,430 mg/L), CRP (75.9 mg/L), ESR (58 mm/h), INR (1.58), PTT (41.7 s), and PT (16.3 s). Extensive investigations for hereditary thrombophilia, including tests for Factor V Leiden, factor II prothrombin genetic mutation, hyperhomocysteinemia, Protein C, Protein S, and Antithrombin III, all returned normal levels. Tests for acquired thrombophilia, including antiphospholipid syndrome markers (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, and b2 macroglobulin) and vasculitis markers (C-ANCA and P-ANCA), were conducted. The lupus anticoagulant test was positive, potentially a false positive due to ongoing anticoagulant therapy. A CT chest showed no evidence of pulmonary embolism, while a CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast revealed thrombosis in the bilateral external iliac veins and femoral veins. After a week of treatment with enoxaparin 50 mg twice daily and notable symptom improvement, the patient was discharged and scheduled for follow-up at the hematology clinic.

input.case_report: An 18-year-old male arrived at the emergency department with a one-day history of persistent vomiting, mild diarrhea, and generalized abdominal pain. He reported no chest pain, fever, rashes, dysuria, or other symptoms. His medical history included poorly controlled type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM) diagnosed at age 7 and well-managed mild intermittent asthma. He also had chronic intermittent left shoulder pain from a motor vehicle accident (MVA) three years prior, during which he sustained a femur fracture that was surgically repaired. No abdominal or chest trauma was reported at the time of the MVA. Upon presentation, his vital signs were: temperature 36.6°C, heart rate 144 beats per minute, respiratory rate 22 breaths per minute, blood pressure 124/80 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Physical examination revealed signs of dehydration, including decreased skin turgor and dry oral mucosa. He was alert and oriented with no focal neurologic deficits. His abdomen was soft and non-tender without palpable masses. Pulmonary and cardiac examinations were normal. Laboratory workup showed elevated glucose (286 mg/dL, normal range: 100–180 mg/dL) and borderline anion gap metabolic acidosis (bicarbonate 17 mmol/L). Complete blood count and pancreatic enzymes were within normal limits, and a respiratory viral panel was negative. The patient was admitted to the pediatric unit for management of vomiting and dehydration in the context of hyperglycemia. He received long-acting insulin at bedtime with a short-acting insulin sliding scale. On the first and second days of admission, he reported new onset chest pain along with persistent nausea and vomiting. Vital signs at that time were: temperature 37°C, heart rate 116, respiratory rate 19, blood pressure 127/87, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Blood glucose levels remained elevated (>200 mg/dL) despite insulin therapy. ECG and cardiac enzyme levels were normal. On the third day, nausea and vomiting continued with minimal abdominal pain. He noted symptom relief following hot showers, and his urine toxicology was positive for cannabinoids and benzodiazepines. An abdominal X-ray (AXR) showed a non-obstructive bowel gas pattern with haziness in the left lung base. The next day, the patient experienced persistent tachycardia (heart rate 140) and mild respiratory distress. He was transferred to the pediatric intensive care unit, where vital signs were: temperature 36°C, heart rate 143, respiratory rate 35, blood pressure 115/76, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Physical examination revealed dry oral mucosa and diminished breath sounds at the left mid- and lower-lung fields. Fluid resuscitation was initiated, and laboratory tests were repeated.

input.case_report: A 69-year-old Japanese woman, who had been practicing yoga for 30 years and working as a yoga instructor for the past 2 years, observed skin hardening on her chest for 13 months before seeking medical advice. She experienced pain during physical activity in the affected areas, and the number and size of these skin lesions gradually increased. She did not report fever, joint pain, cough, or Raynaud's phenomenon. She had no history of ingesting l-tryptophan, toxic oil products, or using statin medications. Upon initial examination, multiple areas of shiny, hardened skin and painful, orange-peel-like lesions were noted on her chest and all four limbs. There were no signs of Raynaud's phenomenon, thickened skin on the fingers, finger ulcers, or bleeding around the nails. Laboratory tests showed elevated eosinophil levels (0.71 × 10^9/l, normal <0.45 × 10^9/l), while serum creatine kinase, myoglobin, aldolase, IgG, IgA, and IgM levels were within normal ranges, and antinuclear antibodies were negative.

input.case_report: A 56-year-old male presented with a 4-week history of night sweats, fevers, a 10-pound weight loss, and increasing shortness of breath. He reported no cough, hemoptysis, rash, or joint pain. His smoking history includes a 2-pack year habit during his teenage years, and he consumes approximately 15 alcoholic drinks per week. He has no history of illicit drug use and denies any known risk factors for tuberculosis or exposure to farm soil. His medical history includes bladder cancer, which was treated with transurethral resection of the bladder tumor 2 years ago. He began BCG immunotherapy a year ago and completed 15 instillations without complications, with the last instillation occurring 2 weeks before the onset of symptoms. Vital signs were within normal limits. Physical examination showed diffuse alveolar breath sounds without any abnormal sounds, and the rest of the examination was unremarkable. Chest X-ray revealed a diffuse nodular pattern, and a subsequent CT scan showed numerous small, dense nodules throughout all lung lobes without significant hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests indicated a mixed hepatocellular/cholestatic transaminitis with alkaline phosphatase at 271 U/L (normal 35-115 U/L), aspartate aminotransferase at 100 U/L (normal 15-43 U/L), and alanine aminotransferase at 179 U/L (normal 6-63 U/L). The Interferon-γ (IFN-γ) release assay was negative, but the purified protein derivative (PPD) test measured 10 mm. Coccidioides serologies showed positive immunoglobulin M (IgM) by immunodiffusion but were negative by complement fixation. CT and ultrasound imaging of the liver showed no abnormalities. Bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) of the right middle lobe revealed a lymphocytic predominance of white blood cells (475 cells/mm3 with 70% lymphocytes; normal: <1% lymphocytes and >95% macrophages) and negative bacterial, fungal, and mycobacterial cultures. Transbronchial biopsies of the right lower lobe showed noncaseating granulomas with negative acid-fast bacilli and fungal stains.

input.case_report: A 37-year-old male presented with weight loss, heat intolerance, excessive sweating, and palpitations for 6 months. On examination, he appeared anxious, had a rapid heart rate, brisk reflexes, and tremors. Thyroid function tests showed T3 at 2.58 ng/ml, T4 at 21.41 μg/dl, and TSH at 0.02 mIU/L. Ultrasound of the thyroid revealed both lobes were diffusely enlarged, measuring 5 × 2 cm. A 99mTechnetium scan indicated uniformly increased uptake at 36%. Tests for TSH receptor antibodies and anti-TPO antibodies were negative. A chest X-ray incidentally showed bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Further clinical evaluation showed no peripheral lymphadenopathy or respiratory abnormalities, and the respiratory system examination was unremarkable. There were no signs of skin, eye, or central nervous system involvement. Montoux test and interferon gamma release assay were normal, and sputum examination was negative for AFB by ZN stain. ESR decreased by 8 mm in the first hour. CECT chest revealed multiple homogenously enhancing enlarged lymph nodes in the mediastinum (maximum size 24 mm) and multiple well-defined nodules (2-4 mm in size) in a perilymphatic distribution. Spirometry and diffusion/lung volumes were normal. Serum ACE was 106.0 IU/L (normal <60 IU/L). Ultrasound of the abdomen was normal, and serum calcium was 8.9 mg/dl. 24-hour urinary calcium excretion was 3.96 g/day. Fiber optic bronchoscopy and transbronchial lung biopsy from the right lower lobe superior segment were performed.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 42-year-old man with no prior health issues experienced sudden vision loss in both eyes over a two-week period. The left eye's visual acuity began to decline two weeks prior, followed by the right eye one week later. He was initially diagnosed with central serous chorioretinopathy (CSC) at another clinic. Despite this, his visual acuity continued to deteriorate, prompting a visit to our clinic. His medical history included intermittent headaches, neck stiffness, and tinnitus for a month. He had no history of systemic disease, ocular trauma, or surgery. His best-corrected visual acuities were 20/200 in both eyes, with no chamber reaction observed. Vitreous cells were present at grade 2+ in both eyes, and fundus examination revealed bilateral serous retinal detachments with Elschnig's spots in the left eye. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) showed macular subretinal serous fluids in both eyes, with a minimum foveal thickness of 944 µm in the right eye and 818 µm in the left eye. Fluorescence angiography (FA) in the early phase indicated delayed choroidal filling and hypofluorescence in the macula, followed by mid-phase punctuate hyperfluorescence at the level of the retinal pigment epithelium along the boundaries of the detached retina. Late phase FA showed diffuse late leakage and fluorescence staining in the area of exudative retinal detachment. Indocyanine green angiography (ICGA) in the early phase showed broad hypofluorescence due to a choroidal filling defect in the posterior pole, followed by mid-phase fluorescence blockage in the area of exudative retinal detachment. Systemic evaluation revealed increased white blood cell counts (290,000/µL), with an abnormally high percentage of blast cells (89%).

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 75-year-old female with no known medical history arrived at the emergency department after fainting on the street, experiencing a loss of consciousness. She had a large exophytic lesion on the right parieto-occipital region of her scalp. The patient was found to be dehydrated and had a strong odor, admitting to a diet consisting only of cookies. During the examination, she was oriented to person, time, and place but exhibited rapid, delusional speech with themes of persecution and erotomania. Her vital signs were: blood pressure 124/66 mmHg, pulse 56 bpm, oxygen saturation 96% on room air, and she was afebrile. The lesion on her scalp was large, painless, erythematous, soft to touch, fluctuating, and multilobulated, with a central ulcer exuding purulent material and emitting a strong odor. The duration of the lesion could not be determined due to her delusional speech. Blood tests revealed microcytic anemia with hemoglobin at 2.8 g/dL and mean globular volume at 49.8 fL, along with iron and folate deficiencies. No urinary toxins were detected. A computed tomography (CT) scan showed a massive, multiloculated/multicystic lesion on the right parieto-occipital scalp, with partially calcified hyperdense areas and a potential central necrotic area. The lesion measured 21 cm transversely, 16.5 cm longitudinally, and 16 cm anteroposteriorly, with no bone erosion or intracranial involvement. There was also an incipient pattern of microvascular chronic ischemic leukoencephalopathy. CT angiography revealed a dense extracranial vascular network. The patient received three packs of red blood cells in the emergency department, raising her hemoglobin level to 7.3 g/dL before being transferred to the medical ward. The anemia was attributed to chronic blood loss and iron and folate deficiency, and she was treated with 1 g of ferric carboxymaltose and started on 5 mg of folate per day.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 41-year-old male tested positive for the Coronavirus Disease 2019 virus in early November of 2020. Initially, he recovered at home; however, he began to deteriorate, reporting whole-body shivering, confusion, paresthesia, gait instability, and urinary retention 16 days post-COVID diagnosis. His medical history included hypertension, ethanol, and methamphetamine substance use. On examination, the patient appeared diaphoretic, uncomfortable, and was slow to respond to questioning. Initial workup revealed elevated BUN/Creatinine ratio of 24 (range 10:1–20:1), prothrombin time 14.2 secs (range 9.1–13.9), INR 1.23 secs (range 0.80–1.20), venous pH 7.44 (range 7.31–7.41), lactate 1.4 mmol/L (range 0.0–1.3), venous bicarbonate 27 mmol/L (range 22–26), WBC count 18.3 × 103/µL (range 3.7–11.0 × 103), CRP inflammatory marker 9 mg/L (range > 8), neutrophil 14.15 × 103 (range 1.50–7.70 × 103) and PO2 30 mm/Hg (range 35–50), and creatine kinase 23 U/L (range 45–225). Upon admission, the patient underwent magnetic resonance imaging of the spine and brain with and without contrast. MRI of the spine revealed abnormal T2 hyperintensity from C2–C4 without post-contrast enhancement. Subsequent lumbar puncture revealed polymorphonuclear leukocytes with an absence of organisms and neutrophilic predominance. CSF revealed 45/µL nucleated cells (range 0–5), 90% lymphocytic predominance, 116 mg/dL protein (range 15–50), 37 mg/dL glucose (range 50–80), 0 oligoclonal bands, and IgG index 0.48. CSF and serum NMO Anti-AQP4 AB were negative. For possible meningoencephalitis, the patient was treated with IV ceftriaxone, vancomycin, ampicillin, and acyclovir. On day 6 of admission, a repeat brain MRI suggested a new T2-FLAIR lesion of the left corona radiata and right parietal subcortical white matter. Antibiotics were discontinued as CSF was negative for viral PCR; however, the patient remained confused and lethargic. IV Solumedrol 1 g/day for 5 days was administered. On day 7 of admission, a follow-up lumbar puncture revealed several polymorphonuclear leukocytes with an absence of organisms and a lymphocytic predominance. CSF analysis yielded 23/µL nucleated cells, protein 62 mg/dL and glucose 44 mg/dL. The patient's mental status improved significantly with the administration of Solumedrol. The patient was discharged 22 days after admission and instructed to follow-up with a neurologist in the next 3 months. At this time, the patient was stable without deficits. Six months later, the patient returned with complaints of a mild left-sided headache and left-sided retro-orbital pain with visual blurriness occurring over 1 week. The patient was initially seen in the ED, and a workup for possible demyelinating lesions was recommended. Examination of visual acuity in both eyes showed impaired left visual acuity (20/70), right 20/25, and left afferent pupillary defect (APD). Intraocular pressure and extraocular muscles (EOM) were within normal limits and intact. Ophthalmology recommended the patient for orbital MRI with/without contrast for evaluation of potential optic nerve lesions. Brain and orbital MRI w/wo contrast revealed pre-chiasmatic (intracanicular) enhancement of the left optic nerve, suggestive of optic neuritis. The patient’s concerning encephalopathy, optic neuritis, spinal, and brain lesions raised concern for neurological inflammatory post-COVID vaccination sequela.

input.case_report: A 42-year-old Chinese man presented with an 8-month history of painless swelling in his left testis and a 6-month history of a painless palpable nodule in his left inguinal region. He had no personal or family history of malignancy. Physical examination revealed a solid lump the size of an egg in the left testis and a slightly mobile, solid nodule measuring 2 × 3 cm in the left inguinal region. Laboratory tests, including α-fetoprotein, β-human chorionic gonadotropin, and lactate dehydrogenase, were within normal limits. Computed tomography (CT) identified a confined solid mass, 2 × 3 cm in size with homogenous attenuation in the left testis, and multiple lesions in the right diaphragmatic crus, retroperitoneal region, peritoneal and pelvic cavity, left inguinal region, and near the bilateral iliac vessels. Additionally, the left renal pelvis and ureter were dilated.

input.case_report: The patient is a 68-year-old male with a history of larynx carcinoma and a radical laryngectomy with bilateral neck dissection performed over 6 years ago, with no prior complications from the surgery. In the last 24 hours, he developed severe pain around the mass and increasing shortness of breath. There was no history of neck trauma, infection, or stroke. On physical examination, the patient was tachycardic, febrile, and dehydrated. A 15 x 5 x 2 cm pulsating and painful mass was noted in the left neck region, accompanied by general swelling and severe tenderness on palpation. A carotid murmur was heard on auscultation. The remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory tests showed leukocytosis with neutrophilia and anemia.

input.case_report: A 15-year-old female reported experiencing intermittent redness, irritation, and discomfort in both eyes over the past year, with the right eye being more affected. She had no notable medical or family history. Her visual acuity was measured at 6/6 in both eyes. Examination using a slit-lamp revealed the presence of large papillae in the inferior conjunctival forniceal regions of both eyes, with the right eye showing more extensive involvement. Routine blood tests, including liver function, electrolytes, and full blood count, were within normal limits. Initial treatment with topical steroids led to an improvement in ocular redness, but the patient continued to experience irritation and discomfort in the right eye and returned for further evaluation three months later.
