input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: An 87-year-old woman presented with pain and an enlarged mass on her left middle finger. At the age of 76, she experienced finger pain due to a mallet finger. Radiographs at that time incidentally showed a typical enchondroma in the proximal phalanx of the left middle finger. To prevent a pathological fracture, curettage was performed under axillary nerve block. The cortex adjacent to the lesion was accessed dorsally by splitting the extensor tendon. After fenestration of the bone cortex, curettage and packing with a granular artificial bone graft made of hydroxyapatite were carried out. Several years postoperatively, the left middle finger gradually started to enlarge, with an increased growth rate in the past year. She had undergone an excision of uterine leiomyoma 30 years earlier and had no significant personal or family medical history. Physical examination revealed the following: Height, 145 cm; weight, 42 kg; and body mass index, 20.0 kg/m2. The left middle finger appeared swollen and tense, with a tumor measuring 5.5 cm × 5.5 cm × 4.5 cm, which was elastic and hard. Laboratory test results were as follows: hemoglobin, 12.1 g/dL (normal range, 11.2-14.5); total leukocyte count, 6040/μL (normal range, 3300–8800); platelet count, 240000/μL (normal range, 130000-350000); erythrocyte sedimentation rate, 49.0 mm/hour (normal range, 0.0-15.0); C-reactive protein, 0.1 mg/dL (normal range, 0.0-0.14); lactate dehydrogenase, 152 IU/L (normal range, 119-229); and alkaline phosphatase (Japan Society of Clinical Chemistry; JSCC), 207 IU/L (normal range, 115-359). Plain radiographs showed residual artificial bone graft material and an expansive osteolytic lesion in the proximal phalanx of the left middle finger.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 24-year-old male office worker visited the outpatient orthopedics department with ongoing symptoms despite a previous surgical procedure. His issues began 6 months prior after a minor injury, which left him unable to support weight on his right leg. He reported no fever or general symptoms. An X-ray of the right hip and pelvis showed a subtrochanteric fracture of the right femur with displaced fragments, but no signs of osteoporosis or bone tumors. He underwent open reduction with intramedullary nail fixation to stabilize the fracture. Six months post-surgery, he returned with persistent local pain and swelling, still unable to bear weight on the right leg. New radiographs showed lysis around the implant and resorption of the fracture ends, with no callus formation. Due to financial constraints, advanced imaging like CT or MRI was not performed. He was scheduled for surgical debridement with external fixation. During surgery, the muscles and soft tissues were found to be indurated, and pearly white structures along with granulation tissue were observed at the lesion site. A thorough betadine wash was conducted, the excised tissue was sent for further examination, and an external fixator was applied. The excised specimen included multiple fragments, one of which was a pearly white-flattened structure measuring 2.8 cm × 2 cm × 1 cm.

input.case_report: A 70-year-old male presented with a color change on the right little finger following an injury caused by repeatedly hitting a metal can with a wrench. The patient has a 14-year history of taking warfarin (3 mg/day), a β-blocker (2.5 mg/day), and an ACE inhibitor (2.5 mg/day) for previously diagnosed conditions. He does not smoke. The injury affected his dominant hand, specifically the area distal to the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint of the little finger. He experienced sensory disturbance, pain, and a feeling of coldness distal to the metacarpophalangeal (MP) joint. The digital Allen test was negative. The range of motion was normal, and manual muscle testing of the flexor digitorum profundus (FDP) and flexor digitorum superficialis (FDS) scored 5/5. The Semmes-Weinstein monofilament test (S-W test) score was 4.56. X-ray findings were normal, and computed tomography (CT) images showed no fracture.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 1-year-old female child presented with distinct physical characteristics and developmental delay. She was born to a young couple with no blood relation, following a normal pregnancy. The mother was 28 years old and the father was 31 years old. At birth, the child weighed 2.5 kg. Physical examination revealed wide-set eyes, increased distance between the eyes, folds of skin over the inner corners of the eyes, drooping of one eyelid, a small nose, a thin upper lip, low-set ears, and a bilateral single crease across the palms. The thumb was nearly absent, with only a rudimentary spot in its place. There were no abnormalities detected in the gastrointestinal, genitourinary, or cardiac systems, and an echocardiogram showed normal heart function. Family history indicated that the father had mild intellectual disability and his sister had a female child with similar physical characteristics. Chromosomal analysis was performed on lymphocytes from peripheral blood using GTG banding. The child's karyotype was identified as 46, XX, inv (4) (q31.2q35.2). Both parents' lymphocytes were also cultured and karyotyped. The mother's karyotype was 46, XX with no chromosomal rearrangements, while the father's karyotype matched that of the child, showing 46, XY, inv (4) (q31.2q35.2).

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 33-year-old, 52.9 kg woman with CF (genotype delta F508/ R560T) was admitted with increased chest congestion, exertional dyspnea, sputum production, fatigue, and low-grade fever, symptoms characteristic of a pulmonary exacerbation. Based on the antimicrobial susceptibility profile obtained on prior outpatient sputum cultures, she received piperacillin-tazobactam (4.5 gm IV every 6 hours), ciprofloxacin (400 mg IV every 12 hours), azithromycin (500 mg by mouth three times per week chronically as part of her outpatient regimen), and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (800 mg trimethoprim component by mouth twice daily) for the treatment of Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Stenotrophomonas maltophilia. She had previously received each of these medications without adverse effect during other hospitalizations. During the night of her seventh hospital day, she experienced nausea and violent vomiting. She complained of severe pain in the back, arms, and chest. She also described weakness and paresthesias in her extremities and a small blur in the left inferotemporal visual field. On examination, the patient was anxious, pale, and diaphoretic. There was no fever, and vital signs included heart rate of 110 beats/min, blood pressure of 106/65 mmHg, respiratory rate of 23 breaths/min, and oxyhemoglobin saturation of 100% while breathing room air. The precordium was hyperdynamic, but her skin was cool, with livedo reticularis over the thighs, knees, and calves. Over the subsequent two days, the urine became dark, with a pink hue. The electrocardiogram and chest radiograph were unchanged. Laboratory evaluation disclosed a hemoglobin level of 4.8 g/dL, decreased from 12.2 g/dL two days earlier. Arterial blood analysis showed that the carboxyhemoglobin level was 5.3%. CT scan of the chest and abdomen did not reveal any acute process, and she was transferred to the intensive care unit for further management. Repeat blood count verified that the hemoglobin level was critically low at 4.3 g/dL, and the blood smear demonstrated stacked spherocytes. The lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) was 418 U/L, haptoglobin <20 mg/dL, and total bilirubin 2.4 mg/dL.

input.case_report: A 54-year-old male was admitted to the Department of Neurology at Tianjin Medical University General Hospital on October 24, 2014, due to left limb weakness persisting for one week. Initial imaging with dot diffusion-weighted MRI of the head revealed high signal intensity and a softened lesion in the right basal ganglia region. The patient reported a lifelong history of excessive thirst and urination, consuming 10-15 liters of water daily and experiencing frequent nighttime urination (5-6 times per night). There was no recent use of medications known to cause renal injury. His family history included a consanguineous marriage between his grandparents and seven relatives with similar symptoms of excessive thirst and urination. His younger brother died from dehydration during infancy. The patient had not sought medical attention or treatment for these symptoms. Physical examination revealed a blood pressure of 140/100 mmHg, a resting heart rate of 94 beats per minute, and a body mass index of 26.45 kg/m2. Muscle strength on the left side of his body was rated at level III. Other physical examinations were unremarkable. Laboratory tests showed elevated serum sodium (152 mmol/l) and chloride (113 mmol/l) levels, with an effective osmolality of 310 mOsm/kg.H2O. Other serum electrolytes were within normal ranges, including potassium (4.04 mmol/l) and calcium (2.53 mmol/l). Liver and kidney function tests were normal, with serum albumin at 43 g/l, urea nitrogen at 3.2 mmol/l, and creatinine at 44 µmol/l. Urine tests indicated a low specific gravity (1.003) and low osmolality (97 mOsm/kg.H2O) compared to normal values, with a total daily urine output of 12.0 liters. MRI of the pituitary gland showed an empty sella turcica. Thyroid and adrenal functions were normal. An ultrasound of the urinary tract revealed mild hydronephrosis in both kidneys and an overfilled bladder containing approximately 800 ml.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: An 87-year-old female underwent a radical cystectomy with an ileal conduit for invasive bladder cancer. Pathological examination demonstrated G3 urothelial carcinoma with a component of squamous cell carcinoma (<5%), pT3b. She received two cycles of adjuvant chemotherapy with methotrexate, vinblastine, doxorubicin, and cisplatin and was in good condition without metastatic disease. Nineteen years after the cystectomy, she was admitted to our hospital with anemia and Hb of 8 g/dL. Bleeding from the stoma continued and contrast-enhanced CT showed a tumor measuring 35 × 30 × 25 mm on the right-side wall of the conduit. Biopsy examination of the tumor revealed erosive mucosa and granulation tissue without malignancy. The bleeding from the stoma stopped with conservative therapy and she refused invasive treatment at the time; she was therefore discharged after 13 days’ hospitalization. After hospitalization, she underwent outpatient checkups every 3 months, with no recurrence of bleeding thereafter. Three years later, acute bleeding from the stoma recurred and she was hospitalized again with severe anemia (Hb of 5.9 g/dL). Contrast-enhanced CT revealed an enlarged tumor measuring 50 × 45 × 40 mm in the ileal conduit. The bleeding did not stop this time with conservative therapy and the size of this tumor had been gradually increasing over 3 years, so she underwent a complete resection of the tumor 16 days after admission. The tumor was recognized from the surface of her abdominal wall under general anesthesia. input.case_report: A 49-year-old Caucasian woman was referred to our center with a history of fibrous dysplasia in the left femur and tibia. X-rays of the left leg showed features of fibrous dysplasia in the proximal femur and tibia, including a ground glass appearance and a shepherd's crook deformity. MRI of the left leg indicated extensive fibrous dysplasia throughout the femur with expansive growth in the greater trochanter and fibrous dysplasia in the proximal tibia extending 20 cm distally from the tibial plateau. Four well-delineated, cyst-like, intra-muscular soft-tissue lesions were observed in the quadriceps region. Surgery was planned in two stages. Before each operation, angiography and embolization of the abnormal vascularization in both the femur and tibia were performed. The first operation involved intra-lesional excision of the tumor in the left proximal femur, followed by cryosurgery. A correctional osteotomy of the left hip was performed, along with homologous bone transplantation and fixation using a proximal femoral nail. Due to a weakened femoral head, the femoral neck screw was stabilized with bone cement, and allograft bone chips were used to induce bone matrix. An incisional biopsy of the soft-tissue lesions in the quadriceps muscle was also performed. Three weeks later, an intra-lesional excision of the fibrous dysplasia in the left tibia was conducted, followed by cryosurgery and implantation of a massive allograft inlay in the tibia, which was fixed with AO screws. During this procedure, the soft-tissue lesions in the quadriceps muscle were excised. Microscopic examination of the bone lesions in both the femur and tibia revealed hypocellular fibrous tissue with irregular bone formations and no cytologic atypia. One lesion contained remnants of fracture callus. The soft-tissue lesions showed a paucicellular tumor with spindle-shaped or stellate cells embedded in a loose myxoid alcian blue-positive inter-cellular matrix with sparse capillary blood vessels.

She was referred for further investigation of obscure gastrointestinal bleeding. Despite receiving repeated red blood cell transfusions, with the most recent one week prior, her initial hemoglobin level was 3.8 g/dL. Her coagulation profile and tumor markers, including carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and carbohydrate antigen 19-9 (CA19-9), were within normal limits.

input.case_report: A 47-year-old heterosexual Caucasian man, initially diagnosed with UC in 2010, presented with disease affecting the entire colon. Initial treatment included oral prednisone followed by mesalazine for approximately three years. In 2013, after a severe relapse with partial response to steroids, the patient began receiving infliximab (IFX) at a standard dosage of 5 mg/Kg, achieving clinical and endoscopic remission. IFX was administered every 8 weeks until July 2017, when it was discontinued due to a progressive loss of response. In September 2017, the patient experienced a flare-up characterized by 5–6 liquid and bloody bowel movements per day and abdominal pain. The partial Mayo index score was 7 (indicating severe disease). Laboratory tests showed anemia (hemoglobin 10.3 g/L) and elevated C-reactive protein (18 mg/L, normal value < 5). After ruling out intestinal infections, the patient was started on the α4-β7 integrin inhibitor vedolizumab (VDZ) at a standard dosage of 300 mg via intravenous infusion, administered at weeks 0, 2, and 6 following the standard induction protocol. The patient reported early clinical improvement and received three additional administrations every 8 weeks. Despite this, he experienced a progressive loss of clinical response, leading to the discontinuation of VDZ. In April 2018, a colonoscopy revealed severe pancolitis with deep ulceration, spontaneous bleeding, and nodular mucosa, particularly in the transverse and right colon (Mayo endoscopic score 3). Rectal biopsies were taken, and histopathological evaluation showed negative results for intranuclear cytomegalovirus inclusions.

input.case_report: A 23-year-old female reported a painful, palpable mass at the junction of her left vulvar crease and proximal thigh, persisting for two months and causing discomfort during sitting and movement. The area showed no erythema or warmth, and there was no history of trauma. Hematology labs were normal. An ultrasound with Doppler ordered by her primary care provider revealed a hypoechoic mass measuring 3.5 × 3 x 2.4 cm in the left inguinal region with hilar blood flow. Further evaluation with a CT or biopsy was recommended. An ultrasound-guided biopsy performed a week later showed irregularly arranged bland spindle cells with myxoid stroma. FISH analysis indicated rearrangement of the USP6 (17p13.2) locus and no fusion of the MYH9 (22q12.3) and USP6 (17p13.2) loci. A CT scan conducted two weeks post-biopsy did not visualize the mass. An MRI performed one month after the CT scan revealed a mass measuring 4.8 × 2.7 × 4.0 cm extending from the uppermost portion of the left gracilis muscle to the inferior left pubic ramus, causing a mass effect on the gracilis muscle. The mass appeared heterogeneous, primarily isointense to muscle on T1, hyperintense on T2, and showed diffuse enhancement after contrast administration. A small tail of enhancing tissue was noted anterior to the dominant mass. No enlarged left inguinal lymph nodes or surrounding edema were observed.

input.case_report: An 18-year-old male presented with a 4-month history of exertional cough and dyspnea. He had no significant medical or familial history and had not pursued further education or employment after junior high school. He initially sought medical attention for epigastric discomfort and nausea, where urinalysis indicated significant proteinuria. He was subsequently referred to our hospital for further evaluation. On admission, his vital signs included a body temperature of 36.7°C, weight of 62 kg, height of 168 cm, and blood pressure of 157/85 mm Hg. Physical examination revealed leg edema and bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy, with no skin lesions observed. Laboratory tests showed decreased serum total protein (5.9 g/dL) and albumin (2.6 g/dL), normal serum creatinine (0.9 mg/dL) and eGFR (95 mL/min/1.73 m2), elevated LDL cholesterol (224 mg/dL), and slightly elevated serum calcium (10.6 mg/dL). BNP level was significantly elevated at 1,850 pg/mL. Urinalysis revealed subnephrotic proteinuria (3.0 g/day), microhematuria (5–9/HPF), with no leukocyturia or bacteria. Elevated soluble interleukin 2 receptor level (2,905 U/mL) was noted, while serum ACE level was normal (14.2 U/L). Renin activity was elevated (39 ng/mL/h), and plasma aldosterone level was within normal limits (184 pg/mL). Urinary N-acetyl-glucosaminidase level was elevated (85.6 IU/L). Tests for hepatitis B surface antigen, hepatitis C antibody, and HIV antigen and antibody were negative. Immunoglobulins, complements, rheumatoid factor, antinuclear antibody, and antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies were all negative. Tuberculin test was negative. Imaging studies included a chest X-ray showing enhanced pulmonary congestion without hilar lymphadenopathy, and chest CT revealing hilar lymphadenopathy without lung field fibrosis. Abdominal CT showed bilateral normal-sized kidneys and bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy. Gallium-67 scintigraphy indicated uptake in the pulmonary hilum, mediastinum, bilateral submaxillary glands, bilateral parotid glands, and bilateral inguinal regions. Echocardiography revealed severe cardiac dysfunction (ejection fraction 25%), diffuse akinesis of the ventricular wall, and atrial septal defect, but no thinning of the interventricular septum base. Electrocardiogram-gated cardiac CT and cine MRI confirmed thinning of the interventricular septum base. During the first admission, the patient was treated for heart failure and hypertension with temocapril hydrochloride, a calcium channel antagonist, carvedilol, spironolactone, and carperitide. This treatment led to a reduction in urinary protein levels to less than 1 g/day and improvement in heart failure and hypertension. The patient was discharged on the 30th hospital day. An outpatient inguinal biopsy detected granulomas.

input.case_report: A 76-year-old male with hypertension and severe pulmonary valve stenosis, who recently began haemodialysis for chronic kidney disease, presented with fever, chills, and weakness. One month earlier, he had been hospitalized with similar symptoms and was found to have bacteraemia with serial blood cultures positive for Staphylococcus haemolyticus. He received appropriate antibiotic therapy, and a previously placed right internal jugular hemodialysis catheter was removed. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a thickened and calcified pulmonary valve with a reduced opening. Continuous wave Doppler measured a peak systolic gradient of 75 mmHg and a mean systolic gradient of 42 mmHg across the pulmonary valve. No valve vegetations were observed. The patient was discharged in good condition after 15 days. Two weeks later, he was readmitted with fever, blood cultures again positive for Staphylococcus haemolyticus, and was referred to our institution. Physical examination on admission was unremarkable.

Forty-eight hours post-surgery, the patient experienced self-limited abdominal bleeding, likely associated with a high dose of low molecular weight heparin (LMWH), which was managed conservatively. Concurrently, the patient developed hiccups that initially responded to haloperidol and chlorpromazine but later increased in frequency and intensity, becoming uncontrollable. A CT scan confirmed no intraabdominal complications, and the patient was admitted to the intensive care unit for enhanced hiccup management. In the following days, the patient developed fever spikes. Blood cultures indicated bacteraemia associated with a central venous catheter (CVC), and there were inflammatory signs at the right jugular venous access site.

input.case_report: A 15-year-old male presented to the medical outpatient department of Mayo Hospital Lahore on July 7, 2022, with complaints of difficulty walking for one year. He had been in his usual state of health around one year ago when he developed constipation, sudden in onset, associated with diffuse abdominal pain which had been relieved by enemas and laxatives given at a local hospital. An abdominal ultrasound scan had been performed at that time, which had been normal. Two days later, he started experiencing weakness in his legs, which he described as an inability to put weight on his feet. The weakness had been gradual and progressive, initially making it difficult for him to walk but had progressed to involve upper limbs over the next three days. It had been associated with numbness of forearms and legs (up to knee joints). The weakness had progressed to make him bedridden within two months. At that time, the patient had started to notice that he had been passing red-colored urine. However, he had not experienced any urinary retention, urinary incontinence, or fecal incontinence. He had started experiencing generalized tonic-clonic fits eight months back. He had multiple episodes of fits over the last eight months. The fits usually lasted two to three minutes. These episodes had been associated with low-grade fever. He had given a history of intermittent abdominal pain and constipation for the last eight months, which had been relieved by laxatives. He had not experienced bloating, weight loss, or mucous in stool. He only had generalized non-specific abdominal pain. The patient had also started experiencing hair fall, cold intolerance, and constipation at that time. There had been no history of oral ulcers, photophobia, skin rash, blurry vision, or diplopia. There had also been no history of headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, hemoptysis, shortness of breath, drug abuse, and weight loss. His parents had noted his aggressive behavior since the beginning of his symptoms. He had started to get angry with his family members over minor things. However, there had been no history of inappropriate laughs, weeping spells, suicidal ideation, and hallucinations. The patient has a brother who has no such complaints. His family history was also insignificant. The patient had a good nutritional intake. On general physical examination, the patient's hands and conjunctiva were pale. He had marked muscle wasting visibly on his arms and calves. His CNS examination showed intact cranial nerves and cerebellar function. He had impaired pin-prick sensation up to the knee joint and mid-forearm. He had decreased bulk and tone in bilateral upper and lower limbs. There were no visible fasciculations. Power in proximal bilateral upper limbs was 5/5 and distal bilateral upper limbs was 2/5. Power in proximal bilateral lower limbs was 4/5 and distal bilateral lower limbs was 0/5. The patient had bilateral wrist and foot drops. His abdominal examination showed a soft and non-tender abdomen. There were no visible lesions, no palpable masses, and no distention. Auscultation showed normal bowel sounds. His digital rectal examination was also normal. The patient had his CT and MRI brain done at a private hospital before his presentation to the Mayo Hospital Lahore. His CT brain showed a very mild ventricular dilatation. There were no abnormalities on the CT scan. His MRI was also completely normal and no pathology was identified. The patient was admitted to the medical ward. His complete blood count and peripheral smear showed hypochromic, microcytic red blood cells with a decreased reticulocyte count (Hb: 8.7 g/dL, MCV: 67 μm3, reticulocytes: 1.4%). The patient was scheduled for nerve conduction studies to determine the type of neuropathy responsible for distal muscle weakness. His nerve conduction studies suggested mixed sensory and motor polyneuropathy more severe distally and in the lower limbs. His electroencephalography (EEG) showed a mixture of theta and delta waves with no epileptic waveforms. The patient’s CSF was drawn through a lumbar puncture and sent for CSF analysis to rule out acute and chronic inflammatory polyneuropathies. His CSF was negative for xanthochromia, atypical cells, and microorganisms with a few RBCs and lymphocytes in the background. The patient had two acute attacks of abdominal pain and fits during his stay in the ward. His abdominal ultrasound scans were normal during both attacks. He was given antispasmodics, which improved his pain. His labs during the acute attack consistently showed hyponatremia (Na+: 119 mmol/L).

input.case_report: A 19-year-old female with a history of frequent bloody stools was admitted to our hospital in 2017. She had been diagnosed with ulcerative colitis (pancolitis) via total colonoscopy in 2013 and was treated with 3600 mg/d of 5-aminosalicylic acid (5-ASA), achieving remission. In 2015, she experienced a recurrence of bloody stools and was treated with 50 mg/d of AZA. Despite this treatment, occasional bloody stools persisted, and prednisolone (PSL) was administered, leading to improvement. The AZA dose was increased to 100 mg/d in 2016, and although she still occasionally experienced bloody stools, her symptoms were managed with additional 5-ASA suppositories. In 2017, she presented with fever, ocular pains, chills, and cough for 5 days prior to admission. Her neutrophil count had decreased to 589/μL, and leukocyte reduction was not evident in blood results 2 days before her symptoms started. She developed a fever of 38.8 °C, but no abnormalities were observed in the chest and abdomen, and there was no cervical lymph node swelling. Clinical findings included fever, mild anemia (hemoglobin, 11.1 g/dL), mild thrombocytopenia (13.0 × 10^4/μL), mildly elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) (1.32 mg/dL), and neutropenia (white blood cell count, 940/μL; neutrophils, 589/μL). Colonoscopy showed mild inflammation of the mucosa on the left side of the colon with a Mayo score of 1. She was treated with 2 g/d of cefepime and granulocyte-colony stimulating factor (G-CSF), resulting in WBC and neutrophil counts improving to 2650/μL and 1661/μL, respectively. Despite treatment, her fever persisted, and right cervical lymphadenopathy appeared. Thoracoabdominal contrast computed tomography revealed splenomegaly and splenic infarction. Biochemical examination showed liver dysfunction (Aspartate transaminase, 225 IU/L; alanine aminotransferase, 200 IU/L), elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) (589 IU/L), ferritin (740 ng/mL), and soluble interleukin 2 receptor (sIL-2R) (5210 IU/mL). Aggravated anemia (hemoglobin, 9.4 g/dL), thrombocytopenia (8 × 10^4/μL), and the presence of atypical lymphocytes (2%) were also noted on peripheral blood examination.

input.case_report: A 19-year-old female student from a central urban district of Kashmir was referred to a genetic counseling center by a local gynecologist. The patient had not experienced menarche and presented with primary amenorrhea. She measured 5.8 feet in height and weighed 54 kg. Secondary sexual characteristics were underdeveloped, with the presence of breast buds rather than fully developed breasts. An interview confirmed the presence of female genital organs. The patient was born from a consanguineous marriage, with both parents being paternally related. She was the first of four children, and the only one to survive gestation following three consecutive abortions. The patient exhibited facial features typically associated with male individuals, including high eyebrows, a prominent forehead, and a pronounced jawline. Regular ultrasonographic examinations were recommended. Ultrasonography revealed the complete absence of the uterus and the presence of small, atretic ovaries. Additionally, it showed Müllerian agenesis. Hormonal profiling indicated lower levels of follicle-stimulating hormone (15.15 mIU/ml), elevated levels of luteinizing hormone (33.71 mIU/ml), and prolactin (5.6 mIU/ml). Estradiol levels were found to be less than 12.5 ng/ml, consistent with levels seen in menopausal adults.

input.case_report: A 22-year-old male presented with fever, sore throat, generalized weakness, myalgia, and abdominal pain for 3 days. Initial outpatient treatment did not alleviate symptoms, leading to hospital admission. Examination revealed tachycardia (heart rate: 118/min) and febrile spikes (Tmax: 102.7°F). The rest of the systemic examination was unremarkable, including a normal oral cavity examination, and no petechiae or muscle tenderness. The patient had a past history of rheumatic fever during childhood, treated with penicillin prophylaxis, which was discontinued seven years prior. There was no history of heart issues or recurrent joint pain. Laboratory investigations showed leukocytosis (thin-layer chromatography: 20.3 × 10^3), with other routine blood tests within normal limits. After blood cultures were taken, intravenous antibiotics were initiated. During hospitalization, the patient developed tachypnea, tachycardia, and chest pain. Electrocardiogram revealed global ST depression, and troponin I levels were elevated. The patient was intubated due to respiratory distress. A 2D echocardiogram showed new onset global hypokinesia, which was normal at admission. Febrile spikes persisted, and antimicrobials were upgraded. Blood cultures, throat swab for H1N1, and diphtheria tests were negative. Febrile spikes continued with evening rises, upper abdominal pain, and tachycardia. A repeat 2D echocardiogram normalized, and troponin I levels decreased. Hemodynamics remained stable throughout the hospital stay. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the chest and abdomen showed bilateral basal consolidation. Baseline PCT levels were elevated. Tests for Leptospira, dengue, malarial parasite, HIV, hepatitis C virus, HbsAg, scrub typhus, and Brucella serology were negative. Antinuclear antibody (ANA), ANA profile, rheumatoid arthritis, C-ANCA, P-ANCA, and antistreptolysin O titers were also negative. Transesophageal echocardiography did not show infective endocarditis. Antibiotics were upgraded to colistin after Acinetobacter was isolated from bronchoalveolar lavage cultures. Despite colistin treatment, febrile spikes and episodes of pulmonary edema persisted. Serial PCT levels remained elevated. The patient failed extubation and required reintubation. Repeat computed tomography of the chest was consistent with prior findings. A septic polymerase chain reaction panel was negative, and bone marrow biopsy and cultures were inconclusive.

input.case_report: A 39-year-old male with a history of left ventricular non-compaction cardiomyopathy since 2014 and an ICD implantation in 2016 underwent an uncomplicated LVAD implantation in June 2017. In June 2018, he experienced a methicillin-susceptible driveline infection after dropping his LVAD controller, which was treated with an 11-day course of intravenous cloxacillin followed by oral cloxacillin suppression. In July 2018, he presented with a 1-week history of exertional dyspnoea and ICD firing due to ventricular tachycardia. He was afebrile with no localizing signs of infection, a well-healed driveline, and a slight increase in CRP at 32.1 mg l−1, while other infective markers were within normal limits. On hospital day 2, he developed a fever of 38.2 °C and a new pustular lesion near his driveline exit site. He was started on cefepime and vancomycin. On hospital day 3, he developed persistent atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, requiring DCCV on hospital days 4 and 5. This was associated with progressive leukocytosis and rising CRP and procalcitonin levels. A contrasted CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis on hospital day 4 did not reveal any infective foci, and transthoracic echocardiograms on hospital days 5 and 8 showed no vegetations. Despite broad-spectrum antibiotics, his condition deteriorated. On hospital day 4, he developed anuric renal failure requiring CRRT. By hospital day 7, his LVAD pump flow had increased, correlating with uptrending inflammatory markers. Serial blood cultures remained negative. He developed pump thrombosis, leading to cardiogenic shock, multi-organ failure, haemolysis (LDH >15,000 U l−1, haptoglobin <0.10 g l−1), and abnormal LVAD parameters with elevated pump power of 7.3 watts and peak pump flow of 10 l min−1. LVAD pump exchange was deemed inappropriate due to high operative mortality risk and ongoing sepsis. On hospital day 10, systemic thrombolysis with alteplase was attempted without success, as cardiogenic shock and haemolysis persisted with elevated LVAD pump power and flow. The patient continued to deteriorate and expired on hospital day 12.

input.case_report: In October 2020, a 75-year-old man was referred to our department due to a one-year history of mild forgetfulness and abnormal brain MRI findings. He had completed 16 years of education and was employed as the president of a service company. Prior to his referral, neuropsychological tests and brain MRI were conducted at another hospital. His Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) and Frontal Assessment Battery (FAB) scores were 30 out of 30 and 18 out of 18, respectively, six months before his referral. The Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale-III (WAIS-III) results showed a verbal IQ of 147, a performance IQ of 131, and a full-scale IQ of 142. Two months before the referral, MRI scans, including DWI and T2-weighted imaging, revealed increased signal intensity with swelling in the cerebral cortices, excluding the medial occipital and cerebellar cortices. His wife noted that he would forget the location of items and repeat questions. Despite these issues, his daily life and professional responsibilities remained intact. His past medical history included only appendicitis, and there was no reported family history of dementia. Neurological examination showed no abnormalities. He declined further investigations at that time.

input.case_report: A 9-year-old boy, with no prior medical history, was admitted due to a persistent high fever reaching 40°C, lasting for one month, accompanied by anorexia, weakness, vomiting, and significant weight loss. Upon clinical examination, the child was found to be in poor physical condition, febrile at 39.5°C, and pale. Abdominal palpation revealed hepatosplenomegaly. Further physical examination showed lenticular cervical and inguinal lymph nodes. Laboratory tests indicated leukopenia (white blood count = 1080/mm3, normal: 4000–10000/mm3), neutropenia (neutrophil count = 750/mm3, normal: 3000–5600/mm3), lymphopenia (lymphocytes = 120/mm3, normal: 3000–5500/mm3), anemia (hemoglobin = 6.6 g/dL, normal: 11.5–14.5 g/dL), thrombocytopenia (platelet count = 79000/mm3, normal: 150000–450000/mm3), elevated C-reactive protein (171 mg/L, normal: 0–6 mg/L), abnormal liver function (serum glutamate oxaloacetic transaminase = 258 IU/L, normal: 5–34 IU/L; serum glutamate pyruvate transaminase = 168 IU/L, normal: 0–55 IU/L), elevated lactate dehydrogenase (973 IU/L, normal: 0–248 IU/L), hyperferritinemia (703 μg/L, normal: 20–250 μg/L), hypertriglyceridemia (3.7 g/L, normal: 0–1.50 g/L), normal fibrinogen levels (3.9 g/L, normal: 2–4 g/L), and hypoalbuminemia (19.2 g/L, normal: 33–50 g/L). Bone marrow aspiration showed numerous macrophages with hemophagocytosis without evidence of malignancy. Bacteriological evaluations (blood, urine, and stool cultures, tuberculosis tests, and mycoplasma serology), viral evaluations (serology of Epstein-Barr virus (EBV), cytomegalovirus, parvovirus B19, human immunodeficiency virus, and hepatitis A, B, and C), and parasite evaluations (toxoplasmosis, leishmaniasis) were negative. Immune tests (antinuclear antibodies, anti-DNA, and rheumatoid factor) were normal. The search for EBV by polymerase chain reaction (PCR) could not be performed due to financial constraints. The patient was treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics and high-dose intravenous immunoglobulin. Despite treatment, his condition worsened, presenting with dry cough and dyspnea. Chest X-ray showed an alveolar syndrome in the lower lobe of the right lung and minimal bilateral pleural effusion. Thoracoabdominopelvic computed tomography revealed scattered nodules in various lung segments, the largest measuring 15 mm in diameter, a right lung parenchymal condensation, multiple enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes, the largest measuring 14 mm, minimal pleural and pericardial effusions, hepatosplenomegaly with multiple small nodular hypodense lesions, and hilar and para-aortic infracentimetric lymph nodes. Echocardiography showed minimal pericardial effusion with normal cardiac function. Bacteriological examination of pleural fluid was negative.

input.case_report: A 7-year-old boy, who predominantly uses his right hand, injured his right wrist after falling while playing football. X-rays of the right forearm in anteroposterior and lateral views showed a greenstick fracture of the distal radius with dorsal angulation. The forearm was initially immobilized with a below-elbow plaster slab, which was replaced with a cast three days later. Follow-up x-rays taken one week post-injury showed no change in the alignment. The cast was removed after three weeks. X-rays at the three-week follow-up revealed a faintly defined lucent area within the subperiosteal callus, just proximal to the fracture site, measuring approximately 8 mm by 5 mm. The child was comfortable, without fever, and there were no signs of local inflammation at the fracture site. Initial x-rays were reexamined to rule out a preexisting lesion. Blood tests, including whole blood-cell count and C-reactive protein levels, were normal. The child was advised to avoid contact sports to prevent reinjury until the next follow-up. At three months, a well-defined 8 mm × 5 mm cystic lesion was observed at the original site within the newly formed subperiosteal bone, proximal to the fracture site. The fracture line remained visible, but clinically, the fracture had united.

input.case_report: A 58-year-old male presented with a gradually enlarging bone mass originating from the right 11th rib. He was on anticoagulant (warfarin potassium) and antiplatelet therapy (aspirin) following a mitral valve replacement for infective endocarditis associated with atrial fibrillation. Imaging studies, including X-ray and CT, revealed a lytic lesion with cortical bone destruction in the 11th rib, along with calcification or coarse trabeculae within the mass. The activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) was within normal limits (24.4 seconds) immediately before the open biopsy. Despite the use of prolonged manual compression and hemostatic agents during the open biopsy with a small surgical incision, the patient experienced moderate bleeding (300 mL).

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 29-year-old female with a 4-year history of SLE and grade II lupus nephritis presented with status epilepticus. She had been treated with cyclophosphamide one month prior for cerebral lupus. She reported noticing a papule over the left deltoid region that progressed to an ulcer over one week. Fever developed several days after hospital admission, and the ulcer site became painful. She had worked in paddy fields several months before admission but did not recall any injury at the affected site. She is a mother of two with uncomplicated pregnancies and denied alcohol abuse or smoking. On examination, she was emaciated with a GCS score of 15/15 post-status epilepticus recovery. There was no lymphadenopathy. The ulcer initially measured about 3 cm and developed a necrotic center with surrounding erythema, eventually advancing into the underlying muscle over three weeks despite antibiotic treatment. Cardiovascular, respiratory, and abdominal examinations were normal. Laboratory results indicated microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, which resolved following plasmapheresis. ESR was persistently normal. Renal and liver functions remained stable during the hospital stay. Chest radiography showed bilateral mild pleural effusions, and echocardiography revealed a thin rim of pericardial effusion with good cardiac function. MRI and MRA of the brain indicated Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome, with repeat imaging showing resolved changes.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 19-month-old Hispanic female toddler exhibited poor growth over time. Despite achieving developmental milestones, her height and weight were below the third percentile, with a weight of 8.7 kg and a height of 75 cm. She was born full-term via spontaneous vaginal delivery without complications during labor or pregnancy. The child was from a nonconsanguineous marriage and was not on any medications. There were no other siblings with similar growth issues, and there was no history of malabsorptive conditions. The review of systems was negative for symptoms such as vomiting, diarrhea, fever, changes in appetite, swallowing difficulties, respiratory symptoms, apnea, frequent acute illnesses, or frequent injuries. Physical examination revealed an alert, playful child who was small for her age but developmentally appropriate. Head/neck, cardiac, respiratory, gastrointestinal, genitourinary, musculoskeletal, and neurological examinations were normal. There was no evidence of dysmorphism or alopecia. Baseline laboratory tests showed calcium levels at 7.6 mg/dl, alkaline phosphatase at 2023 IU/L, PTH at 1115 pg/ml, phosphorus at 2.9 mg/dl, 25-OH vitamin D3 at 14.2 ng/ml, and 1,25(OH)2 vitamin D3 at 505 pg/ml.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: An 18-year-old male was referred to a Tertiary Hospital Allergy department due to recurrent episodes of anaphylaxis attributed to food allergy. The patient had a history of recurrent wheezing since early infancy, rhinitis, and skin lesions on the scalp since the age of 8 months, which frequently flared and relapsed. Some flares were associated with flushing, angioedema, and presyncope. At age 3, the patient was placed on a strict avoidance diet and prescribed an adrenaline autoinjector and glucocorticoids as needed. At this age, he also developed skin lesions suggestive of atopic dermatitis. At age 6, he began experiencing oral allergy symptoms with peanut and tree nuts, and throat tightness following balloon filling. This situation caused significant stress and anxiety, requiring follow-up with Psychiatry of Childhood and Adolescence. At age 12, after ingesting egg or egg-containing food, he complained of throat constriction without other symptoms. A microarray multiple allergen component assay showed polysensitization to food and inhalant allergens, including tree nut, peanut, olive and mugwort lipid transfer proteins (LTP), major latex allergen, kiwi thaumatin-like protein, shrimp, timothy grass pollen, and house dust mites (HDM), with a total IgE of 84 kU/L, leading to further dietary restrictions. He was advised to avoid apple, kiwi, and peach, although no oral food challenges were performed. At age 12, he was started on monthly anti-IgE (omalizumab) for “chronic spontaneous urticaria with angioedema,” which was maintained for 2 years without improvement. He often complained of angioedema and flushing following omalizumab administration. During endoscopic knee surgery at age 17, he experienced flushing despite five previous uneventful anesthetic procedures. At the first appointment at our department, the patient reported spells of flushing, sometimes with angioedema and/or presyncope, occurring 3–4 times per week. He followed a highly restrictive diet, avoiding fresh fruits other than banana, tree nuts, peanuts, chestnuts, walnuts, apple, pear, kiwi, and olive oil, as advised by his previous physician. He also avoided codfish and shellfish, despite tolerating other types of fish and shrimp. He reported mild perennial allergic rhinitis symptoms that worsened during pollen season and exercise-induced bronchoconstriction. Physical examination revealed exuberant widespread keratosis pilaris involving the whole trunk, four scalp lesions, and three nodular lesions on the trunk that developed a wheal and flare reaction upon stroking (Darier's sign). Nasal and oropharyngeal inspection, cardiopulmonary auscultation, and abdominal inspection were unremarkable. Skin prick testing (SPT) with common aeroallergens, nuts, shellfish, and oral allergy syndrome-specific batteries, complete blood counts, specific IgE (sIgE), and serum basal tryptase measurements were performed, along with oral food challenges with apple, pear, and egg. Complete blood counts did not show anemia, leukopenia, leukocytosis, or thrombocytopenia. SPT was positive for HDM and olive tree pollen, while sIgE was positive for HDM (Dermatophagoides pteronyssinus 51.5 kU/L, Lepidoglyphus destructor 32.3 kU/L), grass mix (21.8 kU/L), olive tree (4.5 kU/L), latex (11.9 kU/L), peanut (0.70 kU/L), kiwi (0.54 kU/L), and tree nut (0.40 kU/L). sIgE for both egg yolk and white, and codfish were negative. Serum basal tryptase ranged between 7 and 9.4 ng/mL, 6 months apart. Spirometry was normal with a negative bronchodilation test. All oral food challenges were negative. He was referred to the dermatology department due to the exuberance of the trunk keratosis pilaris and nodules.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: The patient was a 37-week and 5-day female, born to a 32-year-old G4P2022 female via uncomplicated vaginal delivery. The patient exhibited a congenital anomaly in her upper right limb, characterized by a right-hand deformity with a single digit and a partial right ulna. There was a small defect in the skin overlying the proximal portion of the partial radius. The pregnancy began as a dichorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy, with one fetus experiencing vanishing twin syndrome. Additional complications included velamentous cord insertion, low pregnancy-associated plasma protein A (PAPP-A), and gestational hypertension. The patient's mother received full prenatal care, including maternal-fetal medicine follow-up due to previous recurrent pregnancy loss. During the pregnancy, the mother took aspirin, sertraline, and levothyroxine daily and reported no use of tobacco, alcohol, or other drugs. The parents had no known genetic anomalies or family history of limb defects. The upper limb deformity was first observed at the 20-week ultrasound, and the parents chose not to pursue fetal genetic testing. Follow-up ultrasounds indicated a severely shortened ulna and a single digit on the right hand. Besides the limb deformity, the patient was small for gestational age (2425g or 5lb 5.5oz) and had an incomplete cleft of the soft palate. She was otherwise healthy with no other apparent limb deformities. The patient demonstrated a successful range of motion in the elbow flexors, extensors, and wrist flexors and extensors. Capillary refill was less than 1 second when the digit was examined, but active digital flexion or extension was limited. The patient passed her hearing and critical congenital heart disease screen, and metabolic newborn screens were within normal limits. She was discharged home at two days of age with plans for close follow-up with a pediatrician, a cleft palate clinic, and an orthopedic and plastic surgery team.

Laboratory results indicated elevated levels of lactate dehydrogenase at 241 IU/l and creatinophosphokinase at 507 IU/l, with no increase in white blood cell count.

input.case_report: The patient is a seven-year-old Sri Lankan girl. In January 2012, she experienced an upper respiratory infection with fever, followed by yellow discoloration of the eyes and pale stools after one week. Blood tests revealed elevated total bilirubin (19.5 mg/dl, direct 9.4 mg/dl, and indirect 10.1 mg/dl), transaminases (AST 1216 U/L, ALT 1022 U/L), and alkaline phosphatase (524 U/L). An abdominal scan indicated an irregular liver surface with an inhomogeneous structure, consistent with chronic liver disease. In March, the child was admitted to our unit: her general condition was good, with a weight of 12.7 kg (25th percentile) and a height of 92.2 cm (25th percentile); she exhibited yellow discoloration of the eyes and hepatomegaly. Laboratory tests showed elevated ESR (51 mm/h), LDH (1567 U/L), alkaline phosphatase (713 U/L), transaminases (AST 1391 U/L, ALT 1405 U/L), γ-GT (294 U/L), and IgG (2110 mg/dl). Liver kidney antibodies (LKM) were strongly positive (1:640). Other tests, including Anti-native DNA antibodies, ASMA, AMA, EMA, and tTG, were normal.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 49-year-old male presented with fever and shortness of breath, accompanied by a productive cough, sputum, and peripheral cyanosis. His medication regimen included 12.5 mg of thiazide and 40 mg of nifedipine daily for hypertension. He had no history of pulmonary or cardiac conditions and did not smoke or consume alcohol. Over the past three months, he experienced a weight loss of approximately 6 kg. Upon admission, his vital signs were as follows: temperature of 38.3℃, respiratory rate of 20 breaths per minute, pulse rate of 84 beats per minute, and blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg. Lung auscultation revealed coarse breath sounds with crackles in the right lower lobe, and electrocardiography showed a right bundle branch block. Laboratory results included a white blood cell count of 13,770/mm3 (neutrophils 85.7%, lymphocytes 9.5%, monocytes 4.6%, eosinophils 0.1%, basophils 0.1%), hemoglobin level of 12.0 g/dL, platelet count of 195,000/mm3, and lactate dehydrogenase level of 589 U/L. Arterial blood gas analysis on room air showed a PaO2 of 54.2 mmHg, PaCO2 of 30 mmHg, and O2 saturation of 90.7%. Chest radiography revealed diffuse bilateral reticular infiltrates in both lung fields. High-resolution computed tomography (CT) scans demonstrated diffuse ground-glass opacity, ill-defined nodules with a clustered appearance, focal radiolucent lesions indicative of mosaic perfusion in both lungs, and subpleural consolidations in both lower lobes. The patient was administered 100 mg of hydrocortisone intravenously twice daily, along with 1 g of ceftizoxime and 500 mg of amikacin intravenously twice and once daily, respectively. A bronchoscopy was performed on the second day of hospitalization, during which oxygen saturation was monitored via pulse oximetry. Despite supplemental oxygen via nasal prongs, frequent hypoxemia (SaO2 < 85%) was observed, leading to the discontinuation of the bronchoscopy after collecting a bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) sample from the right middle lobe.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 5-month-old girl was brought to our department due to abdominal distension. She was born at full-term via vaginal delivery and was bottle-fed. Her parents, who are not related, reported that her abdomen began to swell gradually starting at 2 months old. She had been treated intermittently at a local hospital with total parenteral nutrition (TPN) and drainage of ascitic fluid, but these measures were not effective. Her current body weight is 5.0 kg, up from a birth weight of 3.0 kg. Physical examination revealed significant abdominal distension leading to severe respiratory distress, without noticeable edema. Erythema spots (nevus flammeus) were observed on her chest, upper limbs, hypogastrium, and a large area of her back since birth. Additionally, demarcated grayish-blue hyperpigmentations (Mongolian spots) were noted on her shoulder, back, buttocks, and lower limbs, which remained unchanged and asymptomatic. Ophthalmic examination revealed bilateral blue spots on the sclera and conjunctiva. Her extremities were symmetrical with no signs of hypertrophy or atrophy in the soft tissues. Abdominal ultrasonography showed normal findings except for the presence of significant ascites. Spinal ultrasonography was performed to rule out tethered cord and showed no abnormalities in the spine. Biochemical examination results were as follows: total protein, 47.5 g/L; albumin, 30 g/L; and white cell count, 4.5 × 10^9 cells/L with 12.2% lymphocytes.

input.case_report: A 60-year-old male resident of Rosheim, Alsace (northeast France) presented with a nodule on the left orbital floor on 8 July 2008. He had recently traveled to Casamance, southern Senegal, in April 2008. Physical examination revealed a palpable nodular lesion on the left orbital floor. Magnetic resonance imaging identified an inflammatory nodular lesion. The patient underwent antibiotic and steroid therapy, which did not alleviate his symptoms. The nodule was surgically excised in September 2008, measuring 5 cm at its longest dimension.

input.case_report: A seven-month-old male infant, born to parents who are second-degree relatives, presented with developmental delay. The parents reported that the child did not smile or react to stimuli and was unable to eat solid food by six months. The child achieved head control at four months. The parents also noted poor weight gain and a history of neonatal seizures. The antenatal period was marked by gestational diabetes managed with insulin, and the mother had two previous early miscarriages. The child was born late pre-term via cesarean delivery with a birth weight of 3.9 kg. Post-delivery, the infant experienced respiratory distress, hypocalcemia, and hypoglycemia, necessitating a five-day stay in the neonatal intensive care unit (NICU). Echocardiography in the NICU revealed a small patent ductus arteriosus, patent foramen ovale, and diabetic hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. The patient is the youngest of three children, with two healthy older siblings. There is no family history of mental, developmental, or neurocutaneous disorders. Physical examination revealed several dysmorphic features, including coarse facial features, a long philtrum, a thin upper lip, a broad forehead, and long toes. Neurological examination showed hypotonia in all four limbs, with normal muscle strength and reflexes. Skin examination was unremarkable, and the patient had normal male genitalia and no organomegaly. Routine blood tests, including full blood count, liver function tests, thyroid function tests, 17-hydroxyprogesterone, and urinalysis, were all within normal limits. Karyotyping confirmed a genetically male child (46XY).

input.case_report: A 51-year-old female presented with generalized edema and weakness persisting for 5 months. Her blood pressure was recorded at 170/100 mmHg, while other vital signs were stable. Physical examination revealed obesity, a moon face, buffalo hump, and purplish abdominal striae. A 2×2 cm hard, fixed mass was palpated in the left supraclavicular lymph node. Pitting edema was noted in both lower legs. Laboratory results showed a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL and a platelet count of 239,000/mm3. Serum levels of AST, ALT, ALP, and LDH were 50 U/L, 140 U/L, 131 U/L, and 1,065 U/L, respectively. Renal function tests, including serum electrolytes, were within normal limits except for a potassium level of 2.5 mEq/L. Fasting glucose was 472 mg/dL, hemoglobin A1C was 11.8%, and spot urine glucose was above 1,000 mg/dL, with no proteinuria detected. Imaging studies, including chest X-ray and chest CT scan, revealed a 12×10 cm well-defined, partially capsulated, and lobulated homogeneous mass in the anterior mediastinum, with no evidence of mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathies.

input.case_report: A 16-year-old female came to the clinic for evaluation of primary amenorrhea. Puberty began at age 12. She did not report headaches, vision changes, or galactorrhea. On examination, she was significantly tall for her age and genetic potential (99.9th percentile for height, +4 SD for age; mid-parental height at the 50th percentile). Her growth chart showed her height was in the 90th percentile until age 9, followed by an accelerated growth velocity of >99th percentile. There were no previous medical concerns or evaluations for her tall stature. She was obese and had acanthosis nigricans. She had coarse facial features and large hands and feet. She was at Tanner stage IV for breast development and pubic hair. Bone age was consistent with chronological age and complete growth plate fusion. An ultrasound of the pelvis showed a prepubescent uterus. Baseline pituitary function labs indicated elevated GH levels (insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) 1162 ng/mL (normal level, 108-548)) and low levels of LH and estradiol (luteinizing hormone (LH) 0.1 IU/L (0.0-26.4), estradiol 11 pg/ml (2-266)). The rest of her pituitary function was normal.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 47-year-old male with no known comorbidities reported experiencing low back pain for 2 months. The pain began gradually, was primarily located on the right side of the lower back, had a dull aching quality, and progressively worsened. It did not radiate and was exacerbated by movement but alleviated by rest and analgesics. There was no history of early morning stiffness, numbness, tingling in the lower limbs, joint pain or swelling, trauma, or physical strain before the onset of pain. Additionally, there was no bowel or bladder incontinence, recent fever, unexplained weight loss, congenital spine abnormalities, or family history of low back pain. Physical examination showed nontender, subcentimetric, bilateral supraclavicular lymph nodes. Local examination revealed tenderness over the lumbosacral spine and right sacroiliac joint. Schober's test indicated lumbar flexion greater than 5 cm. Systemic examination was unremarkable. Laboratory tests showed a normal hemogram and liver function tests, with an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 42 mm/h and serum creatinine of 1.4 mg/dl. MRI of the lumbosacral spine displayed altered signal intensity, hyperintense on T2-weighted/STIR images and hypointense on T1-weighted images, in bilateral iliac blades, bilateral acetabuli, and adjacent soft tissue of the right iliac blade. Multiple focal areas of altered signal intensity were also noted in the lumbosacral vertebrae. Further laboratory tests, including urine Bence–Jones proteins and serum electrophoresis, were normal. Bone marrow aspiration showed no plasma cell dyscrasia. Tumor markers such as alpha-fetoprotein, CA125, CA19-9, beta-human chorionic gonadotropin, and prostate-specific antigen were within normal limits, but serum lactate dehydrogenase levels were elevated at 570 IU/L. A whole-body 18F-FDG PET-CT scan revealed an FDG avid soft tissue density lesion in the abdomen along the midline from LV-1 to LV-3, likely a conglomerate lymph nodal mass. An ill-defined isodense round to oval hypermetabolic lesion was observed predominantly involving the right gluteus medius and right iliacus muscles adjacent to the right ilium near the sacroiliac joint, with a small area of cortical erosion on the lateral aspect of the right ilium. Mildly FDG avid lymphadenopathy was noted in the right cervical, right supraclavicular, right axillary, and left inguinal lymph nodes. FDG avid lesions were also seen in multiple lumbosacral vertebrae, right ilium, left ilium near the sacroiliac joint, the roof of the right acetabulum, and the head of the left femur, with no obvious CT abnormalities.

input.case_report: A nine-month-old male with no significant past medical history presented with two months of increasing fussiness and two days of vomiting. Physical examination showed no abnormalities, and there was no history of excessive urination or thirst. Brain MRI revealed a 3.2 × 3.0 × 2.6 cm lobular mass in the sella-suprasellar region with strong contrast enhancement. The mass was separate from the optic chiasm, had eroded the dorsum sella, and extended into the subfrontal area. Intralesional hemorrhage was also observed. Spine MRI showed no signs of metastases. Endocrinology tests confirmed central adrenal insufficiency, necessitating maintenance and stress doses of hydrocortisone. A craniotomy was performed, and the tumor was found to be adherent to surrounding critical structures, resulting in a subtotal resection.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 39-year-old female presented to the emergency department with facial swelling and difficulty breathing for one week. She had no prior history of heart or major vein procedures. Initial neck CT imaging showed extensive clotting in the left brachiocephalic vein (LBCV) and superior vena cava (SVC) extending into the right atrium. Laboratory tests for antinuclear antibody, anticardiolipin antibody, lupus coagulant, protein C and protein S activities, antithrombin III, factor V Leiden mutation, human leukocyte antigen-B51, and skin pathergy test were all normal. Despite receiving anticoagulation therapy with 60 mg of enoxaparin twice daily for 10 days, the SVC thrombosis did not decrease. Further chest CT imaging revealed an anterior mediastinal mass invading the LBCV and SVC. A subsequent PET-CT scan showed a well-defined hypermetabolic lesion with a maximum standardized uptake value (SUVmax) of 6.0 along the LBCV extending into the proximal SVC.

input.case_report: A 50-year-old male was admitted to Hyogo Medical College Hospital in mid-July 2011 for evaluation and management of an abnormal visual field. A tumor was identified in the sinonasal cavity, extending beyond its original location (Kadish stage C), and chemoradiotherapy was administered. At that time, there were no signs or symptoms of Cushing’s syndrome. By 2012, the primary lesion showed a tendency to decrease in size, but by 2014, it had extended to the opposite side of the sinonasal cavity (Kadish stage C). In January 2015, the patient developed hypokalemia, hyperglycemia, and decreased eosinophil and granulocyte counts. He was referred to the Department of Diabetes, Endocrinology, and Metabolism for further evaluation of hypokalemia in mid-July 2015. From 2011 to 2014, serum potassium and glucose levels, as well as blood eosinophil counts, were nearly normal. The patient had a history of social drinking and smoking 40 cigarettes a day for 20 years before admission. His body weight was recorded as 67.0 kg in 2012, 86.0 kg in 2014, and 73.6 kg at the time of admission. His blood pressure was 165/109 mmHg, and his pulse rate was 80 beats per minute. Physical examination revealed a Cushingoid appearance, including moon facies, truncal obesity, purpura, and muscle atrophy of the bilateral lower extremities. Laboratory findings on admission indicated a slightly increased total white blood cell count, a markedly increased neutrophil count (both segmented neutrophils and bands), and an eosinophil count of 0%. The fasting plasma glucose level was elevated, and the serum potassium concentration was low. Serum magnesium levels and renal function test results were normal. Arterial blood gas analysis showed metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation. Both the fractional excretion of potassium and the transtubular potassium gradient were elevated.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 37-year-old white man with a history of hepatitis B arrived at the emergency department exhibiting symptoms of cough, low-grade fever, and night sweats. He reported no intravenous drug use and worked as a bartender. Initial radiological examinations of the abdomen and chest showed no abnormalities. Laboratory tests revealed the following results: SGOT: 71 U/liter, SGPT: 61 U/liter, LDH: 931 U/liter, CRP: 28.33 mg/dl, white blood cell count of 12,900/μL with 88.2% polymorphonuclear cells, hematocrit at 35%, and hemoglobin at 11.8 g/dl. Serologic tests for HIV and hepatitis C were negative, as was the Mantoux test. The next day, the patient experienced difficulty breathing, and auscultation indicated reduced breath sounds and vocal resonance in the right hemithorax. A chest X-ray identified a pleural effusion in the right hemithorax. CT scans of the chest and abdomen confirmed the presence of a pleural effusion and a liver abscess measuring 14 × 9 × 7 cm, predominantly in the right lobe. An echogram of the liver revealed septae within the abscess, leading to the decision to avoid echo- or CT-guided drainage. Surgery was planned for the following day, but the patient's condition worsened that evening, presenting with fever (oral temperature 39.2°C), low blood pressure, rapid breathing (32 breaths/minute), rapid heart rate (110 beats/minute), and signs of an acute abdomen. Emergency surgery was performed, revealing approximately 2200 ml of free fluid and debris in the peritoneal cavity. A rupture was found in the hepatic abscess at segment VIII, and surgical drainage was conducted, placing two wide drains (32G) in the hepatic cavity. Additionally, a thoracic drain tube (Büllau) was inserted, which produced only yellowish reactive fluid. The patient was admitted to the ICU for 14 days, followed by another 14 days in the department. Cultures of the pus were negative for microorganisms.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 24-year-old Caucasian woman, gravida 1, para 1 at 34 weeks of gestation, presented to the obstetric emergency department. She had no prior medical history and her pregnancy had been progressing well. One week before hospital admission, she experienced progressive weight gain and bilateral lower leg edema. She also reported shortness of breath, chest tightness, loss of appetite, and fatigue. Her blood pressure was within normal limits. Physical examination revealed hyperreflexia, hepatosplenomegaly, and diffuse lymphadenopathy with nodes larger than one centimeter. Laboratory tests showed moderate thrombocytopenia (138 × 10^9/L), hyponatremia (125 mmol/L), elevated C-reactive protein (194 mg/L), and increased uric acid (607 µmol/L). Renal function was normal, although the patient was oliguric. A dipstick urine test was negative for proteinuria. Three days after admission, a cesarean section was performed due to poor tolerance, resulting in the birth of a girl weighing 2,650 grams with Apgar scores of 3 and 9. The surgery was uneventful, but ascites was noted. In the following days, chest pain and hyperreflexia resolved, but edema worsened, leading to a total weight gain of 10 kg. The patient developed respiratory distress, continued to be oliguric despite diuretic treatment, and experienced worsening fever and thrombocytopenia (50 × 10^9/L) accompanied by nosebleeds.

The entire tongue was noted to be enlarged and firm, with multiple nodules extending from the tip to the base and further down. No enlargement of regional lymph nodes was observed. Laboratory tests showed anemia, elevated serum creatinine, and increased kappa light chain levels, while other parameters remained within normal ranges.

input.case_report: A 45-year-old male patient presented with a 10-day history of high fever reaching 40°C, accompanied by chills, excessive sweating, significant fatigue, and left abdominal pain. He did not report symptoms of headache, vomiting, cough, expectoration, chest tightness, or shortness of breath. Consequently, he was transferred to a specialized infectious disease hospital for further evaluation and treatment. Laboratory results from a complete blood count revealed leukopenia and mild anemia, with white blood cell count at 3.42×10^12/L, red blood cell count at 93 g/L, hemoglobin at 286×10^9/L. CNV analysis through host sequence suggested high-risk liver issues. Blood biochemical tests showed elevated serum Lactate Dehydrogenase (398 U/L) and decreased serum cholinesterase (1,640 U/L), while triglycerides were at 2.41 mmol/L. Antigen tests indicated positive results for HBsAg, HBeAb, and HBcAb, but PCR tests were negative for HBV DNA. Bone marrow cytology showed decreased bone marrow hyperplasia with a normal proportion of granulocytes, but a significant increase in eosinophils and erythrocytes. Flow cytometry analysis of the bone marrow indicated T cells at 88.3% of lymphocytes, NK cells at 2.4%, and B cells at 2.7%, with a CD4:CD8 ratio of 1.20, showing no significant immune abnormalities. Echocardiograms performed on the second and eighth day after admission revealed vegetation measuring 10×5 mm on the margin of the aortic valve, specifically the non-coronary valve. Ultrasound examination of superficial lymph nodes showed mild enlargement of the left supraclavicular lymph node (12 mm × 9 mm and 9 mm × 5 mm). The liver and spleen were markedly enlarged, and multiple wedge-shaped areas near the edge of the spleen parenchyma appeared as hypo-enhancing or non-enhancing regions, indicative of splenic infarction. Multiple blood and bone marrow cultures returned negative results.

input.case_report: A 56-year-old African-American man with a history of hypertension and chronic hepatitis C virus infection came to the emergency room with a three-day history of flu-like symptoms, including fever, muscle aches, headache, and decreased appetite. His vital signs were blood pressure 204/104 mmHg, pulse 90 beats/minute, respiratory rate 14 breaths/minute, and peak temperature 105.2°F. Physical examination revealed restlessness, confusion, and neck rigidity. There was no papilledema. He was unable to follow commands, and mild right-sided weakness was observed. A computed tomography scan of the brain showed a subtle, low-density signal in the caudate nuclei bilaterally. Serum laboratory values were normal except for mild leukocytosis, with a total white blood cell count of 10,400/mm3 comprising 82% neutrophils, 9% monocytes, and 8% lymphocytes. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) examination showed a white blood cell count of 29/mm3, comprising 81% lymphocytes, 15% neutrophils, and 4% monocytes, with protein at 87 mg/dl and glucose at 63 mg/dl. CSF Gram staining, acid-fast bacilli staining, a meningitis screen (Neisseria meningitidis, group B Streptococcus, Streptococcus pneumoniae, and Haemophilus influenzae type b), a cryptococcal antigen test, and a Venereal Disease Research Laboratory test were all negative. He was admitted to the intensive care unit in a stupor and treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin, ceftriaxone, ampicillin, and tetracycline) and intravenous acyclovir. Laboratory evaluations for herpes simplex virus (HSV)-1 and HSV-2, tuberculosis (TB), cytomegalovirus (CMV), and WNV were sent. On day two, an electroencephalogram showed diffuse slowing without seizure activity. A brain MRI performed on the same day demonstrated markedly enlarged caudate nuclei and increased intensity of caudate, lenticular nuclei, and the thalamus on T2-weighted images. T1-weighted images showed a subtle, low-intensity signal within the lesions, and the lesions did not enhance following gadolinium infusion. He continued to have fever and confusion with fluctuating mental status. Polymerase chain reaction studies of his CSF for HSV, TB, and CMV were negative. His blood, urine, and CSF cultures were negative. By day seven, his mental status had improved to the point that he was oriented to himself and could state his age. From hospital days eight to 16, he showed slow improvement with phases of intermittent confusion. On day 13, a brain MRI scan showed decreased edema in the basal ganglia and thalamus compared to prior MRI scans. On day 19, he was fully oriented, and his mental status appeared to have returned to baseline.

input.case_report: A 69-year-old male with liver cirrhosis (Child-Pugh A) due to toxic-nutritive causes was found to have hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) in September 2017. This was confirmed through two imaging techniques and elevated α-fetoprotein (AFP) levels. Two suspicious areas were identified in both liver lobes. The lesion in segment VII was treated with microwave ablation, and the one in segment IVb/V with irreversible electroporation (NanoKnife®) in November 2017. A follow-up CT scan in December 2017 showed necrosis of the treated liver lesions and portal vein thrombosis. Four months post-treatment, there was significant disease progression. Liver MRI revealed multilocular HCC in segments V, VI, VII, and VIII, with AFP levels rising dramatically to 28,000 IU/ml. Surgical hemi-hepatectomy was not feasible, so treatment with sorafenib, a multikinase inhibitor, began in March 2018 as per the Barcelona-Clinic Liver Cancer criteria. One month later, the patient visited the Department of Dermatology at the University Hospital Linz. Examination revealed almost symmetrical papulopustular plaques on the dorsum of both hands, lateral forearms, and most prominently on the lower legs. The soles of the feet showed desquamations with blistering. The nails were not affected. Considering the ongoing HCC treatment, the oral tyrosine kinase inhibitor was discontinued, and topical corticosteroids were prescribed. The dermatitis improved, but several psoriasiform, hyperkeratotic, yellowish scaly eruptions and partly verrucous plaques on the palm and dorsum of both hands persisted. There was no history of other dermatological conditions, including psoriasis vulgaris or atopic eczema.

input.case_report: A 39-year-old Japanese woman with a history of one miscarriage at 6 weeks' gestation became pregnant while on anticoagulation treatment with 2500 U danaparoid sodium and 100 mg aspirin per day from 4 weeks' gestation at Nagoya City University Hospital. Her blood pressure was 121/78 mmHg, and laboratory studies showed anti-β2-glycoprotein I (β2GPI) 9.4 U/mL (normal range < 1.9), aPTT 30%, LA by aPTT 30 seconds, LA by RVVT 1.47, and urinary protein 2.0 g/day at 17 weeks of gestation. She was hospitalized at 21 weeks of gestation due to severe edema and acute renal dysfunction, and treatment with 40 mg prednisolone daily started at 22 weeks of gestation. She developed epigastric pain and vomiting. Thrombocytopenia, hemolytic anemia, liver and renal dysfunction, and an elevated level of C-reactive protein were observed.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 37-year-old female ophthalmologist presented in November 2017 with advanced kidney disease. Her medical history dates back to October 2012 when she reported irregular menstrual cycles to her primary physician. Laboratory tests at that time revealed nephrotic range proteinuria (7650 mg/24 h) with bland urine sediment and normal serum creatinine. She was initially treated with steroids. Subsequently, she experienced diarrhea, abdominal pain, and nausea, leading to the discontinuation of steroids and a native kidney biopsy was performed. Light microscopy of the biopsy showed 20 glomeruli, with 6 globally sclerosed, 2 with segmental sclerosis and adhesion to Bowman's capsule, and 12 with mild focal increase in mesangial matrix. Mild tubulointerstitial fibrosis was also noted. Immunofluorescence microscopy results were negative. Electron microscopy revealed diffuse effacement of podocyte foot processes, uniform capillary loop basement membrane thickness, segmental capillary loop obliteration with lipid, hyaline, and foam cell accumulation associated with capsular adhesion, and overlying epithelial cell hyperplasia. No capillary loop immune complex-like dense deposits were observed. The mesangial matrix was minimally expanded without hypercellularity or electron dense deposits. The biopsy findings were consistent with focal and segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS). She was restarted on steroids at 1 mg/kg/day and mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) 500 mg twice daily was added. However, there was no response, and all immunosuppressive drugs were discontinued in December 2014. Over the next three years, her creatinine levels progressively increased, and by November 2017, her eGFR was 10 mL/min, and ultrasound showed small and echogenic kidneys. She was advised to undergo a pre-emptive transplant. Her brother volunteered as a potential donor, but initial evaluation revealed that he also had proteinuria.

input.case_report: An 85-year-old Iranian, Caucasian woman presented with low-grade fever, loss of appetite, abdominal discomfort, and a sense of fullness in her left abdomen for the past six months.
