input.case_report: A 40-year-old male patient was referred to the General Surgery Unit of Complexo Hospitalar Santa Casa with multiple structural deformities, including fractures with poor bone healing and shortening of vertebrae and limbs, which had developed over the past three years. Imaging revealed bone destruction, fractures, and a salt and pepper skull effect. These fractures led to thoracic deformities due to spine and rib alterations. Initially, the patient experienced pain while walking, which progressed to an inability to walk without assistance. Within one year, the patient suffered an acute myocardial infarction without known primary risk factors. A transthoracic echocardiogram showed left ventricular dysfunction (grade 2), diffuse hypokinesia, diastolic dysfunction (grade 2), minimal mitral regurgitation, and a 35% ejection fraction. Pulmonary function deteriorated due to the reduced thoracic volume. Laboratory tests were conducted to investigate the cause of the structural deformities. Radiographic exams showed multiple limb fractures, decreased axial skeleton, and loss of bone mass. CT scans revealed a diffuse reduction in bone attenuation coefficient and vertebral depression, suggesting osteoporosis, but no visceral abnormalities were found. Clinical examination did not reveal additional anatomical changes to identify the initial site of the condition. Whole-body MRI identified a nodular image in the left inguinoscrotal area (2.5 × 2.1 × 30 cm3) with heterogeneous signal intensity in T1 and T2 and high contrast enhancement near the left Long Adductor Muscle, without invasion, and associated lymph nodes. Fracture signs were also found in the inferior ischiopubic area, bilaterally, as well as in the pubic and femur regions, suggesting pathologic fractures. Whole-body PET/CT detected several bone deformities due to a metabolic disorder and a nodular lesion in the subcutaneous tissue of the left inguinal region at the base of the scrotum, measuring 2.5 × 1.6 cm2 (SUV 3.8). Bone scintigraphy with technetium showed diffuse uptake in multiple joints (arms, legs, ribs, spine, head, and pelvis) consistent with bone wear and the same scrotal lesion.

input.case_report: A 55-year-old woman presented to the orthopaedic department with a 2-year history of mild right thigh pain that worsened with prolonged walking or running. The right hip and knee exhibited normal movement with a full range of motion, and there was no lymphadenopathy in the groin or popliteal fossa. Family history included a sister with congenital hypothyroidism and a brother who died at age 18 from neuroblastoma of the kidney. Initial laboratory investigations showed: Total WCC 2.7, Neutrophils 0.9–1.2 × 10^9/L, monocyte count 0.19, Platelet count 283 × 10^9/L, ESR 37, CRP 1. Urea, Creatinine, Electrolytes, TFT, B12, Folate, and Ferritin were all within normal ranges. Follow-up FBC at 3 months showed normalized neutrophil levels. LDH was 186 u/L. CT imaging demonstrated a lytic lesion with surrounding sclerosis eroding the cortex of the femoral diaphysis. MRI detailed a well-marginated, low T1 and high T2 signal, eccentrically located and marginally expansile lesion with homogeneous consistency.

input.case_report: A 63-year-old male was admitted to the inpatient unit due to severe pain and persistent skin lesions that had not responded to treatment over the past three years. The patient presented with widespread lesions at various stages, including several large, tense bullous lesions that were difficult to rupture, particularly on the feet. Additionally, there were numerous painful, unroofed ulcerative lesions covering more than 90% of his body surface area and oral mucosa. These unroofed bullae drained blood-tinged and purulent fluid, causing significant discomfort during movement. The most affected areas were the axillary folds and inner arms, which were covered in ulcerated bullae. The patient experienced significant mental distress due to the deterioration in his quality of life as his condition progressed. His clinical course was complicated by inconsistent and unclear coordination of care. Over the past three years, he had been treated with 200 mg of hydroxychloroquine daily, along with previous use of methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil, and a daily 2.5 mg dose of prednisone. The cutaneous and oral lesions had not been well controlled for the past year and had significantly worsened in the past three weeks. Previous skin punch biopsies were unable to identify the underlying pathology. Emergency department punch biopsy records reported an undifferentiated blister with extensive granulation tissue. Three weeks prior to presentation, the patient’s prednisone therapy was increased to 20 mg daily without improvement. Upon admission, he was started on empiric antibiotic therapy due to the extent of exposed mucosa but was deescalated to observation after active infection was ruled out. The patient was started on 60 mg IV methylprednisolone twice daily. Hydroxychloroquine was discontinued per rheumatology’s recommendations due to its potential to contribute to flares. Recommendations from the wound care team and dermatology included cleansing unroofed bullas with normal saline and dressing with low-adherence contact layers. Moisture-wicking fabrics and adaptable patient bedding with control over pressure points were also recommended.

Intraoperatively, it was noted that the contents of the cyst resembled tuberculosis. Postoperatively, the patient was administered isoniazid and rifapentine.

input.case_report: A 14-year-old male presented with paroxysmal kinesigenic dyskinesia and was referred to our hospital. Previous treatments for enterocolitis or anorexia nervosa at another clinic had not been effective. Upon admission, the patient appeared pale and fatigued. Physical examination revealed a non-distended abdomen with a palpable, tender mass in the lower abdomen. The abdominal pain was described as colicky, intermittent, and occasionally severe. Although there was no diarrhea or “currant jelly” bloody stools, the stool tested positive for occult blood. Stool culture results were unremarkable, and laboratory tests, including lactate dehydrogenase and soluble interleukin-2 receptor antibodies, were within normal limits, except for a slight elevation in C-reactive protein (0.6 mg/dL). An HIV antibody test was negative. Abdominal ultrasonography showed a target sign in the lower abdomen, indicative of intussusception. Enhanced computed tomography (CT) confirmed ileocolic intussusception with a suspicious mass. A previous abdominal CT from 5 weeks earlier also suggested signs of ileocolic intussusception, aligning with the onset of symptoms. Hydrostatic reduction with gastrografin® was attempted but unsuccessful, leading to an emergent operation. Probe laparoscopy revealed ileocolic intussusception, serous ascites, and a markedly edematous ileum and mesentery. Laparoscopic reduction was attempted but failed, necessitating conversion to open surgery. The intussusception could not be reduced even with the Hutchinson maneuver. The mesentery was notably edematous, thickened, and hemorrhagic. Consequently, the mesentery was dissected along with the intestine, and an ileocecal resection with functional end-to-end anastomosis was performed. The patient's postoperative course was uneventful, and cytological examination of the drained ascites fluid was negative for malignancy.

Despite poor weight gain, her systemic examination was normal. At 13 months, she underwent laryngoscopy and a modified barium swallow study, both of which were unremarkable. An esophagram performed a month later was described as “difficult and limited but normal” (Fig. A). Various feeding therapies and acid suppression therapy prescribed by her pediatrician did not significantly improve her symptoms. The first two procedures were unremarkable, but the endoscopy revealed a benign proximal esophageal stricture with an internal diameter of approximately 4–5 mm and 1 mm in length (Fig. ). The stricture was dilated using 18 to 21 Fr bougie dilators without complications such as bleeding or perforation. On the day of the procedures, a physical exam noted new pallor, prompting a complete blood count (CBC). The CBC showed severe pancytopenia (white blood count 2.4 k/µL, hemoglobin 8 g/dL, hematocrit 23%, platelets 22 k/µL). Chart review indicated that the patient had been monitored for mild anemia and borderline thrombocytopenia since her one-year well-child exam (hemoglobin 10.5 g/dL, hematocrit 29.3%, platelets 140 k/µL). A follow-up blood count a month before presentation showed mild improvement in anemia (hemoglobin 11.1 g/dL, hematocrit 30.7%) but low platelets at 46 k/µL, initially attributed to a laboratory error due to sample clotting. Following the endoscopic procedure, the patient was discharged home, and no blood products were administered. She was urgently referred to pediatric hematology.

input.case_report: A 60-year-old, right-handed male presented with involuntary shaking movements of the right-sided extremities. During an episode, a personal note written by the patient with his right hand showed intermittent slight trembling. He remained alert throughout, with no other neurological symptoms observed. Initial neurological examination revealed no abnormalities, including no signs of cerebellar ataxia or extrapyramidal symptoms. One year later, the patient experienced sudden weakness in the right extremities, verbal disturbance, and sensory deficit on the right side of his body. Initial MRI and MR angiography again revealed no responsible lesion, and an EEG showed no evidence of epilepsy. The patient was admitted and started on antiplatelet therapy. The tremor did not recur after hospitalization. On day 5, the right-sided paralysis worsened. MRI showed dilatation of vessels from deep medullary veins to superficial medullary veins in the left frontal/parietal lobe and right frontal/parietal lobe, with white matter changes surrounding these dilated veins. No changes were observed in the bilateral basal ganglia, thalamus, brainstem, or cerebellum.

Cytological examination of the bronchial washing fluid showed no significant result. Despite several courses of antibiotics, there were no clinical and radiological improvements observed. However, purulent phlegm and hemoptysis persisted intermittently during and after treatment. Results of cytology, AFB stain, and polymerase chain reaction for Mycobacterium tuberculosis were negative, as were cultures for bacteria, fungi, and mycobacteria. No eggs were observed in sputum or BAL fluid. Bronchoscopic biopsy showed chronic inflammation with a giant cell. Follow-up complete blood cell count showed no peripheral blood eosinophilia. The patient was re-treated with anti-TB medication, showing transient aggravation at 2 months and subsequent improvement at 3 months after re-treatment. Despite this, the patient suffered recurrent hemoptysis, and chest CT scans revealed newly developed cavitary nodules within the right upper lobe at 5 months after re-treatment.

Her stool tested positive for occult blood, but her complete blood count, coagulation profile, extended clotting study, and thrombophilia screen were all within normal limits. Antenatal screenings for TORCH, HIV, Treponema, and Hepatitis were normal. The patient was born in good condition with Apgar scores of 9 at one minute and 10 at five minutes. Her birth weight was 1250 g, below the 3rd percentile for her age and sex. She had an imperforate anus with a rectovaginal fistula, a heart murmur, and subsequent echocardiography confirmed a perimembranous ventricular septal defect and a persistent foramen ovale. An X-ray of her spine showed hemivertebrae in the sacrococcygeal region. Surgical correction of the imperforate anus and rectovaginal fistula was performed within the first few days of life. She spent four weeks in the neonatal unit before being discharged home. She was doing well at home until she presented at two and a half years of age with vomiting of blood. Her growth trajectory has consistently remained below the 3rd percentile.

input.case_report: A 54-year-old man was admitted to National Cancer Center/Cancer Hospital, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College in August 2011 with a two-month history of positive fecal occult blood test. There were no polyps on histology, and there was no family history of familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP)/colorectal cancer; in addition, there was a rise in carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA). Abdomino-pelvic computed tomography (CT) did not show any metastasis to the lymph nodes or distant organs. Other preoperative auxiliary examinations, including chest radiography, electrocardiogram, and blood tests, did not show any obvious contraindications for surgery. Thus, the patient underwent successful laparoscopic right hemicolectomy and postoperative recovery was uneventful. Postoperative pathology showed that a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma had invaded the superficial muscularis. No lymph node metastasis was found (0/22), so the pathological staging was pT2N0M0. After hospital discharge, the patient was asked to visit the doctors every three months for follow-up. At 12-month follow-up, a CT scan revealed new multiple masses in the abdominal cavity: the largest was 1.5 cm in short diameter. Two months later, another CT scan showed that the masses had grown and that the largest one was 2.3 cm in short diameter. Ultrasonography (USG) of the whole abdomen showed that the largest mass was ~3 cm and located on the left side of the umbilicus. Positron emission tomography-computed tomography (PET-CT) showed multiple nodules located in the abdominal cavity, and that the largest one was 2.6 cm in short diameter (SUVmax=1.9). Following a multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion, we considered that excisional biopsy surgery was the best treatment option. The patient was thus readmitted to our institution in October 2012 with a two-month history of newly detected abdominal masses. Exploratory laparotomy was performed with resection of the middle ileum segment, and end-to-end anastomosis between the proximal ileum segment and the distal ileum was performed. The surgical specimen consisted of an angulated segment of the small intestine, which measured ~25 cm in length, and in the mesentery, showed several masses of varying sizes, the largest of which measured 3.5×3×3 cm.

input.case_report: A 31-year-old female patient was referred to our general surgical unit with a large, painless mass in the right breast, which had been gradually increasing in size over the past 10 years. During her childhood, she had normal growth until around the age of 5 years, when her growth began to slow. At the same age, she noticed small nodular growths on the fingers of her right hand and angular deformities in her right upper and lower limbs. She experienced a fracture of her right tibia from a minor fall and another fracture in the shoulder region a year later. Her limb deformities worsened throughout her childhood and adolescence. Despite these issues, she was in good general health, right-handed, and able to perform daily tasks and write. She could walk with a limp and reported minimal impact on her work as a subsistence farmer. She had no history of oral contraceptive use and experienced menarche at 14 years old. There was no family history of breast neoplasms or similar limb conditions among her 5 siblings, parents, or grandparents. Her drug, family, and psychosocial histories were otherwise non-contributory. Physical examination revealed a well-circumscribed, irregularly shaped, firm, mobile breast mass approximately 15 cm in transverse diameter and 20 cm in superior/inferior diameter, replacing more than 90% of the normal breast tissue. There was associated solitary, mobile ipsilateral lymphadenopathy. The breast had a superolateral fungating ulcer that was friable, surrounded by inflammatory skin changes, and caused downward nipple displacement. The patient was of short stature (149.5 cm) with unilateral bony abnormalities on the right side, including irregular angular deformities and limb shortening in the upper and lower limbs, leading to a limp favoring the right side. She also had shortening and deformity of the thumb and index finger with associated nodularity. There were no facial dysmorphic changes. Additionally, she had a pedunculated, soft, hyperpigmented mass on the anterolateral abdominal wall, measuring 2 × 2 cm.

input.case_report: A 59-year-old self-employed male with a 10-year history of high blood pressure (HBP), managed with two anti-hypertensive medications but with irregular compliance and poor control, presented with various symptoms. His family history includes a mother with early-onset dementia and chronic depression, a brother with schizophrenia, two brothers with alcohol abuse, and a daughter with severe depression. The patient reported psychomotor slowing, sporadic dysphagia, irritability, emotional lability, anxiety, decreased hearing, vertigo, paraesthesia, and lower limb pain over the past year. He was admitted to the Emergency Department (ED) following a generalized tonic-clonic seizure with faecal and urinary incontinence. On admission, he had an altered level of consciousness (Glasgow Coma Scale score of 9), conjugated gaze deviation to the right, flaccid plegia of all four limbs, and an indifferent right plantar reflex, with normal reflex on the left foot, and high blood pressure. Arterial blood gases indicated moderate mixed acidaemia (pH 7.13, pCO2 83.3mmHg, HCO3-28.0mmol/L), respiratory predominance, hyperlacticaemia (lactate 6.2mmol/L), and hypokalaemia. Cranial computed tomography (CT) with angiography revealed cerebral atrophy inconsistent with his age and leukoaraiosis, without acute ischemic or haemorrhagic lesions or thrombi. During his ED stay, he experienced a second seizure controlled with diazepam and was started on levetiracetam 1g. Despite initial improvement, he had a third seizure, leading to orotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation under continuous sedation with propofol and midazolam. Lumbar puncture showed unchanged cerebrospinal fluid cytochemistry and bacteriology, with no relevant findings for neurotropic viruses. He was transferred to the Intensive Care Unit (ICU), where he had no further seizures during sedation weaning, completed ventilatory weaning, and was extubated without issues. He remained hemodynamically stable but required three anti-hypertensive drugs for HBP control. Transferred to the Medicine ward, he maintained clinical stability with slight speech slowing and distal upper limb atrophy, though muscle strength was preserved. Neuropsychological assessment revealed articulatory effort with anomalous pauses, slowed recall, difficulty in executive tasks, and decreased information processing speed. Brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) identified multiple hyperintense areas in periventricular and subcortical frontoparietal white matter on T2 FLAIR sequences, along with lacunar nucleus-capsular and bilateral thalamic sequelae lesions. Electroencephalogram (EEG) showed left temporal slowing without paroxysms, indicating left temporal regional dysfunction, but no epileptiform activity. Transcranial Doppler echo documented increased pulsatility indices indicating arterial inelasticity. Doppler echocardiography of neck vessels showed diffuse carotid atheromatous without hemodynamic repercussions. Transthoracic echocardiogram and 24-hour Holter monitoring showed no relevant changes. Laboratory tests highlighted hypercholesterolemia and hypertriglyceridemia. Prothrombotic and reversible dementia studies revealed no changes. Secondary HBP causes were investigated due to difficulty in controlling hypertension, with main causes excluded. Renal ultrasound showed no renal artery stenosis or parenchymal alterations. Given the cognitive decline with mood alterations over one year and MRI findings, genetic causes were investigated, including genes NOTCH3 (CADASIL), HTRA1, and Mitochondrial Encephalopathy Lactic Acidosis Stroke-like episodes (MELAS), which were excluded.

input.case_report: A 40-year-old male with no prior health issues was referred to an ophthalmologist 12 hours after sustaining ocular trauma from fireworks to his left eye. The right eye was unaffected. Examination revealed a lacerated and perforated cornea with a severe traumatic cataract in the left eye, which could not be sutured. A positive Seidel test was noted. The anterior chamber was shallow, and visual acuity was limited to hand motion. The patient consented to the publication of this case report and images. A bandage contact lens was applied, and the patient was prescribed tobramycin 0.3% and moxifloxacin 0.5% for 4 weeks until the corneal injury healed and the Seidel test was negative. These medications were provided as free samples due to the patient's financial constraints. Six months later, the patient underwent optical penetrating keratoplasty without complications. On the 40th postoperative day, intraocular pressure was 28 mmHg, and brinzolamide 1% was prescribed. Four months post-operation, the cornea was clear, and two loose sutures were removed. In the seventh postoperative month, phacoemulsification was performed, and an intraocular lens was implanted. On the first postoperative day, the eye was calm with an uncorrected visual acuity of 20/400. However, by the fifth postoperative day, the patient reported mild pain, redness, tearing, and decreased vision. An infiltrate was observed around a loose suture on the graft’s temporal interface, measuring 2×1 mm and affecting up to 50% of the stromal depth. The patient also had a fibrin pupillary membrane and visual acuity of light perception. Due to the lack of a local laboratory, the infiltrate was not cultured. The patient was empirically prescribed Vigamox® hourly, moxifloxacin 0.5% + dexamethasone 0.1% four times daily, and nepafenac 0.1% and brimonidine tartrate 0.2% + timolol 0.5% twice daily. On the fifth day of infection, symptoms improved, but the infiltrate remained unchanged. The treatment regimen was maintained. On the 50th day of infection, the patient experienced pain and a marked decrease in visual acuity. The infiltrate had increased to 3.6×2.5 mm, with central pigmentation and feathery margins, a shallow anterior chamber, and a positive Seidel test. A bandage contact lens was applied, and Vigadexa® was discontinued to observe the infiltrate’s progression without antibiotics or steroids. By the 60th day of infection, there was a marked increase in temporal limbal injection, a 2 mm hypopyon, and increased infiltrate density at the recipient border. Ultrasonography showed no changes in the posterior segment. The patient was prescribed hourly natamycin 5%, daily fluconazole 150 mg, and twice-daily Vigadexa®. On the 70th day of infection, the infiltrate size increased to 5.4×3.8 mm, with a hypopyon occupying half of the anterior chamber. The treatment regimen was maintained. By the 85th day of infection, there was a significant reduction in redness and pain, a decrease in infiltrate density and size to 4.6×2.5 mm, a negative Seidel test, and no hypopyon. Visual acuity of light perception persisted. The bandage contact lens was removed, and the eyedrops were maintained. Four months into the infection, the patient reported no pain, but the infiltrate remained unchanged. Natamycin was reduced to three times daily, and fluconazole was maintained at 150 mg/day. The patient was referred to an ophthalmic reference hospital due to the lack of improvement. At the hospital, empirical therapy with topical vancomycin 5% hourly, Vigamox® four times daily, and fluorometholone acetate 0.1% once daily was initiated, and antifungal medication was discontinued. Nineteen days later, the initial infiltrate size decreased to 3.8×2.5 mm, but a new infiltrate measuring 2.5×1.7 mm was observed in the anterior stromal third of the graft, forming a white opacity with needle-like aspects and no surrounding inflammation or edema. Edema was present in the inferior third of the graft. Six months after the infection onset, the infiltrate size and density increased to 6.8×4.5 mm, leading to the discontinuation of medications. An antifungal regimen of natamycin 5% hourly and fluconazole 150 mg/day was restarted. Eight months into the infection, the injury remained unchanged, with a positive Seidel test and a shallow anterior chamber. Another therapeutic keratoplasty was indicated to remove the peripheral corneal infiltrate. This keratoplasty was performed off-center, with a deviation toward the temporal limbus. The excised cornea was sent for culture.

input.case_report: A 33-year-old female patient visited the prosthodontic department requesting a denture due to missing posterior teeth. She reported that none of her posterior teeth had ever been extracted. Imaging revealed the presence of all her missing teeth, and she was referred to the orthodontic department for further evaluation. Her medical history was unremarkable, with no history of radiation or unusual drug therapy. Her family history was also normal, with no relatives having unerupted teeth. Laboratory tests, including thyroid function tests, parathormone levels, hormone assays, serum calcium, and phosphorus levels, were all within normal limits. Intra-oral examination showed 2 retained deciduous teeth (54 and 55). The only permanent teeth present were 11, 12, 21, and all 4 lower incisors. The erupted teeth had normal anatomy. There was grade 1 mobility of the lower incisors in a buccolingual direction. A torus palatinus was observed, along with bulging related to the unerupted teeth, without signs of pain or crackling. A class III incisor relationship with a deep bite and bilateral posterior open-bite were noted. Examination of mandibular movements showed no displacements, excluding pseudo class III malocclusion.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 14-month-old African American boy was brought to the hospital in winter due to hand stiffening that began on the day of presentation. His mother observed that his hands were stiff and bent inward while he was playing. He did not exhibit fever, seizure-like activity, upper respiratory symptoms, vomiting, or diarrhea. His medical history included atopic dermatitis since 3 months of age and a recent diagnosis of iron deficiency anemia. His diet, in addition to breast milk, consisted mainly of rice, chicken, potatoes, and broccoli, which are low in vitamin D. His diet was further restricted due to presumed food allergies to dairy products, eggs, wheat, soy, almonds, and peanuts, based on elevated allergen-specific IgE levels. He was on iron supplements but no other medications. There was no developmental regression, but he was unable to walk or pull himself up to stand. He could crawl but fatigued quickly. He was born full-term, with the pregnancy complicated by maternal gestational diabetes, hypothyroidism, and vitamin D deficiency. Physical examination showed an alert child with normal vital signs and no dysmorphic features. His weight was 10.5 kg (60th percentile) and his length was 77 cm (25th percentile). He had diffuse xerosis on his cheeks, trunk, and flexural areas. His hands were stiff and contracted, but there were no visible skeletal deformities such as joint widening or genu varum. He exhibited mild hypotonia; he could sit unassisted and stand briefly with support but could not walk. His reflexes were normal with no fasciculations, and the rest of the examination was unremarkable.

input.case_report: A 28-year-old woman with multiple sensitivities to food and inhalant allergens presented with intermittent difficulty swallowing, a sensation of a foreign body in the throat, itchiness in the oral cavity after consuming certain foods, heartburn, and prolonged chewing time. Initial symptoms included sneezing, nasal congestion, nasal itching, and watery eyes, first noticed five years ago during the spring. There were no allergic symptoms during childhood or in her family medical history. Symptoms of allergic rhinitis and conjunctivitis increased over the past three years, occurring from March to June. Over the past two years, from March to June, she experienced a dry cough, wheezing, and shortness of breath. She was diagnosed with allergic asthma approximately two years ago. These symptoms only occurred in the spring. Spirometry results showed normal lung ventilation parameters (FEV1% VCmax-88%—9th percentile, FEV1-117%) and after a diastolic test (FEV1% VCmax-90%-14th percentile, FEV1-122%), with no significant increase in FEV1 or FVCex after administering an antispasmodic. The patient experienced itchy lips, tongue, and mouth burning after eating hazelnuts, almonds, apples, plums, and pears, which she avoids due to these symptoms. However, she can consume apples and plums after heat treatment without issues. During symptom exacerbation, she uses inhaled corticosteroids, and her asthma is well controlled. She also suffered from chronic, recurrent contact dermatitis after exposure to metal parts of clothing and jewelry. There was no family history of allergic or gastroenterological diseases. A skin prick test showed positive results for birch tree, alder, hazel, and rye pollen, as well as house dust mites. A patch test revealed positive results for nickel sulfate. Specific IgE tests showed positive results for house dust mites (class 4) and birch tree (class 2), but negative for all food allergens. Skin prick tests for food allergens and an IgE food panel on blood serum showed positive results for hazelnuts and anise, while the rest were negative. Specific IgE showed class 1 for milk, class 2 for beef, and the remaining were negative. Physical examination revealed obesity (BMI 30 kg/m2), multiple pustules, and excoriations on the skin. Laboratory findings, including a complete blood count, liver and kidney function tests, and coagulogram, were unremarkable.

input.case_report: A 40-year-old male with a history of congenital left leg-length discrepancy of about three-fourth inches and multiple corrective surgeries for this condition, as well as severe bilateral hip osteoarthritis with a right hip resurfacing surgery, presents with chronic thoracic back pain and restricted neck motion. He reports intermittent back pain since his 20s and neck pain starting approximately five to seven years ago. The pain averages three on a scale of 10, ranging from one to eight. He is unable to bend down to pick up objects, and his neck pain and stiffness significantly limit his activities. The patient describes his pain as muscular, especially in the neck, and mentions an inability to touch his toes since high school. Stiffness lasts all day, with minimal relief from hot showers in the morning. He has progressively worsening difficulty sleeping. Four years ago, he could practice yoga, which significantly helped his pain. Various nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agents (NSAIDs) provided no significant improvement, and muscle relaxers caused increased drowsiness and weakness. Massage therapy markedly alleviates his neck pain, while physical therapy and chiropractic services were unimpressive. He denies bowel/bladder incontinence, saddle anesthesia, fever, shortness of breath, rectal bleeding, tingling, or numbness. He also denies recent trauma or injury, gastrointestinal symptoms, psoriasis, or eye inflammation. The patient has not had his human leukocyte antigen (HLA)-B27 marker checked. He reports being in good health otherwise. He mentions that people have told him he walks like his father, who had difficulty walking with his head up due to neck stiffness and used a back brace. His brother has Crohn's disease, but he is unaware of his brother's HLA-B27 marker status. He has two healthy children. The patient drinks about one alcoholic beverage a day and smokes a cigar once a month. Examination reveals normal vital signs. He is conscious, cooperative, and well-oriented. He has diaphragmatic respiration with clear lungs on auscultation. Cardiovascular examination is normal, and the abdomen is soft and non-tender without organomegaly. Extremity examination shows a left leg-length discrepancy of about three-fourth inches, with the left leg shorter, markedly atrophic, and weaker compared to the right leg. There is no range of motion in the left ankle joint, and a left foot deformity is present. The patient has only five percent range of motion in his neck. Neurological examination shows a stiff posture and limping gait without touching his left heel to the floor. He first walks on his toes and then supports himself on his left heel. He cannot bend down to touch his toes. Previous and recent laboratory test results, including complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, and thyroid-stimulating hormone levels, were normal.

input.case_report: A 15-year-old male presented with significant fatigue following intense exercise since childhood. His medical history included allergic rhinitis. Upon entering high school, he frequently felt unwell, particularly in the mornings, leading to missed classes. He was initially prescribed amezinium metilsulfate 10 mg/day for orthostatic dysregulation, but symptoms persisted. Eight days later, his medication was switched to etilefrine 5 mg/day. His fatigue worsened, and he was found vomiting and unresponsive after collapsing in the bathroom on the eighth night following the medication change. He was taken to the hospital by his family. In the emergency room, he exhibited mild consciousness disturbance (Glasgow Coma Scale: E4V4M6) and complained of right lower abdominal pain. Laboratory tests (blood and urine), whole-body computed tomography, and head magnetic resonance imaging showed no abnormalities (serum creatinine level 1.0 mg/dL, uric acid level 7.2 mg/dL). His consciousness and abdominal pain improved the next day, but his blood pressure increased from 100/60 to 180/80 mm Hg, and his serum creatinine level rose from 1.0 to 5.5 mg/dL over five days of admission. He was then transferred to our institution for further treatment. Upon admission, he was fully conscious and alert. His body temperature was 37.2°C, blood pressure was 161/98 mm Hg, heart rate was 83 beats/min, and respiratory rate was 17 breaths/min. His height was 174 cm, and his body weight was 54 kg. Physical examination revealed no signs of dehydration, rash, or other abnormalities in the neck, chest, abdomen, or extremities. He had been taking loratadine 10 mg/day for allergic rhinitis for several months, which, along with etilefrine, had been discontinued upon admission to the previous hospital. There was no family history of kidney dysfunction, and he reported no episodes of intense exercise other than daily commuting by bicycle to school. He did not consume alcohol, smoke, or use illegal drugs.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 68-year-old male with a 20-year history of hypertension and a 10-year history of chronic bronchitis was admitted due to dyspnea and intermittent headaches lasting approximately one month. Physical examination identified a 7 × 6 cm non-tender neck mass. There were no signs of hyperthyroidism or hoarseness, and neurological examination showed no positive findings. Blood pressure was 110/70 mmHg. Laboratory results on admission included erythrocytes at 3.94 (4.3–5.8 × 10^12/L), hemoglobin at 124 (130–175 g/L), serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) at 480.0 (15–240 U/L), hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase at 311.0 (50–220 U/L), total bilirubin at 30.1 (5.1–20 μmol/L), direct bilirubin at 11.6 (0.1–10 μmol/L), and indirect bilirubin at 18.1 (3–15 μmol/L). Thyroid function tests were normal. A chest CT scan showed bronchitis and bilateral lung bullae. Head and neck CT scans revealed a 1.5 cm nodule in the cerebellum with a slight space-occupying effect and a thyroid neoplasm invading the anterior superior mediastinum. The left thyroid lobe had an enlarged mass measuring 5.8 × 4.7 × 8.4 cm with scattered calcified lesions, flaky necrosis areas, and inhomogeneous enhancement. The right thyroid lobe had a 1.3 × 1.2 cm nodule without calcification or significant enhancement. The trachea was displaced to the right and narrowed by the mass. No abnormal lymph nodes were visible in the neck. Thyroid color Doppler ultrasound showed a giant hypoechoic mass (7.1 × 4.7 × 2.4 cm) in the left thyroid lobe and a hybrid echo-mass (1.3 × 1.2 × 1.0 cm) in the right lobe, with significant blood flow in the lower density mass. The patient was transferred to the thyroid breast surgery department, where a thyroid mass resection was performed. Intraoperatively, the thyroid was markedly enlarged, and the trachea was displaced to the right. Multiple masses were found in both thyroid lobes, the largest being 10.0 × 6.0 × 5.0 cm in the left lobe, with 2/5 of it behind the sternum. A smaller mass (1.8 × 1.2 × 1.0 cm) was located in the middle of the right lobe. Both masses were removed and sent for frozen section examination, which revealed nodular goiter and focal atypical hyperplasia in the left lobe. The patient then underwent a total thyroidectomy with central neck area lymph node dissection. The thyroid had a complete packing membrane and a dark red appearance. The left lobe mass (8.1 × 5.0 × 4.5 cm) was solid with medium hard features and uneven texture, while the right lobe mass (1.8 × 1.2 × 1.0 cm) was gelatinous, soft, and uniform in texture. Both masses had complete capsules. No enlarged lymph nodes were detected in the central neck area. Twenty paraffin blocks were prepared from the thyroidectomy specimen, with 15 from the left lobe and 5 from the right.

input.case_report: A 46-year-old female smoker presented to the emergency department with a four-day history of cough producing blood-tinged sputum. She reported no additional symptoms. Her medical history was unremarkable, and she denied exposure to toxic substances, handling dogs, or rearing sheep. Physical examination showed no specific abnormalities, and there were no signs of respiratory or cardiovascular dysfunction. Lung auscultation indicated a mild decrease in breath sound intensity, while heart examination was normal with no extra sounds or murmurs. The electrocardiogram (ECG) showed no notable findings. The Mantoux tuberculin skin test was negative. Routine laboratory tests, including liver and kidney function tests, serum proteins, and urinalysis, were normal. The erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) was 12 mm/hr. Sputum specimens tested negative for mycobacteria and other pathogens in both smear and culture. Serological tests for cancer and viruses were negative, but an indirect immunohemagglutination test was positive.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 21-year-old woman was referred to the cardiology department. Ten years prior, she had been taking Ezetimibe 10 mg once a day. Her total cholesterol was 6.1 mmol/L (236 mg/dL), high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol was 2.2 mmol/L (85 mg/dL), and low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol was 2.8 mmol/L (108 mg/dL). The patient wished to discontinue the medication. Upon visiting the outpatient clinic, she had not taken Ezetimibe for 2 weeks. Her total cholesterol increased to 7.0 mmol/L (271 mg/dL) and LDL to 4.0 mmol/L (155 mg/dL). The patient was a non-smoker with a normal body mass index. Her blood pressure was normal, and she had no signs of diabetes [haemoglobin A1c 23 mmol/mol (4.3%)]. Clinical examination revealed a xanthoma on the left hand. She had no complaints of chest pain or shortness of breath. At age 4, the patient had an excrescence at the intergluteal cleft, which was surgically removed. At the same age, she was admitted to the hospital with joint pain and presented with several joint-related tumors, some of which were surgically removed. One was sent for pathological examination and interpreted as granulomatous inflammation. She was treated with methotrexate for 1.5 years. Despite the treatment, her symptoms progressed. At that time, her blood cholesterol was measured for the first time. Total cholesterol was 9.8 mmol/L (379 mg/dL), HDL 7.8 mmol/L (302 mg/dL), and LDL 7.6 mmol/L (294 mg/dL). The previous biopsy was revised and found compatible with a xanthoma. Genetic testing was negative for any known mutations in the LDL receptor and apolipoprotein B genes. Her parents were cousins, and neither had hypercholesterolemia. Her mother’s father had a stroke at age 58 and a coronary artery bypass at age 65. His brother had an acute myocardial infarction at 50 years of age. The patient’s brother was apparently healthy. She changed her diet, which had some effect on her cholesterol levels and symptoms. She was started on Simvastatin 10 mg/day, which had little effect despite a later increase in dose to 20 mg/day and consequently a change to Atorvastatin 80 mg/day. Shortly after, she was admitted to the hospital with nausea and vomiting. She was icteric and had impaired liver function with alanine transaminase of ∼1600 U/L. Ultrasound of the abdomen revealed an enlarged spleen (length of 13.7 cm). The treatment was discontinued. After the hospitalization, treatment with Ezetimibe 10 mg/day was initiated. Eventually, her liver function normalized completely. After 5 years of mild thrombocytopenia on routine blood samples, the patient was referred to the hematology department with a platelet count of 139 × 109/L and reticulocytosis (reticulocyte count of 114 × 109/L). She had no symptoms of hematological disorders. On clinical examination, there was no lymphadenopathy. Hemoglobin was normal (8.4 mmol/L), haptoglobin was slightly decreased (0.30 g/L), free hemoglobin was mildly increased (4 µmol/L), and direct antiglobulin test was negative. The cause of thrombocytopenia was not identified, and the patient was prescribed folic acid treatment for 3 months, which had no effect on her platelet count (122 × 109/L after treatment).

input.case_report: A 43-year-old male with no significant medical history arrived at the emergency department experiencing fevers and cough for eight days. He mentioned repairing water pipes in his basement two weeks earlier but had no other environmental exposures, travel history, or contact with sick individuals. He did not consume alcohol, use tobacco, or illicit drugs. Upon examination, his temperature was 36.5 °C, blood pressure was 157/95 mmHg, pulse rate was 88 bpm, respiratory rate was 21 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation was 91% on room air. Laboratory results showed a leukocyte count of 7.7 cells/mm3 (normal range: 4.2-11.0 cells/mm3) and a procalcitonin level of 0.09 ng/mL (normal: <0.10 ng/mL). Arterial blood gas analysis revealed a partial pressure of oxygen at 67 mmHg (normal range: 83-108 mmHg). Blood cultures taken at admission were negative, and urine antigen tests for Streptococcus pneumoniae and Legionella were also negative. A chest X-ray indicated an infiltrate in the right middle lobe.

input.case_report: A 4-year-old boy arrived at the emergency department experiencing his third episode of severe nosebleed that day, which had been ongoing for about an hour. He had another spontaneous episode approximately three weeks earlier, which, like the first two on this presentation, resolved at home with pressure application. He had no history of runny nose, cough, fever, or excessive nose picking. The patient was born after a full-term pregnancy with no complications except for a nuchal cord at delivery. Petechiae around the face and neck at birth were attributed to the nuchal cord. As an infant, he experienced gastrointestinal reflux, and during severe episodes, petechiae were noted on his face, neck, and trunk. At his 12-month well-child exam, chest bruises were observed in a pattern consistent with car seat pressure, prompting a laboratory workup for coagulation disorders. The lab results showed mild microcytic anemia, normal platelet count, and normal prothrombin time (PT) and activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT). He was started on iron supplementation for anemia, attributed to low iron levels in breastmilk, as his mother had stopped taking prenatal vitamins. Frequent nosebleeds began around age two, lasting up to two hours, from either or both nostrils, occurring as often as weekly but never requiring medical or surgical intervention. Upon arrival at the emergency department, the patient appeared anxious and slightly pale. Physical examination revealed active bleeding from both nostrils and scattered petechiae around the eyes, chest, and extremities. Prolonged external compression of the nostrils with a vasoconstrictive spray was insufficient to control the bleeding, necessitating anterior and posterior nasal packing. These measures controlled the bleeding only briefly before it resumed, prompting the emergency physician to seek assistance from an otolaryngologist. Laboratory studies showed no anomalies except for microcytic anemia, with normal PT/PTT and platelet count. The otolaryngologist achieved hemostasis using silver nitrate cautery and applied petroleum jelly to stabilize the clot and prevent further bleeding. The patient was referred to a pediatric hematologist for further evaluation.

input.case_report: Patient 1 is a 37-year-old male who first visited our clinic at the age of 8 years in 1989. His clinical records up to 1992 were not retained, but a brief summary indicated that he had nystagmus since birth. He was previously noted to have retinitis pigmentosa and hyperopia through clinical examination at the age of 7 at another clinic. Upon his first visit to our clinic, his eyes did not shift in the primary position, and hyperopia and color blindness were detected. Fundus examination was grossly normal. According to the oldest detailed clinical data from 1992, his eye position indicated orthophoria, but horizontal nystagmus was evident, and he reported photophobia. His best corrected decimal visual acuity (BCVA) was 0.2 (S + 6.5/C-3.5/170°) in the right eye and 0.1 (S + 6.0/C-2.5/10°) in the left eye. Fundus examination revealed bisymmetrical inferior focal retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) mottling (Fig. a, b; photo taken in 1993).

input.case_report: A 10-month-old male patient was admitted due to ongoing hepatitis and jaundice. He had no significant perinatal, familial, or past medical history, except for treatment for bronchitis at another hospital two weeks before admission. During his bronchitis treatment, elevated aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) levels were noted. Despite intact liver function initially, he progressed to impending hepatic failure and was referred to the hepatology department. Initial laboratory results indicated acute liver failure with AST at 194 IU/L, ALT at 69 IU/L, total bilirubin at 7.4 mg/dL, direct bilirubin at 6.3 mg/dL, ammonia at 57 µg/dL, and gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase at 101 IU/L. Blood gas analysis showed a normal pH of 7.40 and bicarbonate level of 25.8 mmol/L, with a slightly elevated lactate level of 2.4 mmol/L. Various evaluations, including viral, autoimmune, endocrinologic, and genetic studies for ATP7B and ABCB11, returned normal results, and imaging studies revealed no significant findings. The lactate-to-pyruvate ratio was 82.5, and a plasma amino acid assay indicated the possibility of lactic acidosis. His mental status declined, becoming drowsy with hyperammonemia, and he underwent a living donor liver transplantation on day 24 of admission. Pathological examination of the liver showed cirrhosis with significant fatty change, cholestasis, ballooning degeneration, and marked ductular proliferation, suggesting a metabolic condition. The liver transplantation was successful, and the patient was discharged with normal neurological function.

input.case_report: A previously healthy 9-month-old boy, current with his immunizations, presented with swelling and firmness in his right thigh, first noticed by his mother during a bath. The induration increased over a week without fever, pain, or trauma. Parents mentioned routine immunizations in both thighs three months prior. In Dubai, children receive the Bacille Calmette-Guérin (BCG) vaccine at birth in the deltoid, and diphtheria/tetanus/acellular pertussis (DTaP), inactivated polio (IPV), hemophilus influenza B (HiB), and hepatitis B (HepB) vaccines at 2, 4, and 6 months in the thighs. Initial blood tests showed a CRP of 30 mg/L (normal: <6 mg/L), ESR of 29 mm/hr (normal: <10 mm/hr), and normal complete blood count, AST, ALT, and alkaline phosphatase levels. Ultrasound of the right thigh revealed a 6 cm × 4 cm deep abscess in the anterolateral aspect. The abscess was drained under general anesthesia, yielding 15 ml of purulent material, and the patient was discharged on oral cefuroxime pending culture results. Gram stain of the abscess fluid showed gram-positive cocci and gram-negative rods, but bacterial cultures were negative. After completing 10 days of oral cefuroxime post-drainage without improvement in thigh induration or swelling, he was referred for pediatric infectious diseases consultation. In the clinic, he appeared well with normal vital signs, weight at the 50th percentile, and length at the 90th percentile for age. The anterolateral right thigh was indurated and swollen, without erythema or tenderness, and a 3 cm incisional scar was healing well. He did not bear weight on his legs when placed in a standing position and did not crawl. There was no systemic lymphadenopathy, and the rest of his physical examination was normal. An MRI scan with contrast, obtained after abscess drainage and completion of oral antibiotic therapy, showed extensive inflammatory granulation tissue in the right lateral thigh compartment, with no evidence of underlying femoral osteomyelitis. The involved muscles, particularly the vastus intermedius and vastus lateralis, exhibited significant fatty replacement.

input.case_report: A 72-year-old male presented with chest pain. Initial vital signs were blood pressure of 139/84 mmHg, heart rate of 70/min, respiratory rate of 20/min, and SpO2 of 98% on ambient air. Initial ECG revealed marked ST-segment elevation in leads V1-6, I, and aVL, and ST depression in leads II, III, and aVF. Emergency coronary angiography revealed total occlusion of the proximal left anterior descending artery at the ostium, which was successfully resolved by emergency percutaneous coronary intervention and stent placement. The patient’s clinical course was complicated by mild congestive heart failure that was responsive to intravenous carperitide (human atrial natriuretic peptide: 0.0125 microgram/kg/min for one day) and furosemide 20 mg twice a day for four days, followed by oral azosemide 30 mg and spironolactone 25 mg daily. An echocardiogram showed antero-apical hypokinesis with an ejection fraction of 40%. The patient remained stable up to day three, with improvement in ST-segment elevation.

input.case_report: A 71-year-old man was admitted to the hospital for strict glycemic control in June 2020. He had been treated for diabetes mellitus, hepatic hemochromatosis, and bronchial asthma for 15 years. Previously, he had undergone a left adrenalectomy for adrenal neuroma. His glycemic control had deteriorated since April. A pancreatic tumor was identified by ultrasound, enhanced CT, and MRI. At the request of his family, he was referred to our hospital in August 2020. On physical examination, no tumor mass was palpable in his abdomen, except for surgical scars. The results of the laboratory investigations were as follows: RBC, 4.5 × 10^6/μl; hemoglobin, 9.6 g/dl (normal range: 13.0–17.6); WBC, 3.1 × 10^3/μl (4.5–9.0 × 10^3); platelet count, 195 × 10^3/μl; Fe, 23 μg/dl (49–219); amylase, 89 U/l; trypsin, 1171 ng/ml (100–550); elastase-1, 959 ng/dl (0–300). The results of the liver function tests were within normal limits. The levels of tumor markers were as follows: carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), 3.0 ng/ml; carbohydrate antigen 19-9 (CA19-9), 20 U/ml; Span-1, 39 U/ml (< 30); NCC-ST-439, 1.2 U/ml (< 4.5); AFP, 2 ng/ml. His pulmonary function was impaired, as shown by the following results: FVC, 3.7 l; FEV1, 1.61 l; FEV1%, 43.5%. The CT scan showed a slightly enhanced mass, approximately 27 mm in size, in the tail of the pancreas, with dilatation of the distal main pancreatic duct (MPD). Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) showed mild dilatation (3.5 mm) of the caudal MPD up to the pancreas neck. The distal MPD was dilated to a diameter of 7 mm. In addition, a cystic lesion, a suspected branch duct intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (BD-IPMN), was seen in the pancreatic neck. Thin-slice multi-detector row computed tomography (MDCT) in our hospital showed a homogenous slightly enhanced mass with expanded extrapancreatic progression and moderate dilatation of the distal MPD. However, this tumor showed no infiltration pattern to the retropancreatic tissue and splenic vein. Downstream tumor progression in the MPD was observed up to the left side of the portal vein. The EUS findings were as follows: a hypoechoic mass lesion with a lobulated structure approximately 3 cm in size was found in the tail of the pancreas; dilatation of the upstream MPD and a tumor thrombus in the MPD downstream from the main tumor was observed.

input.case_report: A 9-year-old girl with fair skin visited the Pediatric Dental Clinic at 'Universidade Veiga de Almeida,' accompanied by her guardian, due to concerns about misaligned teeth. Consent was obtained for this case report. Her medical history indicated a normal birth with no systemic issues or other significant findings. A thorough intraoral clinical examination revealed that she was in the mixed dentition stage, with the absence of three first permanent molars, a deviation from the midline, bilateral posterior crossbite, and a deep palate.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 68-year-old male patient was admitted to the hospital in December 2008 with symptoms of fever, cough, right-sided chest pain, and swelling in both lower extremities. The patient had a history of schistosomiasis 20 years prior, which resolved with medication. His medical and family histories were otherwise unremarkable, and he was not on any medications at the time. Upon admission, his body temperature was 38°C and his pulse was 105 bpm. Physical examination revealed bibasal lung crackles and bilateral lower extremity pitting edema. Heart sounds were normal without a murmur. There were no dermatologic manifestations, nasopharyngeal abnormalities, or inflamed joints. Initial laboratory tests showed a urine sediment protein of 1+ without blood cells or casts. Hemoglobin was 8.3 mg/dL. Biochemistry profile indicated a normal serum creatinine level and an albumin level of 3.1 mg/dL. Chest X-ray showed right lower lobe pneumonia with minimal pleural effusion on both sides. The patient was treated with antibiotics and showed significant improvement, leading to discharge. One year later, the patient returned with intermittent bilateral lower extremity edema and skin rashes. On admission, his vitals were normal except for mild blood pressure elevation (150/80 mmHg). Physical examination revealed bilateral pitting edema of the lower extremities, extensive purpura on both legs, hepatosplenomegaly, and a holosystolic cardiac murmur (Levine 2/6). Laboratory data included a leukocyte count of 3.01 × 103/μL (62.1% neutrophils, 31.5% lymphocytes, 5.5% monocytes, and 0.6% eosinophils), hemoglobin of 5.8 mg/dL (microcytic hypochromic anemia), platelets of 59 × 103/μL, total protein of 8.1 g/dL, albumin of 3.1 g/dL, globulin of 5.0 g/dL, serum creatinine of 2.16 mg/dL, and C-reactive protein of 2.62 mg/L. The erythrocyte sedimentation rate was 140 mm/h. Urinary sediment analysis showed protein of 2+ and occult blood of 3+. Rheumatoid factor was 36.2 IU/mL (reference range, <20 IU/mL), and antinuclear antibody was positive with a titer of 1:1000. Serum complement 3 level was low at 32.1 mg/dL (reference range, 80–160 mg/dL), while total complement and complement 4 levels were within normal limits. Indirect immunofluorescence c-ANCA was positive, with a c-ANCA/PR3 titer significantly elevated at 102 ELISA units (reference range, <15), while p-ANCA/MPO was negative. Viral serology for HIV, hepatitis B, and hepatitis C were all unremarkable. A test for cryoglobulin was positive. Serum protein electrophoresis showed increased γ-globulin (48.1%) with mildly increased IgG (4035 mg/dL) and IgM (352 mg/dL). Chest CT revealed multiple intraparenchymal and subpleural nodules in both lungs (solid or with a ground-glass character), hilar lymphadenopathy with pleural thickening, and minimal bilateral pleural effusion. At a corrected gestational age of 42 weeks, the infant weighed 3890 g (P40), measured 53 cm in length (P51), and had a head circumference of 35.5 cm (P53). Follow-up evaluations were conducted every 12 months. At 4 years old, the child's height was 106.4 cm, and weight was 15.8 kg. input.case_report: A 14-year-old female with no significant medical or surgical history was admitted with abdominal pain, vomiting, and fever for one day. Laboratory results revealed a haemoglobin level of 10 g/dl and a total white cell count of 21,600 cells/mm3 with 91% neutrophils; other investigations were within normal limits. She underwent an emergency laparotomy under general anaesthesia. Pre-medication included intravenous injections of glycopyrrolate 0.2 mg, midazolam 1 mg, pantoprazole 40 mg, and ondansetron 4 mg. Anaesthesia was induced with propofol 50 mg following an injection of pentazocine 30 mg. Tracheal intubation was facilitated with atracurium 25 mg IV. Anaesthesia was maintained with a mixture of oxygen and nitrous oxide (50:50), 0.8 to 1% isoflurane, and intermittent boluses of atracurium. Ventilation was adjusted to maintain an end-tidal carbon dioxide value of 30–35 mm Hg. Physiological parameters, including blood pressure, electrocardiogram, oxygen saturation, end-tidal carbon dioxide, and airway pressures, remained stable throughout the procedure. At the end of the surgery, neuromuscular blockade was reversed with injections of neostigmine and glycopyrrolate. The trachea was extubated after the return of adequate motor power and consciousness. The patient was transferred to the post-operative Intensive Care Unit with stable haemodynamics for further monitoring, with maintenance normal saline IV infusion at 75 ml/h. The patient remained haemodynamically stable for the first 14 hours post-operatively. Subsequently, she experienced dizziness upon semi-recumbent positioning, with a systolic blood pressure of 80 mm Hg. A fluid challenge of 150 ml was administered, and the IV fluid infusion rate was increased to 150 ml/h for the next 2 hours, resolving the postural symptoms. She was then moved to the surgical ward. The next morning, upon sitting up in bed (semi-recumbent), she experienced another episode of dizziness. Examination revealed that she was conscious and oriented with normal motor and sensory reflexes and a blood pressure of 100/65 mm Hg. Laboratory results showed a haemoglobin level of 9.5 g%, and 1 unit of blood was transfused. The following day, after multiple episodes of dizziness, a neurological consultation was obtained, and a two-dimensional echocardiography and CT brain with contrast were recommended. An ENT surgeon advised vestibular exercises and prescribed tablet cinnarizine 25 mg. The two-dimensional echocardiography was normal. The CT brain revealed an ill-defined mass in the left cerebellum with calcification, causing a midline mass effect on the 4th ventricle with mild hydrocephalus.

input.case_report: A 36-year-old French Canadian male presented with a 3-week history of left knee arthritis. Left knee radiograph indicated significant joint effusion. Synovial fluid analysis revealed 161,500 white blood cells/ml (91% neutrophils) and low glucose level at 0.8 mmol/L. The patient received treatment but showed no clinical or biochemical improvement. Extensive microbiological investigations were negative. High dose oral prednisone was initiated, resulting in marked clinical improvement and a decrease in CRP from 271 to 106 mg/L within forty-eight hours. Autoimmune disease work-up showed positive HLA-B27 antigen and rheumatoid factor at 160 IU/mL; antinuclear antibodies, extractable nuclear antibodies, anti-double stranded DNA, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptides were negative, with normal complement levels. Fluid analysis showed a non-purulent exudate with low pH, undetectable glucose level, and negative gram stain. Pleural white blood cells were elevated at 7300/uL, but differential count was unavailable. Antibiotics were resumed. Prednisone dose was reduced to 25 mg daily. Chest tube drainage was unsuccessful, leading to pleural thrombolysis, which resulted in massive hemothorax and respiratory distress, necessitating urgent thoracotomy and decortication. Pleural bacterial, fungal, and mycobacterial cultures were negative. A 4-week course of antibiotics was administered, and moderate dose prednisone was maintained. Upon discharge, knee synovitis and pleural effusion had resolved, but CRP remained elevated at 111 mg/L. Immunology consultation was sought. Family history was unremarkable, with no consanguinity. Personal history included first admission at 2 years old for aseptic meningitis, with concurrent diagnoses of sensorineural hearing loss, macrocephaly, and global developmental delay, along with elevated inflammatory markers, specifically leukocytosis with eosinophilia. Eosinophilia persisted above 1500/mm3, despite the absence of atopic manifestations, negative allergy tests, and normal immunoglobulin E level. A maculopapular rash was noted on the abdomen between 7 and 8 years, with no recurrence. Cerebral CT scan showed diffuse atrophy. Recurrent episodes of hyperthermia and lower extremity arthralgia led to a diagnosis of juvenile idiopathic arthritis, treated with aspirin, resulting in transient symptom improvement. Persistent inflammation and failure to thrive prompted upper and lower gastrointestinal endoscopies, revealing eosinophilic enterocolitis. Prednisone was administered for several months, partially reversing growth retardation despite persistent inflammatory markers. At 6 years old, the patient developed pericarditis with significant pericardial effusion requiring drainage, unilateral pleural effusion, left elbow arthritis, and fever. Microbiologic work-up was negative. Aspirin was resumed, improving symptoms. Global developmental delay persisted. During adolescence, pseudopapilledema was diagnosed, and repeat cerebral CT scan showed diffuse brain atrophy with cortical calcifications. The patient experienced alternating buttock pain relieved by NSAIDs. Radiographs showed sclerosis and possible erosion of the right and left sacroiliac joints. Positive HLA-B27 status led to a diagnosis of spondyloarthritis, treated with NSAIDs as needed. In adulthood, the patient continued to experience intermittent arthralgia and was treated twice for suspected septic arthritis of the right wrist, despite negative microbiologic work-up and absence of crystals on joint aspiration.

input.case_report: The patient, the third child of a 32-year-old mother, was born after a normal pregnancy. At 10 months, the patient underwent bilateral inguinal herniorrhaphy and later received a gastrostomy tube for feeding assistance. At 14 years old, the patient was evaluated in a genetics clinic, weighing 27.60 kg, with a height of 147 cm and a head circumference of 55 cm. Notable facial features included mild dolichocephaly, mild midface hypoplasia, deep-set eyes with short palpebral fissures, large protruding ears with a simplified helix and deficient lobuli, bilateral skin tags on the posterior aspect of the ear lobes, and micro- and retrognathia with large appearing teeth. Clinical examination revealed mild facial weakness, global hypotonia, diminished muscle mass, and motor apraxia. Deep tendon reflexes were brisk bilaterally. input.case_report: A 48-year-old African American female with a history of intravenous heroin use and multiple previous admissions for cellulitis and draining abscesses of the extremities presented to the emergency room with 48 hours of body pain, aches, abdominal pain, and weakness. She reported using intravenous drugs earlier that day and complained of severe pain in the right upper extremity. On examination, she was hypotensive (80/60s mmHg) and tachycardic (120 beats/minute). Multiple ulcerations were noted on the bilateral upper and lower extremities, with two particularly tender ulcers on the right forearm near the antecubital fossa. The right forearm was swollen compared to the left, but there was no crepitus, fluctuance, or drainage. Compartments were soft, pulses were palpable, and the sensorimotor examination was normal. Fluid resuscitation with crystalloid and broad-spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin and piperacillin/tazobactam) was initiated. Laboratory analysis showed an anion gap metabolic acidosis with a pH of 7.19 and lactate of 6.9 mmol/L, acute kidney injury with a BUN of 77 mg/dL and creatinine of 6.5 mg/dL, a WBC count of 1.67 × 10^3/µL, and an INR of 1.63. She was admitted to the medical ICU for septic shock with multiorgan dysfunction. A CT scan of the right upper extremity revealed diffuse soft tissue swelling, fat stranding, and lymphadenopathy extending to the right axilla. Three hours later, the patient experienced increased pain in the right upper extremity, ecchymosis, and purple skin changes extending toward the axilla, along with a significant motor deficit (0/5 strength in the hand, forearm, and shoulder). She remained hypotensive and tachycardic, unresponsive to volume resuscitation and antibiotics, requiring norepinephrine (20 µg/kg/minute). She was taken to the OR for radical debridement of the right upper extremity. Intraoperatively, the fascia appeared grossly healthy with no frank purulence, but the subcutaneous tissue and fat were very edematous with a slight gray tinge and easily separated. The muscles appeared healthy, but the tissues were not bleeding normally, with multiple areas of venous thrombosis noted. Radical excision of the affected tissue was performed, including the basilic vein, which was thrombosed. Distal pulses were obtained with Doppler, and fasciotomy of all muscle compartments of the forearm and upper arm was performed. Post-debridement, the remaining tissues appeared healthy and bleeding. Despite increasing vasopressor requirements, the patient was transferred to the SICU for further resuscitation. Over the next 16 hours, she briefly improved with decreasing pressor requirements and a downtrend of lactate to 3.5 mmol/L, but then deteriorated again with increased pressor requirements and lactate rising to 5.9 mmol/L. The right upper extremity muscles and neurovascular bundle became dusky and edematous, with skin changes extending from the axillary region to the lateral chest wall. She was returned to the OR for further debridement, including the shoulder skin and subcutaneous tissues, extending incisions to the lateral chest wall. The tissues were nonviable and nonbleeding, leading to excision of the lateral torso soft tissues to the iliac crest. The rapid spread of the infection compromised the entire right arm, which was necrotic with multiple areas of venous thrombosis. The family consented to a life-saving right shoulder disarticulation. The left forearm was explored with two incisions to investigate ecchymosis, revealing healthy and bleeding tissues. A diagnostic laparoscopy was performed to rule out any intraabdominal source, showing no significant pathology or mechanical obstruction. The patient was returned to the SICU for continued resuscitation.

input.case_report: A 63-year-old woman was admitted with major depression. She had experienced a gradual onset of depressed mood following a diagnosis of ALS five years prior. Clinical presentation at that time included objective weakness, atrophy, and fasciculations in the upper limb muscles, along with extremely brisk tendon reflexes. No clear sensory deficits such as hypesthesia or dysesthesia were documented. Electromyography showed abnormal spontaneous activity in the right deltoid muscle, abducens pollicis brevis, and palmar interossei muscles, with signs of bilateral chronic denervation in the upper limbs. A normal cranial MRI scan was noted. Increased distal motor latencies of the median nerve suggested bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome. Neurological examination at the time of admission (about 7 years after the first symptoms) revealed normal cranial nerves with no signs of bulbar dysfunction and no fasciculations or weakness of facial, tongue, or neck muscles. Distinct muscle atrophy along with mild fasciculations of the upper limbs, particularly in the supraspinatus, deltoid, biceps muscles, and finger extensors, was noted, with motor strength reduced to 3-4/5. In the lower limbs, motor strength was 4/5 in foot extensors, but no obvious atrophy or fasciculations were found. Weakness of the proximal lower limb muscles was suspected due to difficulties in rising from sitting. She was able to walk independently but showed gait ataxia. Reduced vibration proprioception of all limbs and hyper- and dysesthesia of the shoulders and proximal arms were found. Deep tendon reflexes were symmetric and extremely brisk, Babinski's signs were absent, and superficial abdominal reflexes were retained. There were no symptoms of bladder or bowel dysfunction, but she reported neck pain, numbness, and clumsiness of both arms. Proximal muscle atrophy in the arms, along with sensory dysfunction and pain, was observed.

The urologist recommended clean intermittent catheterization (CIC).

General physical examination revealed no additional findings. The boy was the first child, delivered preterm at 35 weeks with a birth weight of 2.51 kg. The Apgar score was 9, and he did not require an oxygen chamber. The mother reported no infections or medication use during pregnancy, and there was no known family history of any related conditions.

input.case_report: A 20-year-old white male student presented with a two-month history of breathlessness on exertion, a non-productive cough, and fatigue. His heart rate was 130 bpm, and his blood pressure was 110/60 mmHg. Examination of the lung fields, lymph nodes, and testicles was unremarkable. Neurological examination, routine blood tests, and urine analysis were within normal limits. A resting 12-lead electrocardiogram showed sinus tachycardia. A posterior-anterior chest radiograph revealed mediastinal widening at the level of the pulmonary cone, suggestive of a mediastinal mass. Transthoracic echocardiography in the parasternal view identified a large extracardiac mass compressing the pulmonary artery. Continuous wave Doppler examination indicated increased velocity (Vmax 458 cm/second, Vmean 300 cm/second) with a peak systolic gradient of 84 mmHg and a mean gradient of 44 mmHg in the pulmonary artery at the compression site. Magnetic resonance imaging of the chest confirmed an anterior mediastinal mass compressing the main pulmonary artery. Gonadal examination and scrotal ultrasonography results were normal.

input.case_report: A 52-year-old woman presented with abdominal pain, bilious vomiting, constipation, and abdominal distension for two days. She had been treated with oral mesalamine therapy for moderately active ulcerative colitis involving the entire colon (pancolitis) diagnosed four months prior. Eight years ago, she underwent an abdominal hysterectomy for uterine fibroids. On clinical examination, she was hemodynamically stable. Her abdomen was distended with hyperperistaltic bowel sounds. Routine blood tests were within normal limits. A computed tomography (CT) scan of the abdomen showed dilated proximal ileal loops with air-fluid levels and collapsed terminal ileal loops. There was an abrupt narrowing and a 'bird beak appearance' in the proximal ileum beneath the anterior abdominal wall. Based on these findings, an emergency laparotomy was planned. During the laparotomy, the proximal ileal loops were found to be grossly dilated, while the terminal ileal loops and large bowel loops were collapsed and appeared unremarkable. The ileal loops were densely adhered to the anterior abdominal wall at the previous scar site, causing luminal obstruction. Extensive adhesiolysis was performed to release the obstruction. A 2 x 2 cm lesion was observed on the mesenteric side of the terminal ileum distal to the site of adhesive obstruction. The involved segment of the ileum was resected, and an end-to-end ileo-ileal anastomosis was carried out.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: A 14-year-old Greek male, born to healthy non-consanguineous parents, at term and without perinatal complications, was evaluated for uncontrolled epilepsy. He experienced generalized motor seizures starting at 3.5 months of age, which were eventually managed with anticonvulsants. He exhibited developmental delays, achieving head control at 4.5 months and walking at 19 months with a broad-based unsteady gait. He was noted to have ataxic cerebral palsy. Additionally, he had delayed speech and generalized learning difficulties in school. Tubulopathy was identified at the age of 8 years, and he was initially treated for Bartter syndrome. His treatment included potassium gluconate, magnesium, spironolactone, and indomethacin. At the same age, sensorineural deafness was detected, and he began using a hearing aid. During his teenage years, he experienced infrequent secondarily generalized focal motor seizures that did not respond to several medication changes. At 14 years old, he was a pleasant, cooperative teenager with mild intellectual disability. His cranial nerve examination (II–XII) was normal, and he had normal muscle strength in the upper and lower extremities. He exhibited mild spastic hypertonia in the lower limbs, increased deep tendon reflexes, and bilateral Babinski signs. There was mildly decreased superficial sensation in the lower extremities, but no disturbance of position sensation or vibration. Romberg sign was absent. His gait was broad-based and slow, and he was unable to walk in tandem. Examination for cerebellar signs showed no tremor or dysmetria, but rapid alternating movements were slow and uncoordinated, resulting in dysdiadochokinesis. Wakefulness and sleep electroencephalogram (EEG) showed slow background rhythms without epileptiform activity. Brain computed tomography (CT) and MRI were normal. Nerve conduction studies revealed mild abnormalities on two occasions without significant differences between the tests. The motor conduction velocities on the second measurement were 41.3 m/s for the right median nerve, 38.2 m/s for the right peroneal nerve, and 40.8 m/s for the left peroneal nerve. The sensory conduction velocities were 38.6 m/s for the right median nerve, 42.9 m/s for the right ulnar nerve, and 42.3 m/s for the right sural nerve. No other abnormalities were noted in these electrophysiological studies. Laboratory tests showed normal serum vitamin E, B12, folic acid, and plasma very-long-chain fatty acid (VLCFA) levels. The patient's epilepsy was fully controlled with topiramate and carbamazepine. At follow-up, the ataxia and spasticity remained stable, with independent home ambulation and the need for support in the community. Episodic weakness occurred on multiple occasions without evidence of electrolyte disturbance or clinically overt seizures, and this responded to adjustment of the anticonvulsants. Overall, his condition was consistent with a static disorder of movement, with activity limitations, also associated with intellectual disability and epilepsy.

input.case_report: A 49-year-old Thai male initially presented with sclerodactyly, symmetrical polyarthralgia, digital pitting scars, and Raynaud's phenomenon, without any chest symptoms. Four years later, he experienced dyspnea on exertion, which worsened over the next two years. A chest radiograph showed interstitial infiltration in both lower lung zones and a prominent pulmonary trunk. Pulmonary function tests indicated a restrictive pattern with a forced vital capacity (FVC) of 53% and a forced expiratory volume in one second/forced vital capacity (FEV1/FVC) of 105%. High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) of the chest revealed minimal thickening of the interlobular septa, thickening of the pleura, and mild bronchiectasis in both lower lungs. An echocardiogram showed mild concentric left ventricular hypertrophy without pulmonary arterial hypertension. Due to worsening symptoms, the patient was treated with oral daily cyclophosphamide (100 mg per day) and prednisolone (10 mg per day). After three months, clinical symptoms improved, but follow-up FVC slightly decreased to 45% with an FEV1/FVC of 90%. Cyclophosphamide was discontinued after one year, and the steroid was tapered off during follow-up. One year after stopping cyclophosphamide, while still on prednisolone, the dyspnea on exertion returned. Repeat chest radiograph and HRCT showed increased thickening of the interlobular septa, sub-pleural emphysema, and tubular bronchiectasis in the right lower lung. Supportive treatment included low-flow oxygen therapy and breathing exercises due to chronic pulmonary fibrosis. The patient was admitted with a low-grade fever, pleuritic chest pain, progressive shortness of breath, and a productive cough lasting five days. Physical examination revealed tachypnea (28 breaths/minute), a body temperature of 38.2°C, decreased breath sounds, and vocal resonance in the right lung with a midline trachea. Oxygen saturation on room air was 92%. A complete blood count showed hemoglobin at 14.2 g/dL, white blood cell count at 8,800 cells/mm3, and platelet count at 247,000 cells/mm3. A chest radiograph indicated a pneumothorax in the right lung. Cloudy yellow-colored fluid was obtained after chest tube insertion, with an exudative profile showing red blood cell count of 890 cells/mm3 and white blood cell count of 1,890 cells/mm3 (polymorphonuclear cells 64%, eosinophils 30%, and lymphocytes 6%).

input.case_report: A 42-year-old man presented with a 6-month history of memory issues, behavioral changes, and walking difficulties. Upon examination, he was conscious with reduced attention span, impaired recent memory, and executive functions, while remote memory was relatively intact. He had a moist voice and signs of bulbar dysfunction. Deep tendon reflexes were normal, and bilateral plantar responses were flexor.
