Laboratory tests, including hemogram; liver, kidney, and thyroid function tests; serum Vitamin B12 levels; and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) studies, were normal. The thyroid peroxidase antibody test and CSF protein 14-3-3 were negative.

input.question: Analyze this clinical case report and diagnose the rare disease.
input.case_report: An 87-year-old man, previously in good health, received emergency care following a traffic accident. Upon hospital admission, his general condition was stable with normal vital signs. Blood biochemistry tests indicated elevated inflammation markers and significantly increased pancreatic enzymes (AMY 818 U/L, P-AMY 705 U/L, and lipase 1191 U/L). Abdominal contrast-enhanced computed tomography revealed fluid accumulation around the pancreas and thickening of the duodenum wall, particularly in the horizontal segment. The pancreatic head showed severe injury, with suspected laceration. The duodenum wall was notably enlarged, especially in the third part, and the horizontal segment was significantly distended. Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography was conducted to assess potential main pancreatic duct injury, revealing substantial contrast medium leakage from the main pancreatic duct at the pancreatic head. Endoscopic naso-pancreatic drainage (ENPD) was placed proximally to the injury. An emergency operation was performed due to the severe pancreatic head injury. Intraoperative findings confirmed complete transection of the pancreatic head, though the disruption was not extensive. The case was classified as grade IV according to the AAST-OIS classification. Additionally, the horizontal segment of the duodenum was widely swollen with a thickened wall. The ENPD tube could not be distinguished from the main pancreatic duct due to movement during the procedure. The initial treatment strategy involved primary repair by suturing the pancreatic laceration. However, the ENPD tube was not detectable at the main pancreatic duct, and the severe laceration was unsuitable for simple suturing. Given the extensive duodenum injury, the decision was made to remove the damaged organ. Reconstruction was performed using PD-II, with pancreatojejunostomy and the Blumgart modification for pancreatic duct-to-mucosa anastomosis. The pancreas was transected diagonally, necessitating additional pancreatic resection for the anastomosis. The surgery lasted 389 minutes with a blood loss of 527 mL. No postoperative complications occurred. Follow-up computed tomography 10 days post-surgery showed no abnormal findings such as abscess or pseudoaneurysm formation. The patient was discharged on postoperative day 25. Examination of the resected pancreatic specimen showed acute pancreatitis with fat necrosis and neutrophil infiltration. The resected duodenum specimen revealed mucosal enlargement and irregular bumps from the descending to the horizontal segment.

input.case_report: A 52-year-old male presented with a long-standing history of gait difficulties since adolescence and reported distal muscle weakness in both lower limbs along with progressive foot deformities in November 2021. He lives with his partner and 12-year-old daughter. He underwent surgery on his left foot in 1987, but no details of the procedure are available. The patient has a history of obesity and dyslipidemia and is on atorvastatin. His 77-year-old mother has had similar symptoms since early adulthood. Other family members, including his daughter, sister, grandparents, uncles, and aunts, are healthy and asymptomatic. Physical examination revealed high-arched feet and equinovarus deformities. He was referred to neurology and physical medicine and rehabilitation for further evaluation and the prescription of orthotic devices. In the same month, a physiatrist prescribed orthopedic footwear, which the patient received in April 2022, leading to an immediate improvement in his daily activities.

input.case_report: A 59-year-old Caucasian woman presented for a cardiological examination due to experiencing palpitations over the past two years. Her medical history included treatment for Hodgkin’s lymphoma with chest radiation and splenectomy at the age of 30. She was a non-smoker and did not use illicit drugs. Physical examination showed a slightly accelerated pulse, but other vital signs and findings were unremarkable. An electrocardiogram indicated sinus tachycardia. Echocardiography identified a 2 × 3 cm mass in the inferior wall of the left atrium without other cardiac abnormalities. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging described the mass as highly vascularized and infiltrating the inferior wall of the left atrium. The mass was best delineated on cardiac magnetic resonance cine images. On T1- and T2-weighted images, the mass appeared isointense and hyperintense to myocardium, respectively. Gadolinium infusion showed positive contrast uptake on first-pass perfusion and strong, heterogeneous delayed hyperenhancement. A computed tomography angiogram of the thorax showed no metastasis. The patient was referred to a tertiary center for surgical treatment. Following sternotomy and initiation of cardiopulmonary bypass with bicaval cannulation, the tumor was exposed via left atriotomy and macroscopically excised. A partial resection of the posterior wall of the left atrium was then reconstructed with bovine pericardium. The postoperative course was uneventful except for a single episode of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, and the patient was promptly discharged.

input.case_report: A 41-year-old male, heavy smoker, with a history of recurrent urinary stones, presented with left flank pain and one episode of gross hematuria. Upon presentation, he was afebrile and did not report chills. Physical examination showed a soft, non-tender abdomen with left costovertebral angle tenderness and no palpable lymph nodes. Blood tests, including complete blood count, electrolytes, blood urea nitrogen, creatinine, and hepatic enzymes, were normal. Urinalysis revealed 23 white blood cells (WBC) and 38 red blood cells (RBC) per high-power microscopic field in urinary sediment. Urinary cytology findings led to further investigations. A computed tomography (CT) urography scan identified a 5 cm mass in the left renal pelvis and multiple infracentimetric perihilar lymph nodes. A few days later, a laparoscopic left nephroureterectomy with perihilar lymph node dissection was performed. Gross examination showed a 3 × 2.5 cm vegetative tumor in the renal pelvis infiltrating the adjacent renal parenchyma and perihilar fat. Microscopic examination revealed a high-grade transitional papillary carcinoma of the renal pelvic lumen infiltrating the muscular layer of the renal pelvis, adjacent renal parenchyma, and perihilar fat, with multiple tumoral emboli in adjacent venous structures. Urothelial carcinoma in situ (CIS) was also present in the mucosa. Four out of six dissected lymph nodes showed disease metastasis. The patient received adjuvant chemotherapy with cisplatin-gemcitabine and was monitored with CT and urinary cytology for two years without signs of relapse. After a year without follow-up, the patient returned with periumbilical abdominal pain and recent weight loss. Physical examination and laboratory tests were unremarkable. A CT scan revealed a left paraaortic mass infiltrating the left psoas muscle over a length of 12 cm. Biopsy of this mass showed carcinomatous proliferation with polygonal cells, eosinophilic and dense cytoplasm, irregular hyperchromatic nuclei with multiple nucleoli, and occasional clear cells.

input.case_report: A 37-year-old male with no significant past medical history presented to the emergency department with complaints of fever, dry cough, malaise for three days, and progressively worsening shortness of breath for one day. Nasopharyngeal swab via real-time reverse transcriptase-polymerase chain reaction analysis confirmed COVID-19. Further workup for euvolemic hyponatremia revealed urine osmolality of 338 mOsm/kg (normal range: 50-1200 mOsm/kg) and urine spot sodium of 45 mmol/L (normal range: 54-190 mmol/L). CA125 level in pleural effusion was over 600 U/mL, and the CA125 level in blood was 401.03 U/mL (0–35 lU/mL). Renal ureteral ultrasound showed a pelvic hypo-echoic mass. input.case_report: A 9-month-old boy was brought to the Department of Rehabilitation Medicine of Shenzhen Children's Hospital in August 2023 due to low muscle tension in the limbs and neck, and the inability to turn over and sit up on his own. He was the mother’s only child, delivered naturally at full-term, with a birth weight of 2,800 g. There was no history of neonatal asphyxia, convulsions, hepatitis, tuberculosis, trauma, or other infectious diseases. He had no history of food or drug allergies. His parents were young and healthy with no family history of genetic metabolic diseases. The boy was born with varus deformity and ulnar deviation of the fingers. Since his 26th day of life, he has undergone four intermittent plaster corrective operations at a hospital in Guangdong. At 70 days of age, he was treated with percutaneous loosening and Ponseti plaster fixation of the bilateral Achilles tendons for congenital equine varus foot; since then, he has worn orthopedic braces for at least 3 hours every day. At the age of 5 months, he was given an orthopedic brace at a hospital in Beijing due to finger contracture deformity. A physical examination at 9 months showed that the patient's head circumference was 44 cm and that his anterior fontanelle was unenclosed at 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm. His facial features included a broad nasal bridge, large nares, a long philtrum, and micrognathia. There were no abnormalities in eye distance or the palatal arch, and the palmprints of his hands were normal. No obvious abnormalities were found in the lungs, chest, anus, or external genitalia. However, he had a right inguinal hernia that developed when crying and spontaneously recovered. The muscles of the patient's limbs and torso were flaccid, and he had developed hypotonia. Neither the knee reflex nor the Achilles tendon reflex was elicited. He had no sleep problems, breathing problems, or problems when exercising. Thyroid function and myocardial enzymes showed no abnormalities. The results of a blood test performed on the patient were as follows: myoglobin, 37 ng/ml; creatine kinase, 171 IU/L; lactate dehydrogenase, 280 IU/L; aspartate aminotransferase, 31 IU/L; and creatine kinase–MB, 7.42 ng/ml. The patient's brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and electroencephalogram data were normal, and no characteristic lesions were found via electromyography. A color Doppler ultrasound revealed a patent foramen ovale in his heart (1.4 mm). At the age of 9 months, the child had low muscle tension in the limbs and neck, an unstable vertical head position, and could not turn over or sit up on his own. The Bailey Infant Development Scale Assessment: Intelligence revealed the patient's intelligence to be consistent with that expected at 6 months of age, and his developmental index was less than 50, indicating developmental delay. His Peabody Motor Developmental Scale (PMDS) score was low, with a gross motor development score of 62. His fine motor development was at a medium to low level.

input.case_report: A 71-year-old right-handed man with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and glaucoma experienced progressive stiffness in his right arm and speech difficulties over one year. Three months later, he exhibited mild guttural speech difficulties, significantly increased muscle tone in his right arm held in flexion, and difficulty extending and abducting his right-hand fingers, although arm strength remained full. Reflexes were slightly brisk in the right arm, with upgoing plantar reflexes on both sides. His gait was characterized by a slightly stooped posture, reduced right arm swing, and mild postural instability, but stride length and gait speed were normal. The rigidity in his right upper extremity became more pronounced and extended to his right leg. His Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) score was 15, with deficits in executive function, attention, visuospatial ability, and delayed recall that did not improve with cueing. A formal speech evaluation revealed a strained, hypernasal, and breathy vocal quality with slow, effortful, and segmented speech. He had frequent misarticulations, imprecise consonants, and produced only one syllable per breath, showing elements of both spastic and flaccid speech difficulties. He also displayed ideomotor apraxia in his right arm. A follow-up EMG of three limbs, cervical and thoracic paraspinal muscles, and tongue performed a year later showed focal denervation in the right flexor carpi radialis but no tongue denervation. A comprehensive laboratory assessment for metabolic, infectious, or inflammatory conditions was negative. A follow-up brain MRI revealed mild progression of atrophy, mainly in the left motor and pre-motor cortex and left temporal lobe.

input.case_report: A 69-year-old Belgian Caucasian man reported experiencing cramps in the right calf and difficulties walking, which had progressively worsened over the past 6 months to the extent that he needed to use the banisters for support when climbing stairs. He did not experience any sensory disturbances. His medical history included arterial hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, complete deafness in the right ear following a mastoidectomy in early childhood, and asymptomatic elevated alkaline phosphatase levels identified 7 years earlier, confirmed by bone diphosphonate technetium-99m scintigraphy. His family history included a sister who died at 62 years with dementia and a mother who died at 67 years with dementia and walking difficulties. Initial neurological examination revealed slight weakness in the dorsal extensors of the left foot, left quadriceps, and right deltoid muscle, atrophy of the left thigh, and diffuse tendon hyperreflexia. Fasciculations were observed in the right deltoid muscle, but not in the tongue, which showed no atrophy. Cranial nerves and sensory functions were intact, and there was no Babinski sign. Electromyography (EMG) showed a diffuse chronic neurogenic pattern in all tested muscles of the left leg and the right deltoid muscle, without signs of subacute denervation such as fibrillations or positive sharp waves. Motor nerve conduction of the right peroneal nerve was markedly slowed, without conduction block. Laboratory tests were largely normal except for slight elevations in alkaline phosphatase (115 mU/mL, normal range 30-90 mU/mL) and lactate dehydrogenase (308 mU/mL, normal range 140-280 mU/mL). MRI of the lumbar spine and CT scans of the cervical spine and pelvis did not show significant abnormalities. Over the next 3 years, the patient found it difficult to walk for more than 20 minutes, experienced falls, and had intermittent paresthesias in the left big toe. He presented with a right Babinski sign and absent ankle reflexes. Further electrophysiological studies indicated diffuse sensorimotor neuropathy with a chronic neurogenic denervation pattern in the bilateral tibialis anterior and rectus femoris muscles, the right vastus lateralis, and soleus. Signs of subacute denervation (fibrillations and/or fasciculations) were noted in the right tibialis anterior muscle, the left vastus lateralis, and the right biceps. Muscle and nerve biopsies were discussed but declined by the patient. Somatosensory evoked potentials (SEP) suggested subcortical, supramedullary slowing of central somatosensory pathways, while motor evoked potentials (MEP) induced by transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) of the central motor pathways showed elevated stimulus thresholds and slightly delayed central conduction, restricted to the lower limbs. A lumbar puncture revealed normal protein, glucose, cytology, and IgG index, with identical oligoclonal bands in the serum and cerebrospinal fluid. Tests for syphilis and borrelia were negative. Levels of serum human T-lymphotropic virus 1 antibodies, serum arylsulphatase-A, and urinary catecholamines were normal. Further investigations for polyneuropathy, including vitamin B status, diabetes screening, thyroid function, protein electrophoresis, and tumor markers, did not identify any metabolic, inflammatory, paraneoplastic, or toxic causes. MRI of the brain showed bilateral frontotemporal atrophy. MRI of the dorsal spine revealed focal atrophy spanning approximately 5 cm of the spinal cord at the D4 level, with secondary centromedullary dilatation.

input.case_report: A 68-year-old female presented with progressive fatigue over the past 3 weeks. She had no history of surgeries, harmful habits, or allergies. On admission, she was pale, afebrile (36.8º C), with stable hemodynamics (blood pressure 140/70 mmHg, pulse 90 bpm). Physical examination showed no cachexia (height 164 cm, weight 68 kg, BMI 25.28 kg/m2) and palpable abdominal pain. Her liver, spleen, and superficial lymph nodes were not enlarged. Her ECOG performance status was 1 point. Laboratory tests indicated hemolytic anemia (low hemoglobin, reticulocytosis, elevated lactate dehydrogenase, low haptoglobin), thrombocytopenia, elevated liver enzymes, stage 3A chronic kidney disease, and signs of inflammation. No deficiencies in ferritin, folate, or vitamin B12 were found. Abdominal ultrasound showed hepatomegaly (right lobe ~171 mm) and multiple liver metastases of varying echogenicity, with the largest being ~25 mm in diameter. A nonhomogenous increased uterus with several calcified myomas was also noted. Full-body CT scan confirmed hepatomegaly (right lobe ~184 mm), multiple hypodense solid masses in the liver (up to 3 cm), spleen (up to 3.7 cm), and kidneys (up to 12 mm), moderate interstitial, para-aortic, paratracheal, and aortocaval lymphadenopathy, and disc atelectasis in the lungs. The primary tumor location remained unknown. Lesions were differentiated among several possibilities. Hematologist consultation led to transfusions of irradiated blood components, and bone marrow biopsy and aspiration were performed. Trepanobiopsy showed reactive bone marrow changes with massive megakaryopoietic hyperplasia, and no metastases were detected. No tumors or other pathological changes were found during colonoscopy.

With the patient in the lateral position, a right temporal craniotomy extending to the base of the middle cranial fossa was performed. No mastoid air cells were encountered during the bone work. The tumor was excised by extradural approach and was found to be grey-colored, well-defined, and encapsulated, with variable consistency and areas of necrosis. Anterior petrosal bone was found to be eroded at the area of tumoral attachment. No drilling of the petrosal bone was done during the procedure. Nerve monitoring was not employed. Intraoperative frozen section was reported as round to spindle cell tumor. After the surgery, the patient was extubated and shifted to the intensive care unit for further management. The patient complained of decreased hearing in the right ear on the first postoperative day. The patient was mobilized the same day and had no significant complaints otherwise. Barring cranial nerve VIII, the rest of the cranial nerve examination was normal. Aminoglycoside antibiotics and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs were withheld as these drugs are ototoxic. On the third postoperative day, the patient was discharged at his insistence. He was prescribed tapering doses of steroids and advised to follow up in one week. The patient was reviewed the following week after discharge. Other than the complaint of hearing loss, he was recovering well, with no significant headache or seizure episode.

input.case_report: A 61-year-old male with no prior medical conditions was admitted with symptoms of malaise, night sweats, a weight loss of 11 kg over six months, and worsening shortness of breath over two weeks. He exhibited significant shortness of breath (NYHA III) and severe leg edema. ECG revealed atrial fibrillation, and a chest X-ray indicated pulmovenous congestion and a large pleural effusion. Abdominal and thoracic CT scans identified multiple mediastinal and mesenteric lymph nodes, bilateral perirenal masses, and a large pericardial lymph node conglomerate infiltrating the right atrium, ventricle, and parts of the ascending aorta. Blood smear and flow cytometry of peripheral blood and bone marrow biopsy appeared normal. Echocardiography showed severe right ventricular impairment due to pronounced hypertrophy of the free right ventricular wall and a 50 × 60 mm tumor adjacent to the right ventricular wall, correlating with severe right heart failure symptoms. Before a planned mini-laparotomy to analyze an abdominal lymph node, the patient experienced cardiac arrest, was resuscitated, and transferred to the ICU. Despite high doses of catecholamines and volume resuscitation, hemodynamics remained unstable (lactate level 17 mmol/L, SAPS II 64, SAFE score −6). A VA-ECMO was implanted as a “bridge to diagnostic and therapy.” Chemotherapy (CHOEP without Doxorubicin) was initiated immediately. Following clinical stabilization, myocardial biopsy and abdominal lymph node extirpation were performed while on VA-ECMO support. No signs of myocarditis were found, and histology and immunohistochemistry showed no lymphocytic infiltration of the myocardium, with only reactive changes in the lymph node, likely due to chemotherapy. The lymph nodes, pericardial tumor, and right ventricular wall hypertrophy regressed rapidly, and right heart function improved significantly during chemotherapy. The patient was weaned from the respirator after nine days, recovered from renal failure after ten days, and had the VA-ECMO explanted after 16 days. The course was complicated by Aspergillus pneumonia during neutropenia, treated for 23 days. Due to a good response with regression of cardiac infiltration and abdominal lymph nodes, the patient received a second chemotherapy cycle including rituximab (R-CHOEP) on day 21. A CT scan four weeks later showed partial tumor remission, with decreased diameters of the tumor mass around the ascending aorta, near the right ventricle, and at the upper kidney pole. The patient was discharged seven weeks after admission. Over the following months, he received four additional chemotherapy cycles, maintaining the tumor in partial remission.

input.case_report: A 5-year-old girl, born to nonconsanguineous parents, presented with congenital deafness and muteness, while other developmental milestones were normal. Examination showed consciousness with meningeal signs and no focal deficits. Routine tests were normal except for mild anemia. CSF analysis showed 250 cells (100% polymorphs), protein of 65 mg/dl, and glucose less than 20 mg/dl. CSF culture was negative. input.case_report: A 53-year-old male presented with a two-week history of visible blood in urine and progressively reduced urine output. At an outside hospital, a Foley catheter was placed, resulting in appropriate urine output, and bladder irrigation was initiated for hematuria. After a two-day hospital stay, he was discharged with a referral to urology. The next day, he visited our emergency department with symptoms including shortness of breath on exertion, sweating, chills, fatigue, loss of appetite, lower abdominal pain, and constipation. He reported not having a bowel movement for four days. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed mild right-sided hydronephrosis, diverticulosis in the descending and sigmoid colon, mild enlargement of the prostate, and a heterogeneous hyperdense mass within the bladder. Serial abdominal x-rays showed moderate small bowel dilation and significant colonic distention. Admission vitals indicated a heart rate of 152 beats per minute and blood pressure of 162/123. Laboratory values showed leukocytosis of 18.8 cells/µl and lactate of 3 mmol/l. Urinalysis revealed 3+ protein, 20-50 WBC, and 3+ occult blood with packed RBCs. On the fourth day of hospitalization, there was no improvement in bowel distension, and severe abdominal pain persisted. An immediate decompressive cecostomy was performed on the fifth day, revealing a distended cecum and releasing one liter of watery stool. The procedure was complicated by spillage of bowel contents into the abdominal cavity, which was isolated to the right lower quadrant and irrigated. The patient was placed on a five-day course of IV cefoxitin post-operatively. Mild improvement in abdominal pain was noted following decompression. Output into the cecostomy bag was minimal despite the stoma being patent. Persistent constipation and large bowel dilatation raised concerns for obstruction due to colonic mass or stricture. On the tenth day, a colonoscopy showed normal colonic mucosa and no evidence of colonic obstruction. The delay in performing the colonoscopy was due to ongoing constipation and prioritization of treatment for urosepsis.

input.case_report: A 76-year-old male with no known co-morbidities presented with a neck swelling that had been present for three months. The swelling was adherent to the hyoid bone, and a part of the hyoid bone was excised along with the entire thyroglossal tract. Two suspicious lymph nodes were isolated and sent for frozen section examination.

input.case_report: A 52-year-old Caucasian male presented to the Emergency Department (ED) with low-grade fever and pleuritic chest pain persisting for five days. His medical history includes liver haemangioma, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) due to esophageal achalasia, and recurrent pericarditis. He was on a tapering regimen of prednisolone (5mg/day) and colchicine (1mg/day). There was no family history of pericarditis or autoimmune/auto-inflammatory diseases among his first- and second-degree relatives. The patient had no history of arthralgias, myalgias, or skin rash. Nearly a year prior, he was hospitalized for acute pericarditis and initially treated with Ibuprofen (2400mg/day) and colchicine (1mg/day), which did not yield satisfactory results, leading to the addition of prednisolone. Given his weight of 80kg, he received 40mg of intravenous prednisolone along with colchicine. Subsequent management with low-dose corticosteroids and oral colchicine improved his condition, and he was discharged after nine days with complete symptom resolution. Seven months later, he returned to the ED with chest pain, flu-like symptoms, low-grade fever, and excessive fatigue. Laboratory tests indicated elevated inflammation markers, and a transthoracic echocardiogram revealed a small pericardial effusion around the lateral wall of the left ventricle without signs of constriction. He was admitted to the Cardiology Department, and various immunological tests, tumor marker tests, Mantoux tuberculin skin test, and chest and abdominal CT scans showed no evidence of an underlying cause. After a few days of intravenous prednisolone (40mg/day), he showed significant clinical and laboratory improvement and was discharged with instructions for a slower tapering of prednisolone and continuation of colchicine (1mg/day). Four months later, he returned to the ED with similar symptoms and was hospitalized again. Intravenous prednisolone (40mg/day) was administered, and he was discharged six days later with advice to consult a Rheumatologist. On his current examination, the patient was febrile (T=38°C), hemodynamically stable (blood pressure: 134/72mmHg, 60 beats per minute), with good oxygen saturation on room air. Lung examination was normal, but pericardial friction rub was noted on auscultation. Ear, nose, throat, and neurological exams were normal. ECG showed sinus rhythm with widespread concave ST elevation and PR depression in most limb leads (I, II, aVF) and precordial leads (V2-6), along with PR elevation in lead aVR and Spodick’s sign. Arterial blood gases (ABGs) indicated insignificant respiratory alkalosis (ABGs-fraction of inspired oxygen (FiO2) 21%: pH 7.47, partial pressure of carbon dioxide (pCO2) 34.7mmHg, partial pressure of oxygen (pO2) 82mmHg, bicarbonate (HCO3) 24.9mEq/L, lactate 0.3mmol/L). Laboratory tests showed markedly raised inflammation markers (WBC 12,500/μL, CRP 27.22pg/ml). High-sensitive cardiac troponin, renal function, and urinalysis were normal. Blood and urine cultures were taken, and a bedside echocardiogram showed a small pericardial effusion around the lateral wall of the left ventricle with normal size and function of both ventricles, no regional abnormalities, and no signs of constriction or tamponade. COVID-19 tests were negative, and the chest X-ray did not show any signs of infection. The patient was admitted to the Cardiology Department for further management. Blood and urine cultures were negative, and given his previous good response to corticosteroids, low-dose intravenous prednisolone (40mg/day) and oral colchicine (1mg/day) were initiated. During his hospital stay, a more detailed history revealed recurrent episodes of self-limiting fever during childhood and a hospitalization at age 12 for sudden fever and severe abdominal pain, attributed to a gastrointestinal tract infection.

Neurological examination showed chorea, hyperreflexia, and a positive Babinski’s sign. All deep tendon reflexes were brisk, and plantar reflexes were extensor on both sides. Systemic examination and laboratory investigations, including blood cell counts, hepatorenal functions, urinalysis, and chest radiograph, were normal.
